children.title: June 05, 2025
children.description: This section serves as the formal front-matter for the PCI Express® Base Specification Revision 7.0, outlining legal disclaimers, intellectual property rights, and trademark notifications. It addresses questions regarding copyright status, distribution permissions, and the liability limitations set forth by PCI-SIG for the 7.0-1.0-PUB technical release.

children.title: Table of Contents
children.description: This section provides a technical overview of the PCI Express (PCIe) interconnect architecture, detailing link characteristics and fabric topology components including the Root Complex and various Endpoint classifications. It addresses foundational questions regarding the structural hierarchy, interface evolution, and specific operational rules governing legacy, standard, and integrated PCIe endpoints.

children.title: List of Figures
children.description: This section catalogs technical documentation figures involving Flit Mode, non-Flit Mode processing, and 8b/10b or 128b/130b encoding schemes for PCIe transmit and receive paths. It serves as a navigational index for identifying specific revisions or comparisons of link-layer error checking and signaling diagrams.

children.title: List of Equations
children.description: This section provides the mathematical definitions and credit-tracking formulas for shared flow control mechanisms in transmitter architectures. It specifies calculations for consumed credits, cumulative requirements, and usage limit gating for both merged and non-merged Shared Transmitter configurations.

children.title: List of Tables
children.description: This section provides a tabular reference for PCIe signaling characteristics, TLP header encodings, and address space routing, specifically covering Flit and non-Flit mode configurations. These tables define technical field mappings for Fmt/Type fields, OHC-Ax structures, and ARI ID routing protocols to clarify packet formatting and data length encodings.

children.title: Status of this Document
children.description: This section identifies the PCI Express Base 7.0 Specification as an evolution of Base 6.4, clarifying that the normative version is devoid of changebars while the informative comparison includes them. It addresses the document's validity, data rate enhancements to 128.0 GT/s, and the adoption of the ReSpec open-source documentation system.

children.title: Revision History
children.description: This section serves as the formal document control log for the PCI Express Base Specification, outlining versioning milestones, data rate upgrades (up to 8.0 GT/s), and incremental technical integrations. It provides a chronological roadmap of implemented Engineering Change Notices (ECNs) and error corrections, answering questions regarding the evolution, feature compliance, and specific update history of the standard.

children.title: Critical Errata
children.description: This section outlines critical errata and revision history for PCIe Base Specifications 7.0, 6.4, and 6.0.1, detailing technical corrections for Partial Header Encryption, Flit Mode parsing, and Shared Flow Control. It clarifies architectural requirements, encoding inconsistencies, and updated registry definitions for developers auditing specification compliance.

children.title: Important Errata and Approved ECNs
children.description: This section outlines essential errata and Engineering Change Notices (ECNs) for PCIe specifications, detailing technical updates regarding Flit Mode rules, Alternate Protocol capabilities, MSI configurations, and Retimer behavior. It addresses critical implementation clarifications, signaling inconsistencies, and documentation corrections necessary for ensuring hardware compliance and interoperability.

children.title: Objective of the PCI Express® Architecture
children.description: This section outlines the PCI Express® base specification, covering electrical, protocol, platform architecture, and programming interface requirements for multi-vendor interoperability. It addresses how the architecture achieves flexible, scalable integration across client, server, and embedded markets while maintaining backward compatibility.

children.title: PCI Express Architecture Specification Organization
children.description: This section outlines the PCI Express architecture framework, categorizing the Base Specification's coverage of protocol, Data Link, Physical Layer, and software interfaces alongside form factor-specific companion documents. It addresses how the specification hierarchy integrates mechanical, electrical, and structural standards across all PCIe variants.

children.title: Documentation Conventions
children.description: This section defines document-wide standards for capitalization, hexadecimal and binary notation formatting, and the distinction between formal Notes and Issues within the specification. It provides essential technical metadata required to interpret register definitions, address values, and status placeholders throughout the documentation.

children.title: Terms and Acronyms
children.description: This section defines key PCI Express nomenclature and technical standards, covering data encoding schemes (8b/10b, 128b/130b), Tag capabilities, and Access Control Services (ACS) frameworks. It provides authoritative definitions for architectural components like ARI Forwarding and AER to clarify regulatory specifications and protocol-level error reporting mechanisms.

children.title: Reference Documents
children.description: This section provides a categorized catalog of PCI and PCI Express (PCIe) base and Card Electromechanical (CEM) specifications, ranging from legacy 1.0 versions to current 7.0 standards. It functions as a technical index, identifying the precise revision history and document naming conventions required for standards compliance and hardware architectural design.

children.title: Chapter 1. Introduction
children.description: This introductory chapter details the architecture and operational foundations of PCI Express, covering interconnect evolution, link signaling, fabric topology, and the hardware-software configuration model. It addresses fundamental questions regarding how PCIe devices are discovered, initialized, and layered to facilitate high-speed, scalable peripheral communication.

children.title: Section 1.1 An Evolving I/O Interconnect
children.description: This section introduces the transition from parallel PCI to a scalable, serial-based PCI Express architecture, covering key topics like point-to-point interconnects, packetized protocols, and legacy software compatibility. It answers questions regarding the design requirements for unified I/O performance, physical form factor versatility, and the architectural evolution of power management and error handling across diverse computing platforms.

children.title: Section 1.2 PCI Express Link
children.description: This section defines the architectural capabilities of the PCI Express Link, covering quality of service (QoS), Hot-Plug support, data integrity, error handling, and process independence. It addresses how the interconnect manages power budgeting, fabric efficiency, fault recovery, and electrical compliance for dual-simplex communications.

children.title: Section 1.3 PCI Express Fabric Topology
children.description: This section defines PCI Express fabric topology, detailing link architecture, differential signaling methods, and data transmission rates. It answers questions regarding how signaling evolved through seven generations by outlining transitions in modulation (NRZ to PAM4), encoding schemes (8b/10b to 1b/1b), and resulting throughput performance.

children.title: Section 1.3.1 Root Complex
children.description: This section defines the Root Complex (RC) as the architectural bridge between the CPU/memory subsystem and I/O hierarchy domains, which consist of Endpoints, Switches, and Root Ports. It addresses technical requirements for hierarchy domain isolation and specifies that peer-to-peer transaction routing between Root Ports is an optional, implementation-dependent capability that may involve RC-mediated request reformation.

children.title: Section 1.3.2 Endpoints
children.description: This section details the architectural requirements and classification of PCI Express Endpoints, specifically covering Legacy Endpoints, PCIe Endpoints, and Root Complex Integrated Endpoints (RCiEPs). It addresses critical questions regarding hardware compliance, configuration space behaviors, and interface specifications for diverse device implementations.

children.title: Section 1.3.2.1 Legacy Endpoint Rules
children.description: This section outlines peer-to-peer routing capabilities for Root Complexes (RCs), specifically addressing packet splitting permissions, performance trade-offs, and critical exceptions for UIO, Deferrable Memory Write, and Vendor-Defined Message transactions. It defines the technical constraints under which an RC may decompose packets while maintaining compliance with Max_Payload_Size and Read Completion Boundary (RCB) requirements.

children.title: Section 1.3.2.3 Root Complex Integrated Endpoint Rules
children.description: This section defines technical compliance requirements and architectural constraints for Legacy Endpoints and PCI Express Endpoints regarding memory addressing, interrupt handling (MSI/MSI-X), I/O resource allocation, and transaction behavior. It specifies whether these devices must support 64-bit addressing, Configuration Requests, and atomic operations while clarifying the operational differences between legacy and PCIe-native endpoint implementations.

children.title: Section 1.3.3 Switch
children.description: This section defines the architectural requirements for RCiEPs (Root Complex Integrated Endpoints) and the logical structure of PCIe Switches using virtual PCI-to-PCI bridges. It addresses technical specifications regarding MSI/MSI-X support, register implementation, power management, Root Complex Event Collector associations, and hierarchy constraints, clarifying how these components participate within a PCI Express topology.

children.title: Section 1.3.5 PCI Express to PCI/PCI-X Bridge
children.description: This section defines the architectural requirements for **PCI Express to PCI/PCI-X Bridges** and the functional specifications for **Root Complex Event Collectors (RCEC)**. It clarifies RCEC configuration, endpoint association, and resource decoding, detailing how these components integrate into the PCI Express hierarchy to support system-wide error reporting and discovery.

children.title: Section 1.4 Hardware/Software Model for Discovery, Configuration and Operation
children.description: This section defines the hardware/software architectural model for PCIe device discovery and configuration, covering technical requirements for Endpoints, Root Complex Event Collectors (RCEC), and PCI-to-PCI bridges. It addresses how the configuration software identifies and manages internal bus topologies, RCiEP associations, and resource decoding rules for PCIe-compliant fabrics.

children.title: Section 1.5 PCI Express Layering Overview
children.description: This section outlines the PCI Express architectural framework, detailing the enhanced configuration mechanism, virtual PCI-to-PCI Bridge structures, and the three-layer communication model (Transaction, Data Link, and Physical). It clarifies how devices are mapped into Configuration Space and explains how the layered specification facilitates packet-based communication across the Root Complex and Switch ports.

children.title: Section 1.5.3 Physical Layer
children.description: This section details the Transaction Layer's role in TLP assembly, credit-based flow control, and Split Transaction management within the PCI Express stack. It defines the four address spaces—Memory, I/O, Configuration, and Message—and explains how Message Space replaces traditional sideband signals with "virtual wire" in-band transactions.

children.title: Section 1.5.4 Layer Functions and Services
children.description: This section defines the architectural responsibilities and service primitives—such as packet encapsulation, error detection, and synchronization—within the Transaction, Data Link, and Physical layers. It explains how these layers interact via standardized interfaces to facilitate reliable data transport and flow control across the interconnect.

children.title: Section 1.5.4.1 Transaction Layer Services
children.description: This section outlines Transaction Layer responsibilities within the PCIe architecture, specifically detailing Flow Control mechanisms, Link configuration storage, TLP generation/processing, and PCI-compliant ordering rules. It addresses how devices manage data integrity, throttle traffic via credit tracking, and convert TLP packets for device core consumption.

children.title: Section 1.5.4.3 Physical Layer Services
children.description: This section outlines Physical Layer and Data Link Layer responsibilities in a system, covering key topics such as Virtual Channels, Traffic Class labels, TLP flow control, and link maintenance. It addresses architectural questions regarding how data flows are multiplexed, prioritized via ordering domains, protected through CRC/retry mechanisms, and initialized during link startup.

children.title: Section 1.5.4.4 Inter-Layer Interfaces
children.description: This section defines the architectural boundaries and functional service primitives between PCI Express protocol layers, specifically addressing the Transaction/Data Link and Data Link/Physical layer interfaces. It answers how TLP flow control, sequence numbering, and framing are managed during inter-layer communication between the Data Link Layer (DLL) and neighboring PHY/Transaction layers.

children.title: Section 1.5.4.4.1 Transaction/Data Link Interface
children.description: This section details the Transaction/Data Link interface architecture, covering data framing, flow control, layer-to-layer signaling, and protocol-specific data integrity mechanisms. It addresses technical requirements for packet encapsulation, buffer management, and synchronization between the Transaction and Data Link layers.

children.title: Section 1.5.4.4.2 Data Link/Physical Interface
children.description: This section defines the architectural requirements for the Data Link/Physical layer interface, covering elastic buffering, multi-lane de-skew, DFT compliance patterns, and specific signal handshake mechanisms. It details the bidirectional exchange of TLP/DLLP framing information, power state management, and error reporting between the Transaction, Data Link, and Physical layers.

children.title: Chapter 2. Transaction Layer Specification
children.description: This section details the PCIe Transaction Layer architecture, covering Transaction Layer Packets (TLPs), ordering rules, Virtual Channel (VC) flow control, data integrity, and completion timeout mechanisms. It answers, "How are data packets formatted, sequenced, routed, and validated across the PCIe link to ensure reliable transaction completion and adherence to ordering models?"

children.title: Section 2.1 Transaction Layer Overview
children.description: This section outlines the PCI Express transaction layer architecture, covering distinct address spaces, supported transaction types, and standardized packet formatting. It defines the rules for initiating TLP (Transaction Layer Packet) exchanges and addresses how data routing, memory mapping, and I/O operations are structured within the interconnect hierarchy.

children.title: Section 2.1.1 Address Spaces, Transaction Types, and Usage
children.description: This section defines the Transaction Layer’s operational foundation within the PCIe architecture, covering pipelined split-transactions, credit-based flow control, TLP construction, and traffic ordering rules. It addresses how the protocol manages device resources, Virtual Channel (VC) differentiation, and the mapping of four distinct address spaces to specific transaction types between Requesters and Completers.

children.title: Section 2.1.1.2 I/O Transactions
children.description: This section outlines PCI Express transaction protocols, categorizing operations into Memory, I/O, Configuration, and Message address spaces. It details address formats (32-bit/64-bit), TLP prefix requirements like PASID, and the technical specification for legacy I/O compatibility versus modern memory-mapped data transfer.

children.title: Section 2.1.1.4 Message Transactions
children.description: Section 2.1.1.4 defines PCI Express Message Transactions and Configuration Transactions as mechanisms for device communication via Request/Completion packets and TLP structures. It specifies how vendors utilize the framework for Vendor-Defined Messages, addressing questions regarding packet composition, TLP header organization, and protocol interoperability standards.

children.title: Section 2.1.2 Packet Format Overview
children.description: This section defines the PCIe transaction layer packet (TLP) architecture, covering Configuration and Message transaction types, Vendor-Defined Message frameworks, and TLP structure requirements. It explains how packet data is organized, including headers, payloads, prefixes, and optional digests within Non-Flit Mode.

children.title: Section 2.2 Transaction Layer Protocol - Packet Definition
children.description: This section defines the Transaction Layer Protocol (TLP) packet structure, covering header fields, payload rules, routing mechanisms, and descriptor formats for Memory, I/O, Configuration, and Message transactions. It specifies technical requirements for byte enables, TLP digests, and prefixes, addressing how PCIe devices format and govern packet transmission in both Non-Flit and Flit modes.

children.title: Section 2.2.1 Common Packet Header Fields
children.description: This section defines the structural requirements for common packet headers, specifically distinguishing between non-flit and flit-mode communication protocols. It addresses fundamental framing specifications, including field organization, bit-level encoding, and data alignment requirements essential for interoperability in packet-switched architectures.

children.title: Section 2.2.1.1 Common Packet Header Fields for Non-Flit Mode
children.description: This section outlines the foundational structure, addressing formats, and transaction types for PCI Express Non-Flit Mode (NFM) Transaction Layer Packets (TLPs). It addresses requirements for Requests, Completions, Transaction ID matching, and the protocol for handling Reserved fields and mode-specific translations.

children.title: Section 2.2.1.2 Common Packet Header Fields for Flit Mode
children.description: This section defines the packet structure for PCI Express Flit Mode, detailing the TLP grammar, the mapping of the first DWORD, and the use of Orthogonal Header Content (OHC) for Steering Tags and metadata. It addresses how Flit Mode differentiates TLP field encoding, payload size calculation (1–1024 DW), and the mandated transmission of NOP TLPs during idle states.

children.title: Section 2.2.2 TLPs with Data Payloads - Rules
children.description: This section details rules for managing OHC-A5 (Option Header Component) inclusion in PCIe Transaction Layer Packets (TLPs) based on Segment Captured bits, Completion Status, and address alignment. It clarifies addressing requirements for Route by ID Messages and defines the conditional logic for attaching segment-related metadata to successful data payload completions.

children.title: Section 2.2.3 TLP Digest Rules - Non-Flit Mode Only
children.description: This section defines PCIe TLP Digest (ECRC) handling protocols in Non-Flit mode, specifying TD bit validation for Malformed TLP detection and Receiver ECRC interpretation requirements. It addresses how devices must handle digest fields based on support capabilities and includes an implementation note regarding Endian format interoperability for RC AtomicOp Completers.

children.title: Section 2.2.4 Routing and Addressing Rules
children.description: This section defines the architectural frameworks for Address-Based and ID-Based packet routing, detailing protocols for node identification, path selection, and logical addressing hierarchies. It addresses how systems resolve destination endpoints, manage network topology, and execute deterministic routing decisions based on unique identifiers versus routed network addresses.

children.title: Section 2.2.4.1 Address Based Routing Rules
children.description: This section outlines TLP routing mechanisms for NFM and FM configurations, specifying header formats, DW lengths, and address bit-widths for Memory, I/O, and Address Routed Message requests. It answers how Transaction Layer Packets are structured and addressed for routing based on their specific non-flit or flit mode architectural requirements.

children.title: Section 2.2.4.2 ID Based Routing Rules
children.description: This section defines ID-based routing rules and address mapping schemes for 32-bit versus 64-bit TLP addressing. It specifies mandatory 64-bit addressing requirements for UIO Requests, IDE TLPs, and ID-routed messages while outlining decoding constraints and the application of Bus, Device, and Function numbers.

children.title: Section 2.2.5 First/Last DW Byte Enables Rules
children.description: This section defines the mandatory constraints for First/Last Double Word (DW) Byte Enables in PCI Express transactions, detailing distinct protocol requirements for Non-Flit and Flit operational modes. It clarifies how to correctly signal data validity and partial-payload handling across varying data link layer configurations.

children.title: Section 2.2.5.1 Byte Enable Rules for Non-Flit Mode
children.description: This section delineates the TLP Byte Enable protocols for Non-Flit Mode, detailing how bit-level header fields manage Memory, I/O, and Configuration Requests. It defines specific functional repurposing for ST[7:0] fields in requests where the TH bit is set and establishes the header-positioning rules for First/Last DW Byte Enables.

children.title: Section 2.2.5.2 Byte Enable Rules for Flit Mode
children.description: This section outlines Byte Enable (BE) rules for PCIe Flit Mode, specifically addressing OHC-A1 inclusion requirements, default BE values for 1 DW and multi-DW memory requests, and flush semantic constraints for zero-length reads. It clarifies when optional header components are mandatory for non-reserved BE configurations and establishes mandatory Completer behavior to prevent side effects in flush-based traffic.

children.title: Section 2.2.6 Transaction Descriptor
children.description: This section defines the Transaction Descriptor structure, detailing the Transaction ID, Attributes, Relaxed/ID-Based Ordering, No Snoop, and Traffic Class fields. It addresses how PCIe transactions are identified, prioritized, and managed for ordering and cache coherency within the fabric.

children.title: Section 2.2.6.1 Overview
children.description: This section defines Byte Enable rules for PCIe Flit Mode, detailing mandatory “flush” semantics for zero-length Read requests and the specific conditions requiring Optional Header Component OHC-A1. It governs Memory Request formatting, implicit Byte Enable values, and the validation of PASID, PMR, and ER fields within the OHC-A1 header extension.

children.title: Section 2.2.6.2 Transaction Descriptor - Transaction ID Field
children.description: This section defines the PCIe **Transaction Descriptor**, specifically detailing the **Transaction ID** structure composed of **Requester ID**, **Tag**, and **Traffic Class (TC)** fields. It addresses how these elements function as a logical entity to associate **Completions** with **Requests** across three distinct namespace **Groups**.

children.title: Section 2.2.6.2.1 Tag Rules for Non-UIO Channel Operation
children.description: This section defines Tag rules for non-UIO channel operations, specifying the composition of Transaction IDs and uniqueness requirements for Group II and Group III requests. It establishes architectural standards for 5-bit, 8-bit, 10-bit, and 14-bit Tag sizes, clarifying the regulatory constraints for implementing larger-Tag Requester Enable bits in PCIe functions.

children.title: Section 2.2.6.2.2 Tag Rules for UIO Channel Operation
children.description: This section defines UIO channel operation requirements for 14-bit Tags, including Tag field transparency, cross-hierarchy Transaction ID handling, and Attribute-based transaction modification. It addresses technical specifications for supporting larger-tag namespaces within UIO Completers and Requesters while clarifying the scope regarding heterogenous tag-support configurations.

children.title: Section 2.2.6.3 Transaction Descriptor - Attributes Field
children.description: This section details 14-bit Tag requirements, Attribute field functionality, and hierarchy-based Transaction ID handling for UIO transactions. It addresses how UIO Requesters and Completers manage Tag namespaces, hardware coherency, and ordering under varying system-wide Extended Tag support.

children.title: Section 2.2.6.4 Relaxed Ordering and ID-Based Ordering Attributes
children.description: This section defines the PCIe Attributes field, detailing the bit-wise configurations for Relaxed Ordering and ID-Based Ordering (IDO). It clarifies the structural differences between Flit and Non-Flit mode header layouts and explains how these attributes modify the standard PCI Strongly Ordered model.

children.title: Section 2.2.6.6 Transaction Descriptor - Traffic Class Field
children.description: This section defines the 3-bit Traffic Class (TC) field used to label PCI Express Transaction Layer Packets (TLPs) for end-to-end traffic differentiation and prioritization. It explains how TC labels facilitate packet routing through Virtual Channels (VCs) and clarifies the application of the No Snoop attribute for cache coherency management.

children.title: Section 2.2.7 Memory, I/O, and Configuration Request Rules
children.description: Section 2.2.7 defines formal rules for Memory, I/O, and Configuration request transactions by comparing operational constraints between Non-Flit and Flit mode protocols. This section details packet formatting, transaction ordering, and completion requirements essential for ensuring bus protocol compliance and data integrity.

children.title: Section 2.2.7.1 Non-Flit Mode
children.description: This section outlines the protocol requirements for PCI Express Memory, I/O, and Configuration Requests in Non-Flit Mode. It defines specific header field compositions, including Transaction ID formats, byte enable repurposing for steering tags, and request length constraints, addressing how these packets must be structured and routed via 32-bit or 64-bit addressing.

children.title: Section 2.2.7.1.1 TPH Rules – Non-Flit Mode
children.description: This section defines strict header field constraints and malformed TLP error conditions for PCIe Configuration Requests in Non-Flit mode. It specifies mandatory values for TC, Attr, AT, Length, and Byte Enable fields, detailing receiver-side enforcement requirements and the structural layout of the Configuration Request header.

children.title: Section 2.2.7.2 Flit Mode
children.description: This section defines the TLP header specifications for Flit Mode, specifically detailing Steering Tag (ST) field placements, Transaction ID compositions, and modified Byte Enable rules for Memory, I/O, and Configuration Requests. It establishes how PCIe functions must handle Steering Tags and TPH Completer capabilities when operating in Flit versus Non-Flit architectural modes.

children.title: Section 2.2.8 Message Request Rules
children.description: Section 2.2.8 details the structural requirements and field definitions for PCIe Message Requests, including the TLP types (Msg, MsgD), Message Code specifications, and common header fields like Requester ID and EP. This section establishes the standardized protocol for signaling categories such as INTx interrupts, Error Signaling, and PTM, answering how various control and status messages are formatted and routed within the PCIe fabric.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.1 INTx Interrupt Signaling - Rules
children.description: This section defines PCIe message routing protocols, specifically addressing INTx virtual wire signaling, message routing encodings (r[2:0]), and the mandatory usage of Optional Header Components (OHC-A4/A1) for PASID-enabled or inter-segment routing. It establishes the technical requirements for interrupt delivery mechanisms, specifying how messages are routed to the Root Complex, by ID, or as local terminations within the PCI Express fabric.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.2 Power Management Messages
children.description: This section specifies the protocol rules for PCI Express Power Management Messages (PM_Active_State_Nak and PM_PME), detailing constraints on TLP payload, routing, Requester ID fields, and mandatory TC0 traffic class requirements. It answers technical requirements for message handling, error reporting of Malformed TLPs, and specific routing support across Root Complexes, Endpoints, Switches, and Bridges.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.3 Error Signaling Messages
children.description: Section 2.2.8.3 defines the protocol requirements for PCI Express Error Signaling Messages, including `ERR_COR` and `ERR_NONFATAL`, specifying message formatting, TC0 traffic class requirements, and Requester ID assignment. It details how agents report interface errors to switches and system components while outlining header constraints for non-data TLP message types.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.4 Locked Transactions Support
children.description: This section defines the structural formatting, message encoding, and field specifications for PCI Express `ERR_COR` messages in both Non-Flit and Flit modes. It details the `ERR_COR` Subclass (ECS) encodings for signaling DPC, SFI, AER, or RP PIO events, while introducing the `Unlock` message mechanism required for PCIe Lock Transaction sequences.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.5 Slot Power Limit Support
children.description: This section defines the PCIe message protocols for "Unlock" and "Set_Slot_Power_Limit" messaging, detailing packet structure, TLP types, routing requirements, and TC0 traffic class enforcement. It establishes the technical standards necessary for a Downstream Port to communicate power limitation values to an attached component's Upstream Port, including mandatory Malformed TLP error handling protocols.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.6 Vendor-Defined Messages
children.description: This section details the protocol requirements for `Set_Slot_Power_Limit` message handling, including specific exceptions for planar-integrated components and system board power reporting. It establishes the mandatory format and implementation rules for vendor-defined messages, clarifying the usage of Requester IDs and routing mechanisms in PCIe messaging extensions.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.6.1 PCI-SIG Defined VDMs
children.description: Section 2.2.8.6.1 defines the structure and formation rules for PCI-SIG Vendor-Defined Messages (VDMs), specifically detailing Message Code 0111 1111b, Vendor ID 0001h, and Subtype encoding. It addresses how PCIe endpoints process these messages and specifies the mandatory layout requirements for the VDM header to ensure proper identification by a Completer.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.6.2 Device Readiness Status (DRS) Message
children.description: This section defines the Device Readiness Status (DRS) message format, specifying compliance rules for PCI-SIG-Defined Vendor-Defined Messages (VDMs) using TLP routing, Subtype 08h, and local termination. It establishes technical parameters for TLP formation, field constraints, and handling of malformed TLP errors to ensure compliant device-to-host signaling.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.6.3 Function Readiness Status Message (FRS Message)
children.description: This section defines the structural format and protocol requirements for the Function Readiness Status (FRS) message using the PCI-SIG-Defined Vendor-Defined Message (VDM) mechanism. It details TLP header configuration—including Subtype 09h and specific FRS Reason encodings—to answer how system components signal Device Readiness Status (DRS) receipt and D3Hot-to-D0 transition completion.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.6.4 Hierarchy ID Message
children.description: This section defines the routing and structural requirements for PCI Express Hierarchy ID and Function Readiness Status (FRS) messages, including mandatory Root Complex routing and TLP formatting specifications. It addresses how compliant receivers validate message fields, identify Malformed TLPs, and interpret FRS non-flit and flit mode frame layouts.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.7 Ignored Messages
children.description: This section details the formatting requirements for "Hierarchy ID" PCIe messages in both Non-Flit and Flit modes, utilizing System GUIDs and specific requester identifiers. It defines legacy constraints for obsolete Hot-Plug signaling, clarifying that such messages are no longer supported and must adhere to [PCIe-1.0a] standards if implemented.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.8 Latency Tolerance Reporting (LTR) Message
children.description: This section defines the Latency Tolerance Reporting (LTR) message protocol within the PCIe architecture, specifying TLP formation rules, flow control requirements, and handling procedures for ignored messages listed in Table 2-32. It clarifies how Receivers process LTR Transaction Layer Packets (TLPs) and outlines the structural constraints—such as payload absence and default Traffic Class requirements—necessary for reporting device service latency tolerances.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.9 Optimized Buffer Flush/Fill (OBFF) Message
children.description: This section specifies the Optimized Buffer Flush/Fill (OBFF) Message format and signaling requirements used to communicate platform power management states to PCI Express Endpoints. It details TLP construction rules—including Message Code, Requester ID, and Reserved Length fields—to define how link-layer components coordinate buffer resource utilization and power-saving efficiencies.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.10 Precision Time Measurement (PTM) Messages
children.description: This section defines the structural requirements and packet formats for PCI Express Precision Time Measurement (PTM) and Optimized Buffer Flush/Fill (OBFF) messages. It specifies mandatory Traffic Class (TC0) constraints, Malformed TLP error handling procedures, and bit-level layout definitions for both Non-Flit and Flit mode message headers.

children.title: Section 2.2.8.11 Integrity and Data Encryption (IDE) Messages
children.description: This section details the formatting, routing, and traffic class requirements for PCIe Integrity and Data Encryption (IDE) Sync and Fail messages. It defines how to manage RID-based addressing and protocol-specific constraints for Link versus Selective IDE streams.

children.title: Section 2.2.9 Completion Rules
children.description: This section specifies the protocol requirements and message formats for PCIe IDE (Integrity and Data Encryption) Fail Messages and standard Completion header structures. It defines the routing and field definitions for Flit and non-Flit mode failure notifications, answering how failures in integrity-protected streams are identified and how Completion Requests are formatted for Read, Write, and AtomicOp transactions.

children.title: Section 2.2.9.1 Completion Rules for Non-Flit Mode
children.description: This section defines PCIe Non-Flit mode completion routing using 3 DW headers and Requester ID-based addressing. It clarifies mandatory TLP header fields, including Completer ID, Completion Status, Byte Count encoding, and Tag utilization for transaction tracking.

children.title: Section 2.2.9.2 Completion Rules for Flit Mode
children.description: This section specifies PCIe Flit Mode completion rules, detailing Requester ID, Tag, Traffic Class, Attribute consistency, and reserved bit handling. It defines requirements for Completer ID generation, including UR (Unsupported Request) status handling for Multi-Function Devices (MFD) and transient post-configuration state expectations.

children.title: Section 2.2.10 TLP Prefix Rules
children.description: This section details the architectural requirements for TLP Prefixes, covering distinct operational frameworks for Non-Flit and Flit Mode synchronization. It addresses critical technical specifications regarding Local and End-to-End TLP Prefix processing, routing rules, and header manipulation protocols within the PCIe interconnect.

children.title: Section 2.2.10.1 TLP Prefix General Rules - Non-Flit Mode
children.description: This section details the formatting, placement, and architectural constraints for TLP prefixes within Non-Flit Mode PCIe transactions. It governs rules for prefix-header ordering, field validation, and TLP structure to ensure compatibility with legacy transaction layer processing.

children.title: Section 2.2.10.2 Local TLP Prefix Processing
children.description: This section defines the logic for calculating coalesced TLP Completion status and specifies formatting requirements for UIORdCpl/UIOWrCpl payloads, including reserved bit fields and Protocol Prefix identification within the Non-Posted Flow Mapping (NFM). It addresses how to resolve status conflicts during UIO completion coalescing and clarifies the signaling of Local versus End-End TLP Prefixes using Fmt and Type field encoding.

children.title: Section 2.2.10.2.1 Vendor Defined Local TLP Prefix
children.description: This section defines the structural and configuration requirements for **Vendor Defined Local TLP Prefixes** (Types `1110` and `1111`) within the PCI Express protocol. It specifies error-handling procedures for unsupported prefix types, ECRC exclusion rules, and interoperability mandates governing the implementation of these custom TLP headers.

children.title: Section 2.2.10.3 Flit Mode Local TLP Prefix
children.description: This section defines the "Flit Mode Local TLP Prefix" and its associated type encoding (1101) within the PCIe specification. It establishes mandatory error-handling requirements for unsupported TLP prefixes, clarifies ECRC exclusion, and outlines interoperability rules for Vendor Defined Local TLP Prefixes.

children.title: Section 2.2.10.4 End-End TLP Prefix Processing - Non-Flit Mode
children.description: This section defines the structural requirements and error-handling protocols for End-End TLP Prefixes, specifically addressing the "Flit Mode Local TLP Prefix" in Non-Flit Mode (NFM) environments. It outlines technical specifications for flow control credit usage, identifies valid TLP Prefix types (TPH, PASID, IDE, and Vendor Defined) via the E[3:0] field, and establishes compliance rules for Malformed TLP detection.

children.title: Section 2.2.10.4.1 Vendor Defined End-End TLP Prefix
children.description: This section defines the structural format and decoding requirements for Vendor Defined End-End TLP Prefixes within PCIe packets. It addresses technical specifications for TLP Prefix routing, vendor-specific field identification, and the rules governing how Receivers interpret these non-header, end-to-end information headers.

children.title: Section 2.2.10.4.2 Root Ports with End-End TLP Prefix Supported
children.description: This section defines the operational constraints and requirements for routing Vendor Defined End-End TLP Prefixes (VendPrefixE0/E1) across PCI Express Root Ports. It specifies configuration protocols, the mandatory use of the End-End TLP Prefix Supported bit in the Device Capabilities 2 register, and the protocol for handling Unsupported Requests (UR) when peer-to-peer routing between Root Ports is not supported.

children.title: Section 2.2.11 OHC-E Rules - Flit Mode
children.description: This section defines mandatory enablement mechanisms and interoperability guidelines for using VendPrefixE0 and VendPrefixE1 End-End TLP Prefixes within a PCI Express topology. It clarifies the implementation requirements for optional peer-to-peer routing between Root Ports, outlining how the End-End TLP Prefix Supported bit functions and the mandatory handling of unsupported routing paths as Unsupported Requests (UR).

children.title: Section 2.3 Handling of Received TLPs
children.description: This section defines protocol-level TLP processing rules for incoming packets, specifically addressing Flow Control (FC) type violations, TLP Prefix parsing in Non-Flit and Flit modes, and error handling for oversized OHC-E fields. It establishes compliance requirements for ingress blocking, TLP header validation, and error reporting for Unsupported Requests or Unexpected Completions.

children.title: Section 2.3.1 Request Handling Rules
children.description: This section defines the PCIe routing and processing logic for incoming Request Transaction Layer Packets (TLPs), detailing decision workflows for Message handling, egress port propagation, and Unsupported Request (UR) error signaling. It addresses how switches evaluate Message Code validity, TLP type compatibility, and device-specific configuration settings to determine whether to process, route, drop, or reject a packet.

children.title: Section 2.3.1.1 Data Return for Non-UIO Read Requests
children.description: This section defines PCI Express protocol requirements for data return behavior regarding non-UIO Read Request completions, covering data fragmentation, Maximum Payload Size (MPS) limits, and completion status constraints. It addresses technical specifications for multi-packet data transmission, Completion (Cpl/CplLk) types, and the Request Retry Status (RRS) mechanism used during configuration initialization.

children.title: Section 2.3.1.2 UIO Read Completions
children.description: This section outlines protocols for UIO (Upstream IO) Read Completions, specifically detailing relaxed address ordering requirements and restricted programming model exemptions for Completer Abort statuses. It clarifies how PCIe devices handle payload granularity, RCB (Read Completion Boundary) alignment, and multi-packet completion strategies for non-standard read request sizes.

children.title: Section 2.3.1.3 UIO Write Completions
children.description: This section defines the technical specifications for UIO Write Completions, covering Write Completion Boundaries (WCB), completion coalescing rules, and handling of Unexpected Completions. It clarifies how Completers manage transaction IDs, address fragmentation, and TLP field validation for outstanding UIO requests.

children.title: Section 2.3.2 Completion Handling Rules
children.description: This section defines UIO Write Completion protocols, specifically addressing 64B Write Completion Boundary (WCB) constraints, permissible address ordering, and mandatory rules for completion coalescing. It clarifies error handling requirements for Unexpected Completions, including criteria for Transaction ID mismatches and TLP field validation.

children.title: Section 2.4 Transaction Ordering
children.description: This section outlines PCIe transaction ordering rules, covering TLP sequencing, UIO stream protocols, and the atomicity associated with read/write granularity. It specifies requirements for maintaining producer-consumer ordering, system-level memory consistency, and the precise conditions under which data updates become visible to subsequent read requests.

children.title: Section 2.4.1 Transaction Ordering Rules for TLPs not using UIO or Flow-Through IDE Streams
children.description: This section defines PCI Express transaction ordering and error-handling requirements for TLPs, specifically detailing the handling of non-successful Completion statuses, including Unsupported Request (UR) and Completer Abort (CA). It addresses how Completers report errors, specifies that non-successful Completions terminate read or atomic requests without returning data, and explains the resulting configuration requirements for Root Ports and RCRB headers.

children.title: Section 2.4.2 Ordering Rules for UIO
children.description: This section defines the mandatory TLP ordering and deadlock avoidance rules for Untranslated IO (UIO) transactions, specifically addressing flow-control (FC) dependencies for requests and completions. It answers how UIO TLPs relate to reordering constraints, interaction with non-UIO traffic, and the architectural requirements for maintaining ingress and egress acceptance in downstream ports.

children.title: Section 2.4.3 Update Ordering and Granularity Observed by a Read Transaction
children.description: This section defines the consistency models, update ordering, and memory granularity guarantees provided to read transactions. It details how Non-UIO and UIO reads interact with system memory, addressing technical questions regarding transaction visibility, execution ordering, and atomicity constraints within the memory hierarchy.

children.title: Section 2.4.3.1 Ordering and Granularity for Non-UIO Reads
children.description: This section defines the architectural boundaries regarding ordering and data granularity for concurrent non-UIO read transactions and buffer updates. It addresses how PCI Express fabric specifications handle non-deterministic data consistency when host CPUs or external entities update memory buffers during an active read request.

children.title: Section 2.4.3.2 Ordering and Granularity for UIO Reads
children.description: This section defines the mandatory atomic ordering and 64B granularity requirements for concurrent UIO (User-space I/O) Read and Write Requests to ensure data consistency. It specifies that UIO Writes must be observed as either fully complete or not-at-all within 64B aligned blocks and explains how Completers may enforce these constraints via restricted programming models or Request termination.

children.title: Section 2.4.4 Update Ordering and Granularity Provided by a Write Transaction
children.description: This section details how hardware architectures manage update sequence and access granularity for write transactions, specifically differentiating between standard memory operations and Updatable I/O (UIO) protocols. It addresses critical questions regarding data consistency, transaction ordering rules, and the byte-level granularity constraints applied to distinct interconnect traffic types.

children.title: Section 2.4.4.1 Ordering and Granularity for Non-UIO Writes
children.description: This section defines memory consistency models, atomicity requirements, and write-ordering constraints for non-UIO (Uncacheable I/O) memory operations. It addresses how hardware handles transaction granularity and serialization to ensure data integrity during non-UIO bus cycles.

children.title: Section 2.4.4.2 Ordering and Granularity for UIO Writes
children.description: This section defines the ordering and atomicity requirements for UIO Writes and Reads within 64B aligned blocks, ensuring consistency across PCIe Links and Ports. It addresses how memory consistency is maintained during concurrent read-write operations and establishes the conditions under which a Requester can guarantee an update is fully completed.

children.title: Section 2.5 Virtual Channel (VC) Mechanism
children.description: This section details the Virtual Channel (VC) mechanism, covering Traffic Class (TC) mapping, independent Flow Control, and hardware resource optimization via Shared Flow Control in Flit Mode. It addresses how PCIe fabrics manage bottleneck-free data transmission and enforce 64B update granularity requirements for BAR-mapped resources.

children.title: Section 2.5.1 Virtual Channel Identification (VC ID)
children.description: This section defines the Virtual Channel Identification (VC ID) field, detailing its bit-width, encoding structure, and role in hardware-level packet multiplexing. It answers how distinct data flows are uniquely addressed and logically separated within a shared physical interconnect architecture.

children.title: Section 2.5.2 TC to VC Mapping
children.description: This section details the architectural requirements for mapping PCIe Traffic Classes (TCs) to Virtual Channels (VCs), specifically addressing VC ID uniqueness, cross-Link configuration symmetry, and Multi-Function Device (MFD) resource allocation. It defines the procedural rules for managing hardware VC resources and Flow Control accounting to ensure consistent TLP traffic prioritization across the PCIe Link.

children.title: Section 2.5.3 VC and TC Rules
children.description: This section details the architectural requirements for PCI Express Traffic Class (TC) and Virtual Channel (VC) mapping, covering flow control, ordering constraints, and error handling for Malformed TLPs. It defines the implementation obligations for Endpoints, Switches, and Root Complexes, addressing how TC-to-VC arbitration impacts TLP routing, peer-to-peer capabilities, and configuration compliance.

children.title: Section 2.6 Ordering and Receive Buffer Flow Control
children.description: This section details PCIe Flow Control and TC/VC mapping requirements, specifically addressing error handling for unmapped Traffic Classes (TCs) and the point-to-point nature of buffer management. It defines the architectural constraints for Root Ports, switches, and Multi-Function Devices (MFDs) while clarifying that Flow Control ensures link-level buffer integrity rather than end-to-end delivery.

children.title: Section 2.6.1 Flow Control (FC) Rules
children.description: This section defines PCIe Flow Control (FC) mechanics, detailing how Transaction Layer Packets (TLPs) are regulated via Data Link Layer Packets (DLLPs) and credit-based accounting. It addresses requirements for FC unit sizing, credit-based gatekeeping, and the exclusion of framing symbols or link-level errors from credit management.

children.title: Section 2.6.1.1 FC Information Tracked by Transmitter
children.description: This section details flow control (FC) mechanisms for tracking `CREDITS_CONSUMED` in TLP transmission, distinguishing between Non-Flit and Flit modes. It explains the technical motivations for shared credit blocks, addressing how buffer management, fragmentation, and complex TLP storage constraints influence credit-based flow control implementation.

children.title: Section 2.6.1.2 FC Information Tracked by Receiver
children.description: This section defines the PCIe Flow Control (FC) gating logic, detailing the Equation 2-13 transmitter gate formula used to validate TLP transmission eligibility against credit limits. It covers credit consumption rules, Flit Mode requirements, TLP ordering constraints during stalls, and clarifies that credit returns are strictly for buffer management rather than transaction completion status.

children.title: Section 2.7 End-to-End Data Integrity
children.description: This section covers PCI Express end-to-end data protection mechanisms, specifically focusing on ECRC rules, error reporting, and Data Poisoning (error forwarding). It addresses how transactions maintain integrity between endpoints and how hardware detects and propagates corrupted data packets across the fabric.

children.title: Section 2.7.1 ECRC Rules
children.description: This section defines the implementation of End-to-End Cyclic Redundancy Check (ECRC) mechanisms for PCI Express Transaction Layer Packet (TLP) integrity, specifically addressing TLP invariant fields, Switch forwarding requirements, and error poisoning processes. It answers how systems ensure data integrity across intermediate network components and how corrupted payloads are signaled to the consuming device.

children.title: Section 2.7.2 Error Forwarding (Data Poisoning)
children.description: This section defines Error Forwarding (data poisoning) in PCIe, covering the EP bit and Physical Layer Logical Block mechanisms used to handle corrupted data payloads. It details standardized receiver requirements for poisoned TLPs and specifies permissible use cases for Read/AtomicOp/Write data while excluding headers.

children.title: Section 2.7.2.1 Rules For Use of Data Poisoning
children.description: This section outlines the rules for using TLP data poisoning, covering the EP (Error Poisoning) bit, NFM-to-FM translation requirements, and constraints on payload-bearing packets. It answers how poisoned data is handled by the Data Link Layer, when transmitters may apply poisoning, and how routing elements must preserve error indicators during format conversions.

children.title: Section 2.8 Completion Timeout Mechanism
children.description: This section defines the mandatory error-handling protocols for poisoned TLP (Transaction Layer Packet) requests targeting control registers or structures. It establishes requirements for Completer behavior—specifically identifying "Poisoned TLP Received" errors and Unsupported Request (UR) status—and clarifies Switch routing obligations when such malicious or corrupted data is encountered.

children.title: Section 2.9 Link Status Dependencies
children.description: This section details PCI Express Transaction Layer operational states, specifically addressing protocol behavior during **DL_Down**, **DL_Up**, and **Downstream Port Containment (DPC)** events. It provides technical requirements for packet handling, link integrity maintenance, and error containment responses when the physical data link status fluctuates.

children.title: Section 2.9.1 Transaction Layer Behavior in DL_Down Status
children.description: This section details Transaction Layer protocols during a `DL_Down` Link state, covering Completion Timeout mechanics for UIO/Configuration Requests and the impact of PCI Express-to-PCI/PCI-X Bridge retry configurations. It specifically addresses how the system handles non-operational Links when DPC is not triggered.

children.title: Section 2.9.2 Transaction Layer Behavior in DL_Up Status
children.description: This section details PCI Express Transaction Layer requirements during a `DL_Up` status transition, focusing on error handling protocols, Completion generation for Non-Posted and UIO Requests, and the management of PME_Turn_Off handshakes and Vendor-Defined Messages. It defines how the Transaction Layer processes or discards pending Requests when the link status prevents successful transmission or completion to the targeted Function.

children.title: Section 2.9.3 Transaction Layer Behavior During Downstream Port Containment
children.description: This section details Transaction Layer behavior during Downstream Port Containment (DPC), specifically addressing TLP handling requirements, LTSSM Disabled state transitions, and the silent discarding of Upstream TLPs. It defines the operational constraints imposed on Downstream Ports when the DPC Trigger Status bit is set, ensuring system integrity following link fault events.

children.title: Chapter 3. Data Link Layer Specification
children.description: This section details the Data Link Layer’s operational foundation, covering state machine management, feature exchange, flow control initialization, DLLP formatting, and data integrity mechanisms. It addresses how link-level connectivity is established, how error detection is enforced, and how data transmission is synchronized between communicating nodes.

children.title: Section 3.1 Data Link Layer Overview
children.description: This section defines the Data Link Layer as the PCIe architectural intermediary responsible for reliable Transaction Layer Packet (TLP) exchange between components. It details essential mechanisms, including data transmission flows, TLP sequence number generation, LCRC calculation, and recovery services via Data Link Layer Retry.

children.title: Section 3.2 Data Link Control and Management State Machine
children.description: This section details the Data Link Layer’s responsibilities, including TLP/DLLP error detection, link state management, and power control mechanisms. It explains how point-to-point data integrity is maintained using CRC checks and sequence numbers, while defining the operational distinctions between DLLPs and routed TLPs.

children.title: Section 3.2.1 Data Link Control and Management State Machine Rules
children.description: This section defines the Data Link Layer state machine behavior, covering initialization, flow control, and feature exchange states from `DL_Inactive` to `DL_Active`. It specifies reset procedures, buffer management, and status signaling requirements for PCI Express links, answering how the component transitions between operational states after a hardware reset.

children.title: Section 3.3 Data Link Feature Exchange
children.description: Section 3.3 defines the Data Link Feature Exchange protocol, detailing error-blocking conditions, dependency requirements for Flit Mode and 16.0 GT/s operations, and the structure of the Data Link Feature Extended Capability. It clarifies mandatory versus optional implementation criteria, recovery mechanisms for DL_Active transitions, and the use of status fields for managing local and remote feature support.

children.title: Section 3.4 Flow Control Initialization Protocol
children.description: This section outlines the Flow Control Initialization Protocol, covering state machine transitions, operational rules, and scaled flow control mechanisms for managing data credit allocation. It defines the handshake procedures and logic required to establish reliable buffer credit exchanges between connected network ports.

children.title: Section 3.4.1 Flow Control Initialization State Machine Rules
children.description: This section defines the Flow Control Initialization State Machine rules, specifically detailing Flit Mode TLP packing requirements and architectural constraints for VC0 and additional Virtual Channel activation. It addresses technical parameters for transmitter compliance, TLP-per-half-flit encoding, and mandatory initialization procedures required following power-up or interconnect resets.

children.title: Section 3.4.2 Scaled Flow Control
children.description: Section 3.4.2 defines Scaled Flow Control, a mechanism for high-speed PCIe links (16.0 GT/s+) that expands credit limits beyond the standard HdrFC and DataFC bit-widths. It specifies the configuration rules for InitFC and UpdateFC DLLPs, detailing how HdrScale and DataScale fields are used to manage flow control performance at high round-trip latencies.

children.title: Section 3.5 Data Link Layer Packets (DLLPs)
children.description: This section details the **Scaled Flow Control** mechanism for **PCIe Data Link Layer Packets (DLLPs)**, specifying rules for **HdrScale** and **DataScale** fields based on link speed requirements. It defines how to manage **flow control credit** limitations at 16.0 GT/s and higher by transitioning from standard credit counters to scaled values in **InitFC** and **UpdateFC** packets.

children.title: Section 3.5.1 Data Link Layer Packet Rules
children.description: This section defines Data Link Layer Packet (DLLP) structure, types, and formatting rules, distinguishing between Non-Flit Mode (NFM) and Flit Mode requirements. It catalogs control packets—including Ack, Nak, Flow Control, Power Management, and Link Management—and specifies the mandatory inclusion of DLLP Type, type-specific information, and CRC fields.

children.title: Section 3.6 Data Integrity Mechanisms
children.description: This section details PCIe Data Link Layer integrity, covering Link Cyclic Redundancy Checks (LCRC), TLP Sequence Numbers, and hardware-level TLP retry mechanisms. It specifies error detection and recovery protocols for ensuring reliable packet delivery between Transmitters and Receivers in both flit and non-flit operating modes.

children.title: Section 3.6.1 Introduction
children.description: This section details PCI Express Data Link Layer integrity mechanisms, specifically focusing on TLP Sequence Number application, LCRC error detection, and Data Link Layer Retry recovery procedures. It defines how the Transaction Layer facilitates boundary identification for successful TLP validation and link-level fault handling.

children.title: Section 3.6.2 LCRC, TLP Sequence Number, and Retry Management (TLP Transmitter)
children.description: This section defines transmitter responsibilities for LCRC calculation, TLP sequence number assignment, and hardware-level retry management within PCIe link layer operations. It addresses protocol compliance requirements for error detection and reliable data recovery by detailing how transmitters manage replay buffers, sequence numbering, and DLLP-based flow control in both Non-Flit and Flit modes.

children.title: Section 3.6.2.1 LCRC and TLP Sequence Number Rules (TLP Transmitter)
children.description: This section details the Data Link Layer procedures for appending Sequence Numbers and Link CRC (LCRC) to TLPs to ensure packet integrity within the retry buffer protocol. It specifies framing constraints for non-flit mode, Protocol Multiplexing (PMUX) identifier requirements, and the recovery sequence triggered when persistent transmission failures necessitate Link retraining or state transition to DL_Inactive.

children.title: Section 3.6.2.2 Handling of Received DLLPs (Non-Flit Mode)
children.description: This section outlines the Data Link Layer’s error-handling protocol for received DLLPs in Non-Flit Mode, specifically addressing Physical Layer signaling, CRC validation, and the disposal of corrupt or unsupported packets. It defines the mandatory processing flow—including error reporting compliance and handling of reserved fields—to ensure reliable Link Layer communication.

children.title: Section 3.6.2.3 Handling of Received DLLPs (Flit Mode)
children.description: This section details the PCIe Data Link Layer's procedure for processing Ack/Nak DLLPs within Flit Mode, specifically governing retry buffer management, sequence number synchronization (ACKD_SEQ), and replay initiation logic. It defines the technical requirements for discarding or purging transmitted TLPs and explains how a receiver handles Flow Control DLLPs and Nak-triggered transmission errors.

children.title: Section 3.6.3 LCRC and TLP Sequence Number (TLP Receiver) (Non-Flit Mode)
children.description: This section details the TLP Receiver's mechanisms for LCRC validation and Sequence Number verification in Non-Flit Mode. It addresses technical requirements for error detection, integrity checking, and protocol compliance within the PCI Express Transaction Layer.

children.title: Section 3.6.3.1 LCRC and TLP Sequence Number Rules (TLP Receiver)
children.description: This section outlines the PCIe Data Link Layer procedure for verifying TLP integrity using LCRC and Sequence Numbers, while detailing the conceptual state machines—specifically `NEXT_RCV_SEQ`, `NAK_SCHEDULED`, and `AckNak_LATENCY_TIMER`—required to manage TLP reception and Data Link Layer Retry. It answers how a receiver validates incoming packets for corruption, tracks expected sequence numbers, and schedules acknowledgement signaling to maintain link flow control.

children.title: Chapter 4. Physical Layer Logical Block
children.description: This chapter details the Physical Layer (PHY) architecture, covering logical sub-block operations, signal conditioning via retimers, and electrical interface specifications. It addresses critical questions regarding data transmission integrity, protocol-defined sub-block functionality, and the structural requirements for high-speed link optimization.

children.title: Section 4.2 Logical Sub-block
children.description: This section defines the Physical Layer’s logical sub-block, detailing its role in coordinating transceiver states, managing data transmission paths, and handling protocol-specific encoding (8b/10b, 128b/130b, 1b/1b) and stream modes (Flit vs. Non-Flit). It answers how incoming/outgoing data is prepared for the electrical sub-block and how PCI Express structures data streams between the Data Link and Physical layers.

children.title: Section 4.2.1 8b/10b Encoding for 2.5 GT/s and 5.0 GT/s Data Rates
children.description: This section details 8b/10b line coding protocols for PCIe 2.5 GT/s and 5.0 GT/s data rates, covering symbol encoding, framing, lane mapping, and LFSR-based data scrambling. It addresses how raw data bytes are transformed into 10-bit symbols to ensure DC balance and clock recovery, while explaining the structural requirements for serializing data across physical lanes.

children.title: Section 4.2.1.1 Symbol Encoding
children.description: This section details the 8b/10b transmission encoding used at 2.5 and 5.0 GT/s, mapping 8-bit data characters into 10-bit symbols via ANSI X3.230-1994 standards. It additionally provides implementation notes regarding the negotiation and identification of Flit Mode, covering relevant hardware registers and status variables.

children.title: Section 4.2.1.1.1 Serialization and De-serialization of Data
children.description: This section outlines 8b/10b encoding and decoding protocols, detailing the bit-level serialization process where 8-bit data bytes are mapped to 10-bit symbols. It defines the transmission sequence, specifying that bits are placed on physical lanes in an order starting from ‘a’ to ‘j’ to facilitate high-speed data flow.

children.title: Section 4.2.1.1.2 Special Symbols for Framing and Link Management (K Codes)
children.description: This section details the 8b/10b encoding scheme, focusing on Special Symbols (K-codes) required for frame signaling, link management, and protocol distinction between DLLPs and TLPs. It defines the physical lane bit transmission order and clarifies when these framing symbols are applied versus when Flit Mode operations supersede them.

children.title: Section 4.2.1.1.3 8b/10b Decode Rules
children.description: This section defines 8b/10b symbol encoding rules for PCIe, specifying the operational distinctions for control characters like STP, SDP, END, and EDB between Flit and Non-Flit modes. It serves as a technical reference to determine valid symbol usage, framing conventions, and link state management behaviors across different data rates.

children.title: Section 4.2.1.2 Framing and Application of Symbols to Lanes
children.description: This section details the physical layer framing and lane mapping protocols for Transaction Layer Packets (TLPs) and Data Link Layer Packets (DLLPs) when operating in non-Flit mode. It defines symbol distribution, alignment mechanisms, and link-width lane association required for standard PCIe packet transmission.

children.title: Section 4.2.1.2.1 Framing and Application of Symbols to Lanes for TLPs and DLLPs in Non-Flit Mode
children.description: This subsection defines 8b/10b encoding requirements, specifically addressing Transmitter/Receiver disparity logic, electrical idle transitions, and error-handling protocols. It outlines how Physical Layer implementations detect and report invalid symbols or disparity violations to the Data Link Layer in accordance with PCIe Non-Flit Mode specifications.

children.title: Section 4.2.1.3 Data Scrambling
children.description: This section details PCIe data scrambling using Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) logic to improve link electrical characteristics. It defines the implementation requirements for 8b/10b encoding synchronization, multi-lane skew management, and the architectural integration of scrambling at the Physical Layer.

children.title: Section 4.2.2 128b/130b Encoding for 8.0 GT/s, 16.0 GT/s, and 32.0 GT/s Data Rates
children.description: This section defines the 128b/130b scrambling architecture and linear feedback shift register (LFSR) operation for PCIe data rates from 8.0 to 32.0 GT/s. It addresses technical requirements for COM symbol initialization, LFSR seeding, and specific rules for scrambling exclusion regarding K codes, Ordered Sets, and compliance patterns.

children.title: Section 4.2.2.1 Lane Level Encoding
children.description: This section defines the 128b/130b lane-level encoding mechanism, specifying Sync Header formats (10b, 01b), block structures, and bit-transmission ordering. It explains how physical layer data and ordered sets are encapsulated into 130-bit blocks for multi-lane link transmission.

children.title: Section 4.2.2.2 Ordered Set Blocks
children.description: This section defines the structural requirements and formatting constraints for Ordered Set Blocks within the data stream. It addresses technical specifications for block alignment and control character sequences, answering how systems must organize and synchronize data packets to ensure protocol-compliant transmission.

children.title: Section 4.2.2.2.1 Block Alignment
children.description: Section 4.2.2.2.1 describes the **Block Alignment** mechanism for multi-Lane links, detailing how **Ordered Set Blocks**, **Sync Headers**, and **Symbol** distribution function across a x4 Link. It answers how synchronous transmission is maintained across lanes during link training and how Ordered Set structures, including SKP variants, are organized in both Flit and Non-Flit modes.

children.title: Section 4.2.2.3 Data Blocks
children.description: Section 4.2.2.3 details data block transmission protocols, specifically addressing framing token usage, link-level transmitter/receiver requirements, and error recovery mechanisms. Technical coverage includes distinct operability procedures for Flit and Non-Flit modes, defining how synchronization is maintained and framing errors are mitigated.

children.title: Section 4.2.2.3.1 Framing Tokens in Non-Flit Mode
children.description: This section defines PCIe physical layer framing protocols, specifically governing receiver alignment adjustments for SKP Ordered Sets, EIEOS-validated TS processing in Recovery, and block alignment requirements for Loopback Lead and Follower states. It details technical operational constraints regarding when and how receivers synchronize bit streams and transition between alignment phases during link training and loopback testing.

children.title: Section 4.2.2.3.2 Transmitter Framing Requirements in Non-Flit Mode
children.description: This section defines the physical layer transmitter framing protocols for non-flit mode data transmission, covering the structural requirements for STP tokens, TLP encapsulation, and nullification procedures via EDB tokens. It specifically addresses how the PCIe link must rhythmically sequence TLP Data Widths, LCRC, and control symbols to maintain signal integrity and packet delineation.

children.title: Section 4.2.2.3.3 Receiver Framing Requirements in Non-Flit Mode
children.description: This section specifies receiver framing requirements for PCIe Non-Flit mode, detailing IDL Token placement, lane alignment for TLPs, DLLPs, and framing tokens (STP, SDP, EDB, EDS) on xN links. It resolves technical questions regarding valid symbol positioning, lane-width-specific constraints for multi-token transmission, and the rules for block transition syntax in the received data stream.

children.title: Section 4.2.2.3.4 Receiver Framing Requirements in Flit Mode
children.description: This section defines PCIe receiver framing error conditions in Flit Mode, covering protocol requirements for IDL tokens, Ordered Set blocks, and Sync Header validation. It specifies exactly which sequences—such as invalid Ordered Set placements or undefined Block types—constitute a Framing Error and mandates error reporting protocols for multi-lane links.

children.title: Section 4.2.2.3.5 Recovery from Framing Errors in Non-Flit Mode and Flit Mode
children.description: This section specifies the protocol-mandated recovery sequence for PCIe framing errors in Non-Flit and Flit modes, detailing required LTSSM transitions and error reporting obligations. It answers how a receiver must handle data stream interruptions and which specific LTSSM states necessitate transition to Recovery upon detection of a framing-related fault.

children.title: Section 4.2.2.4 Scrambling in Non-Flit Mode and Flit Mode
children.description: This section details requirements for handling Framing Errors in PCI Express non-flit and flit modes, covering LTSSM state transition procedures, receiver error reporting, and multi-lane LFSR scrambling implementations. It addresses how a receiver must respond to streaming data corruption and provides the specific state-machine logic required to recover from link-layer framing failures.

children.title: Section 4.2.2.5 Precoding
children.description: This section defines the mandatory requirements and signaling procedures for enabling transmitter precoding at data rates of 32.0 GT/s and higher in PCIe. It details configuration rules for Ports, synchronization with LTSSM states, and the specific use of the Transmitter Precode Request bit within EQ TS2 Ordered Sets to standardize signal integrity across Flit and Non-Flit Modes.

children.title: Section 4.2.2.5.1 Precoding at 32.0 GT/s Data Rate
children.description: This section details the operational requirements and logic implementations for transmitter and receiver precoding at 32.0 GT/s data rates, including status register management and state machine resets. It provides technical specifications for handling scrambled bits, block boundary “previous bit” initialization, and the feed-forward architecture required for consistent signal decoding.

children.title: Section 4.2.2.6 Loopback with 128b/130b Code in Non-Flit Mode and Flit Mode
children.description: This section defines 128b/130b encoding requirements for PCIe Loopback mode, specifically addressing Sync Header transmission, SKP Ordered Set adjustments for clock compensation, and packet structure rules for Lead and Follower components. It clarifies operational constraints regarding Ordered Set transitions, EIEOS usage, and the technical protocols for bit-level retransmission between loopback participants.

children.title: Section 4.2.3 Flit Mode Operation
children.description: This section details PCIe Flit Mode operations for data rates of 64.0 GT/s and higher, covering 1b/1b encoding, ordered set management, and flit layout byte structures. It answers how high-speed data streams are formatted, encoded, and integrated with ordered set signaling within the Flit Mode architecture.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.1 1b/1b Encoding for 64.0 GT/s and higher Data Rates
children.description: This section defines 128b/130b encoding protocols for Loopback Leads and Followers at 64.0 GT/s data rates, focusing on Sync Header handling, Ordered Set transmission rules, and SKP Ordered Set clock compensation requirements. It specifies operational constraints for retransmitting bits and formatting valid SKP sequences to ensure link synchronicity during loopback testing.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.1.1 PAM4 Signaling
children.description: This section describes the 64.0 GT/s and higher PCIe transmit/receive data path, detailing the signal processing pipeline including scrambling, Gray coding, PAM4 voltage conversion, and flit-level FEC/CRC. It addresses how data is serialized, encoded across multiple lanes, and subsequently decoded and error-corrected to ensure high-speed link integrity.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.1.2 1b/1b Scrambling
children.description: This section details PCIe PAM4 signaling, including Gray coding, 2-bit scrambling, and DC-balance values used to mitigate bit error rate (BER) impacts. It explains how low-overhead Forward Error Correction (FEC), Flit-level CRC, and link-level retry mechanisms ensure data integrity despite the inherent noise susceptibility of 4-level pulse amplitude modulation.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.1.3 Gray Coding at 64.0 GT/s and Higher Data Rates
children.description: This section details Gray coding, scrambling, and NRZ-mapping protocols for 64.0 GT/s and higher PCIe data rates, focusing on TS0/TS1/TS2 Ordered Set processing and LFSR advancement rules. It addresses how receivers handle scrambled versus unscrambled symbols, the sequence of PAM4 signal translation, and the application of precoding to ensure signal integrity across the data stream.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.1.4 Precoding at 64.0 GT/s and Higher Data Rates
children.description: This section details the precoding mechanisms for 64.0 GT/s and higher data rates, specifically defining PAM4 voltage error propagation, bit-alignment constraints, and the transmit/receive logic. It addresses how precoding mitigates signal errors, providing mathematical truth tables for differential decoding and the handling of scrambled bits across UI boundaries.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.1.5 Ordered Set Blocks at 64.0 GT/s and Higher Data Rates
children.description: This section details the physical layer framing requirements for PCIe 64.0 GT/s and higher, focusing on the byte lengths, redundancy, and detection criteria for Ordered Sets like TS, EIOS, EIEOS, I-EIEOS, and SKP. It defines specific error-resilient matching rules and boundary behavior for receivers processing PAM4-encoded signaling outside of active Data Streams.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.1.6 Alignment at Block/ Flit Level for 1b/1b Encoding
children.description: This section details the 1b/1b encoding link alignment process, covering technical procedures for Block and Flit-level synchronization via EIEOS and Control SKP Ordered Sets. It explains state transitions—Unaligned, Aligned, and Locked—and provides the operational criteria for receivers to establish bit stream boundaries following Electrical Idle or state changes.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.2 Processing of Ordered Sets During Flit Mode Data Stream
children.description: This section specifies requirements for PCIe Flit Mode synchronization, detailing how Receivers manage Block and Flit alignment during Aligned, Locked, and Loopback states. It outlines specific handling protocols for Control SKP Ordered Sets, EIEOS patterns, and TS Ordered Sets to ensure data integrity during LTSSM transitions and Loopback operations.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.3 Data Stream in Flit Mode
children.description: This section defines the 256-byte structural composition of a PCIe Flit, detailing the byte-level allocation for TLPs, Data Link Layer Payloads, CRC, and ECC. It specifies how these flits are interleaved across physical lanes at 64.0 GT/s and higher, contextualizing the processing of Data Stream Ordered Sets within the Flit Mode architecture.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4 Bytes in Flit Layout
children.description: This section details the byte-level organization of PCIe Flit layouts, covering TLP and DLP encapsulation, sequence number handshake protocols, and replay mechanisms. It provides technical specifications for managing reliable data packet exchange, including ACK/NAK processing rules, flit scheduling, and the payloads for various NOP flit types.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.1 TLP Bytes in Flit
children.description: This section details TLP byte mapping within Flit Mode, covering packet framing, NOP TLP scheduling requirements, and architectural differences between Flit and non-Flit mode processing. It addresses how the Transaction Layer manages data alignment and credit-independent idle padding when specific TLP payload boundaries are not met.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.2 DLP Bytes in Flit
