children.description: This section specifies the 6-byte Data Link Layer Packet (DLP) allocation within 128b/130b flits, detailing the encoding of Ack/Nak, optimized credit release (UpdateFC), and CRC/ECC mechanisms. It establishes how DLP payloads are structured to reuse legacy DLLP formats while defining the functional distinctions between IDLE, TLP, and DLP flit types.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.2.1 Flit Sequence Number and Retry Mechanism
children.description: This section defines the flow control and error recovery protocols for data transmission, specifically detailing Flit Sequence Numbers, Nak/Ack response mechanisms, and TX/RX Retry Buffer management. It establishes technical requirements for Standard/Selective Replay procedures and the Nak Ignore Window to ensure reliable delivery during link-level communication.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.2.1.1 IDLE Flit Handshake Phase
children.description: This section details protocol-level state transitions and `IMPLICIT_RX_FLIT_SEQ_NUM` maintenance during the IDLE Flit Handshake phase. It defines specific logical conditions involving `NON_IDLE_EXPLICIT_SEQ_NUM_FLIT_RCVD`, `NAK_WITHDRAWAL_ALLOWED`, and `Prior Flit was Payload` to determine when to increment, decrement, or reset sequence numbers upon receiving valid or invalid flits.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.2.1.2 Sequence Number Handshake Phase
children.description: This section defines the Sequence Number Handshake Phase for PCIe-based flit protocols, detailing transition requirements, packet transmission rules, and device recovery triggers. It specifies the logic for alternating Explicit Sequence Number, Ack, and Nak flits to establish valid link communication and determine when the Port transitions to the Normal Flit Exchange Phase.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.2.1.3 Normal Flit Exchange Phase
children.description: This section defines the "Normal Flit Exchange Phase" protocol, detailing Flit sequence numbering, replay buffer management, and Nak scheduling requirements. It specifies rules for Explicit Sequence Number Flits, including NOP, replayed, and new payload handling, to ensure reliable flow control and data transmission integrity.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.2.1.4 Received Ack and Nak Processing
children.description: This section defines the logic for processing received Ack and Nak Flits, including rules for validating Flit Sequence Numbers, managing the TX Retry Buffer, and handling Data Link Protocol Errors. It provides technical requirements for state variable updates (ACKD_FLIT_SEQ_NUM, CONSECUTIVE_TX_NAK_FLITS) and instructions for identifying invalid sequence numbers versus valid acknowledgment sequences.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.2.1.5 Ack, Nak, and Discard Rules
children.description: This section delineates logical procedures for handling Standard and Selective NAKs, including conditions for updating `NAK_IGNORE_FLIT_SEQ_NUM` and triggering replay schedules. It provides standardized pseudo-code for routing logic regarding invalid flit detection, TLP byte discarding, and NAK withdrawal protocols within the receiver state machine.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.2.1.6 Flit Replay Scheduling
children.description: This section defines the logic for Flit Replay Scheduling, detailing state transitions involving IDLE Flit handshakes, NAK scheduling, and sequence number management. It specifies the conditions for discarding flits, managing RX Retry Buffers, and error handling during implicit and explicit sequence numbering workflows.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.2.1.7 Flit Replay Transmit Rules
children.description: This section specifies the protocol logic for triggering flit retransmissions, detailing the transition conditions between Standard and Selective Nak handling, the management of TX Retry Buffers, and the state updates for REPLAY_SCHEDULED. It answers how a transmitting port orchestrates error recovery when receiving valid Nak flits and how it prioritizes replay types during concurrent or conflicting signaling events.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.3.2 NOP.Debug Flit
children.description: This section details the identification, sequencing, and error-handling requirements for NOP.Debug Flits, specifically addressing NOP Stream ID uniqueness, NOP Flit Counter progression, and rollover behaviors. It defines how to manage stream state through NOP.Empty Flits, resets, and error signaling (FFFh) during link-to-link packet forwarding.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.3.2.1 PCI-SIG Defined Debug Chunk Opcode Values
children.description: This section details the structure and composition of `NOP.Debug` flit payloads, specifically defining the layout of Debug Chunk Headers, Vendor IDs, and Opcode values. It addresses how to format multiple sequential Debug Chunks within a single flit and specifies the mandated handling of unused Data Word (DW) slots.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.3.2.2 Empty Debug Chunk
children.description: This section defines the "Empty Debug Chunk" and outlines specific opcodes for various debug-related telemetry, including Flow Control information, Flit Mode retry flags, and buffer occupancy. It specifies the structural requirements for empty padding chunks, dictates receiver handling of Reserved encodings, and addresses how to maintain alignment within Flit-based debug data transmission.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.3.2.3 Start Capture Trigger Debug Chunk
children.description: This section details the implementation of the `Start Capture Trigger` within the Debug Chunk architecture, covering signal synchronization, event logging, and state machine transitions. It provides technical specifications for hardware-level capture initiation, answering how specific debug triggers are initialized and validated for real-time diagnostic performance.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.3.2.4 Stop Capture Trigger Debug Chunk
children.description: This section defines the Stop Capture Trigger Debug Chunk, detailing its use in signaling debug tools to terminate link traffic capture. It specifies requirements for payload implementation, vendor ID signaling, and the prohibition of continuation bits.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.3.2.5 FC Information Tracked by Transmitter Debug Chunk
children.description: This section defines the "FC Information Tracked by Transmitter Debug Chunk" format within PCIe debugging protocols, detailing the structure of Flow Control (FC) quantity fields, including Virtual Channel (VC) identifiers and Header/Data credit values. It addresses how telemetry data is encapsulated and communicated by a transmitter to monitor credit-based flow control gating mechanisms.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.3.2.6 FC Information Tracked by Receiver Debug Chunk
children.description: This section specifies the format and structure of the **FC Information Tracked by Receiver Debug Chunk** used for **TLP Receiver flow control accounting**. It defines the **Debug Chunk Payload** organization, detailing how **VC**, **Hdr_FC**, and **Data_FC** fields map credit allocations to specific **FC Quantity encodings** within the data stream.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.3.2.7 Flit Mode Transmitter Retry Flags and Counters Debug Chunk
children.description: This section defines the "Flit Mode Transmitter Retry Flags and Counters Debug Chunk," documenting the specific bit-level layout and field definitions for managing Flit Sequence Numbers and replay tracking. It answers technical questions regarding the structural specifications, required payload length, and field-specific registers (such as `TX_REPLAY_FLIT_SEQ_NUM` and `REPLAY_IN_PROGRESS`) necessary for monitoring transmitter retry state machine performance in PCIe Flit Mode.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.3.2.8 Flit Mode Receiver Retry Flags and Counters Debug Chunk
children.description: This section details the structure and bit-level mapping of the Flit Mode Receiver Retry Flags and Counters Debug Chunk, including fields for sequence number tracking, NAK scheduling, and replay timeouts. It provides the technical specifications required to interpret receiver-side retry state, sequence number synchronization, and flow control metadata within a Flit Mode PCIe environment.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.3.2.9 Buffer Occupancy Debug Chunk
children.description: This section defines the binary structure and diagnostic signaling protocols for the Buffer Occupancy Debug Chunk. It details field specifications, data encoding, and memory mapping telemetry required to troubleshoot latency bottlenecks and overflow events within system buffer queues.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.3.2.10 Link Debug Request Debug Chunk
children.description: This section defines the Link Debug Request Debug Chunk and the Buffer Occupancy Debug Chunk, detailing their respective DW layouts, Buffer ID encodings, and NOP.Debug Flit request mechanisms. It specifies how to interpret buffer occupancy data and structure diagnostic requests for link-layer debugging within the reported protocol.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.4 CRC Bytes in Flit
children.description: This section defines the structural formatting, vendor-specific payload fields, and cyclic redundancy check (CRC) generation parameters—based on the primitive polynomial $x^8+x^5+x^3+x+1$—used for validating Link Debug Request and NOP.Vendor flits. It clarifies technical requirements for proprietary data transmission, header field mapping, and the error-detection mechanisms applied during flit transmission and reception.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.5 ECC Bytes in Flit
children.description: This section outlines the CRC generation and error-checking process for Flits, utilizing a 1936-bit data input and a standardized 1936x64 generator matrix. It details the bit-ordering mechanism and 3-way interleaved GF(2$^8$) FEC scheme to address how data integrity is verified, how mismatches trigger replay requests, and how burst error protection is guaranteed.

children.title: Section 4.2.3.4.6 Ordered Set insertion in Data Stream in Flit Mode
children.description: This section defines protocol requirements for SKP and SDS Ordered Set insertion within flit-based data streams for PCIe links operating at or above 8.0 GT/s. It addresses how to maintain synchronization and link rhythm during flit-boundary transmissions, specifically detailing Ordered Set placement rules for L0p state transitions, lane up-sizing, and power-management events.

children.title: Section 4.2.4 Link Equalization Procedure for 8.0 GT/s and Higher Data Rates
children.description: This section details the PCIe Link Equalization procedure for data rates of 8.0 GT/s and higher, focusing on transmitter/receiver adjustments, LTSSM scope, and mandatory Ordered Set insertion intervals for various link widths and clocking modes (Common Clock/SRNS/SRIS). The text defines requirements for signal quality optimization, equalization persistence across data rates, and operational parameters for maintaining reliable high-speed link performance.

children.title: Section 4.2.4.1 Rules for Transmitter Coefficients
children.description: This section specifies the constraints for transmitter FIR filter coefficients (C-2, C-1, C0, C+1) and Full Swing (FS) ranges required for PCIe link equalization across varying data rates. It defines parameter absolute values, usage rules for pre/post-cursors, and operational modes for reduced versus full swing signaling.

children.title: Section 4.2.4.2 Encoding of Presets
children.description: This section specifies the constraints and encoding requirements for transmitter equalization coefficients (C-1, C0, C+1, C+1) and LF (Low Frequency) voltage advertising during PCIe Link Training. It clarifies protocol compliance rules for preset adaptation, defining valid coefficient ranges and distinct encoding classes for presets (P0–P10 vs. Q0–Q10) across various data rates.

children.title: Section 4.2.5 Link Initialization and Training
children.description: Section 4.2.5 details the PCIe Link Training and Status State Machine (LTSSM) process, covering link width, data rate, lane polarity, and reversal configuration. It addresses how the physical layer initializes links, recovers from errors, manages low-power state transitions, and resolves quantization errors for 64.0 GT/s+ receiver presets.

children.title: Section 4.2.5.1 Training Sequences
children.description: This section details the physical layer initialization process, covering critical technical parameters such as link data rate, bit/symbol lock, lane polarity, and de-skew. It defines the structure and transmission rules for Training Sequences (TS0–TS2) across various encoding schemes (8b/10b, 128b/130b, 1b/1b), providing specific protocols for link width negotiation and Ordered Set implementation.

children.title: Section 4.2.5.2 Alternate Protocol Negotiation
children.description: This section defines the mechanisms for alternate protocol negotiation within PCIe PHY link training, specifically covering **Modified TS1/TS2 Ordered Sets**, **8b/10b vs. 128b/130b/1b/1b encoding rules**, and parity constraints for **Half Scrambling**. It addresses how non-PCIe protocols integrate with physical layer configuration during the **Configuration.Lanenum** and **Configuration.Complete** substates while maintaining link-state compliance.

children.title: Section 4.2.5.3 Electrical Idle Sequences (EIOS and EIEOS)
children.description: This section defines the protocol negotiation requirements for Upstream and Downstream PCIe Ports using Modified TS Ordered Sets for Alternate Protocol selection. It clarifies mandatory response behaviors, fallback mechanisms, and the state-transition process from Link training to alternate protocol control in L0.

children.title: Section 4.2.5.4 Inferring Electrical Idle
children.description: This section details the mechanical requirements for transmitting EIEOS during LTSSM transitions between Recovery, Configuration, and Idle states at data rates exceeding 2.5 GT/s. It defines Electrical Idle detection reliability, EIEOS interval count resets, and specific regulatory conditions for skipping hardware-based idle circuitry during state exits.

children.title: Section 4.2.5.6 Fast Training Sequence (FTS)
children.description: This section details PCI Express Lane polarity inversion mechanisms using TS1/TS2 Ordered Set symbol detection and specifies the Fast Training Sequence (FTS) protocol for achieving bit and symbol lock when exiting L0s. It addresses how receivers correct D+/D- swaps and defines the structural requirements for FTS patterns needed to facilitate recovery from Electrical Idle at 2.5 GT/s and 5.0 GT/s.

children.title: Section 4.2.5.7 Start of Data Stream Ordered Set (SDS Ordered Set)
children.description: This section details the Start of Data Stream (SDS) Ordered Set and N_FTS requirements for 128b/130b and 1b/1b encoding transitions. It addresses how data rates, Extended Synch settings, and LTSSM states govern bit lock, symbol lock, and link recovery procedures.

children.title: Section 4.2.5.8 Link Error Recovery
children.description: This section defines PCIe Link Error Recovery mechanisms, detailing SDS Ordered Set symbol values for 32.0 GT/s and higher data rates alongside error-handling requirements for 8b/10b and 128b/130b encoding schemes. It clarifies which physical layer faults—such as framing errors, loss of lock, or elasticity buffer issues—must trigger mandatory Receiver Errors versus those where reporting is optional across specific LTSSM states.

children.title: Section 4.2.5.9 Reset
children.description: This section defines hardware initialization procedures, specifically detailing Fundamental Reset and Hot Reset mechanisms for system recovery. It addresses technical requirements for state transitions, power-on sequences, and signal behavior during device reboot processes.

children.title: Section 4.2.5.9.2 Hot Reset
children.description: This section defines protocol and fundamental reset requirements, detailing Receiver/Transmitter electrical states, LTSSM initialization, and 2.5 GT/s Link training sequences. It addresses how PCIe devices verify symbol lock, manage interface impedance during reset, and navigate the transition from initial 2.5 GT/s negotiation to operational data rates.

children.title: Section 4.2.5.11 Link Width and Lane Sequence Negotiation
children.description: This section details PCIe Link Width and Lane Sequence negotiation, covering 8b/10b error handling, physical layer reset states (Fundamental and Hot Reset), and LTSSM-managed initialization. It defines mandatory Link configurations (x1 to x16), signal skew tolerances, and the 2.5 GT/s training requirement for initial Link establishment.

children.title: Section 4.2.5.11.1 Required and Optional Port Behavior
children.description: This section defines mandatory and elective link training behaviors for xN ports, specifically addressing requirements for width negotiation (xN and x1), optional link width flexibility, port bifurcation capabilities, and lane reversal implementation. It establishes the design constraints for component interconnection to ensure interoperability and defines the operational boundaries for multi-lane link configuration.

children.title: Section 4.2.5.12 Lane-to-Lane De-skew
children.description: This section outlines the requirements for PCI Express Link width negotiation, including mandatory xN and x1 configurations and optional features like Link bifurcation and Lane reversal. It defines the physical routing constraints and component interoperability standards necessary to ensure successful Link initialization between Ports.

children.title: Section 4.2.5.13 Lane vs. Link Training
children.description: This section details technical requirements for PCI Express Lane-to-Lane de-skew and Link initialization, covering Ordered Set triggers (FTS, SKP, SDS) across various data rates (2.5 GT/s to 16.0 GT/s+). It defines the criteria for establishing associated Lanes and specifies the mandatory de-skew mechanisms enforced during Configuration, Recovery, and L0s states within the LTSSM.

children.title: Section 4.2.6 Link Training and Status State Machine (LTSSM) Descriptions
children.description: This section details the PCIe Link Training and Status State Machine (LTSSM), covering state transitions, physical layer initialization, power management (L0-L2), and link maintenance mechanisms. It explains how a PCIe link discovers devices, configures lane width and data rates, manages low-power states, and handles recovery, loopback, or reset scenarios.

children.title: Section 4.2.6.2 Polling Overview
children.description: Section 4.2.6.2 defines the **Polling** state within the **LTSSM**, detailing requirements for multi-link port configuration, terminal detection, and link training parameters. It addresses operational questions regarding how a **Port** achieves **bit lock**, **Symbol lock**, and **Lane polarity** configuration, while outlining the use of **Polling.Compliance** for transmitter and interconnect testing.

children.title: Section 4.2.6.7 L0p Overview
children.description: This section provides a technical overview of PCIe Link training and power management states, specifically covering Configuration, Recovery, L0, and L0s. It addresses how links negotiate link width, lane de-skew, and data rates, while detailing the constraints and operational requirements for entering the L0s power-saving state in Flit Mode or Retimer environments.

children.title: Section 4.2.6.7.1 Link Management DLLP
children.description: This section defines the Link Management Data Link Layer Packet (DLLP) structure, specifically focusing on L0p transmitter and receiver state transitions. It details technical requirements for L0p request behaviors, width adjustment protocols, and software control configurations for managing autonomous link width changes.

children.title: Section 4.2.6.9 L2 Overview
children.description: This section details PCIe L2 power-state technical requirements, specifically focusing on auxiliary power usage, low-impedance receiver termination, and Beacon signal generation for wakeup. It clarifies power-saving constraints, identifying when transmitter or receiver circuitry can be disabled and defining the port-specific responsibilities for maintaining wakeup capabilities via Beacon signaling.

children.title: Section 4.2.6.11 Loopback Overview
children.description: This section defines the PCIe Loopback state for test and fault isolation, detailing the roles of the Loopback Lead and Follower, entry criteria using TS1/TS2 Ordered Sets, and encoding requirements for 8b/10b, 128b/130b, and 1b/1b schemes. It addresses how devices initiate link-level or per-lane loopback testing and specifies the Follower's responsibility to transparently retransmit the received bit stream.

children.title: Section 4.2.6.12 Hot Reset Overview
children.description: This section defines the Hot Reset mechanism for resetting downstream PCIe devices via in-band signaling and outlines how specific Link status bits—including LinkUp, Link Speed, and Receiver Error—are managed across LTSSM states. It addresses how software monitors device status and identifies the mandatory versus optional requirements for reporting errors during link state transitions.

children.title: Section 4.2.7 Link Training and Status State Rules
children.description: This section defines LTSSM state-specific requirements for managing PCIe Link status bits, including LinkUp, Link Speed, Link Width, Link Training, Receiver Error, and In-Band Presence Detect. It establishes formal rules for how software and hardware monitor these status indications during various training and power-management states (Detect, Polling, Configuration, Recovery, L0, and L0s).

children.title: Section 4.2.7.1 Detect
children.description: This section categorizes state-based detection methodologies between [Section 4.2.7.1.1 Detect.Quiet] (passive monitoring, signature-based analysis, and anomaly detection) and [Section 4.2.7.1.2 Detect.Active] (probing, traffic injection, and real-time validation). It addresses how security systems classify operational modes to identify threats while balancing stealth requirements against diagnostic granularity.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.1.1 Detect.Quiet
children.description: This section defines the **Detect.Quiet** substate within the **PCIe LTSSM**, detailing electrical and operational requirements such as **Electrical Idle**, **2.5 GT/s frequency selection**, and **$Z_{RX-DC}$ impedance compliance**. It establishes the mandatory initialization conditions and register resets required to transition a link into the detection process.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.1.2 Detect.Active
children.description: This section defines the PCIe LTSSM **Detect.Active** substate, specifying the logic for **Receiver Detection** sequences across un-configured lanes. It details transition criteria between **Polling**, **Detect.Quiet**, and **Electrical Idle** states based on detection consistency to ensure successful **Link Training**.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.2 Polling
children.description: Section 4.2.7.2 details the Link Training and Status State Machine (LTSSM) **Polling** transition logic, focusing on **Receiver Detection** sequences and **Electrical Idle** behavior for unconfigured lanes. This documentation explains how the transmitter manages link initialization, re-association, and state transitions to **Detect.Quiet** when lane detection discrepancies occur across multiple attempts.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.2.1 Polling.Active
children.description: This section specifies the **Polling.Active** state requirements for PCIe link training, detailing mandatory **TS1 Ordered Set** transmission, **Data Rate Identifier** advertisements, and **Electrical Idle** exit procedures. It defines the transition conditions for moving to **Polling.Compliance** or **Polling.Configuration**, addressing how a Transmitter establishes initial signaling parameters and transitions based on register settings and receiver feedback.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.2.2 Polling.Compliance
children.description: This section defines the entry criteria for the **LTSSM Polling.Compliance** substate, addressing how PCIe devices exit **Electrical Idle**, detect **Compliance_Receive_Request**, and identify **TS1 Ordered Sets**. It explains technical requirements for **Transmit Margin**, **Link Control 2 register** adjustments, and the algorithmic determination of **data rates** and **de-emphasis levels** for generating **Compliance Patterns**.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.2.3 Polling.Configuration
children.description: This section specifies the signal integrity test patterns—High Swing/Low Swing Toggle Patterns and Compliance Patterns—assigned to specific PCIe lanes based on discrete configuration settings. It serves as a technical reference for hardware validation, defining which test stimulus corresponds to each numerical setting to ensure standardized transmitter testing.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.2.4 Polling.Speed
children.description: This section details the **Polling.Speed** substate of the **PCI Express Physical Layer**, specifically defining transition conditions to **Configuration** or **Detect** states. It clarifies that PCIe links initially train at 2.5 GT/s, outlining the **TS2 Ordered Set** requirements and the logic for subsequent speed negotiations via **Recovery**.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.3 Configuration
children.description: This section details the PCIe Configuration LTSSM states, focusing on link width negotiation, lane reversal, and physical layer handshaking protocols. It defines the behavioral transitions and operational requirements for downstream and upstream lanes during the establishment of link width and lane numbering.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.3.1.1 Downstream Lanes
children.description: This section specifies the transition logic for Downstream Lanes within the PCI Express Polling and Configuration substates. It details how higher-layer commands and specific ordered set patterns trigger transitions to the Disabled or Loopback states for downstream and crosslink-capable ports.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.3.1.2 Upstream Lanes
children.description: This section defines the initialization protocols for **Upstream Lanes** during the PCIe training process, specifically addressing **Electrical Idle** settling times, **Link and Lane number** assignment via **TS1 Ordered Sets**, and the transition requirements into **Configuration.Linkwidth.Accept**. It clarifies how components distinguish between single-link and multi-link topologies and dictates the signaling sequence necessary for crosslink-capable or standard downstream port detection.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.3.2 Configuration.Linkwidth.Accept
children.description: This section defines the **Configuration.Linkwidth.Accept** state within the **PCIe Link Training and Status State Machine (LTSSM)**, establishing the criteria for a component to validate and transition to a proposed link width. It specifies protocol requirements for **Lane-to-Lane deskew**, **Symbol lock**, and **receiver detection handshake** to confirm successful link width negotiation during initialization or recovery.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.3.2.1 Downstream Lanes
children.description: This section defines the PCIe LTSSM `Configuration.DownstreamLanes` substate, specifying procedures for link width upconfiguration, TS1 Ordered Set exchange, and lane activation requirements. It addresses how the Transmitter handles receiver detection, padding (PAD) values, link configuration timing, and synchronization delays to ensure reliable link width establishment.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.3.2.2 Upstream Lanes
children.description: This section defines the transition criteria for Upstream Lanes into the Configuration.Lanenum.Wait state, specifying requirements for matching or reversing Lane numbers via TS1 Ordered Sets. It addresses technical protocols for handling 64.0 GT/s skew delays, Modified TS1 Ordered Set usage, and the sequential assignment constraints for configuring valid lane widths.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.3.3 Configuration.Lanenum.Accept
children.description: This section details PCIe LTSSM configuration procedures during the `Configuration.Lanenum.Accept` sub-state, specifically addressing the transmission of Modified TS1 Ordered Sets, multi-link bifurcation logic, lane numbering, and optional lane reversal. It defines how ports negotiate link numbers and handle asymmetric lane reception scenarios to ensure proper link establishment.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.3.3.1 Downstream Lanes
children.description: This section specifies the transition criteria for the Configuration.Complete state, including Modified TS1 Ordered Set identification and Alternate Protocol Negotiation timing delays. It addresses how Downstream Ports validate lane matching, initialize SRIS_Mode_Enabled status, and store EFM_CNTL1 parameters for high-speed Flit Mode operations.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.3.3.2 Upstream Lanes
children.description: This section defines protocols for identifying PCIe Optical Retimer presence, detecting reversed lane configurations, and handling TS1 Ordered Set errors during link training. It specifies timing requirements for evaluating lane alignment and establishes criteria for successfully forming a configured link across valid lane subsets using 8b/10b, 128b/130b, or 1b/1b encoding.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.3.5 Configuration.Complete
children.description: This section details PCIe link state machine requirements during **Configuration.Complete**, specifically mandate the use of **Modified TS1 Ordered Sets** when the `use_modified_TS1_TS2_Ordered_Set` variable is enabled. It specifies transition triggers for **Configuration.Lanenum.Accept**, including lane numbering synchronization, receiver detection, and the permitted 1 ms delay to mitigate link width configuration errors from inter-lane skew.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.3.5.1 Downstream Lanes
children.description: This section defines protocol-specific behavior for the Downstream Lanes substate, governing conditions for advertising Upconfigure and L0p Capability based on Flit Mode status and TS_Enhanced_Flit_Encode configurations. It details requirements for Modified TS2 Ordered Set transmission, link parameter synchronization, and state-transition prerequisites such as N_FTS acquisition and 8b/10b de-skewing.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.3.5.2 Upstream Lanes
children.description: This section details PCIe LTSSM transitions for upstream lanes, specifying timeout behavior for electrical idle exits at various data rates (2.5–8.0+ GT/s). It defines procedures for state transitions, `upconfigure_capable` variable updates, and TS2 Ordered Set transmission protocols during Link configuration or recovery.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.3.6 Configuration.Idle
children.description: This section details PCIe LTSSM `Configuration.Idle` state behaviors, specifically focusing on Receiver termination requirements for Lane upconfiguration, Electrical Idle transitions, and state machine timeouts at varying data rates. It clarifies the conditions for transitioning to `Detect` versus maintaining `Configuration.Idle` and defining the association requirements for non-configured Lanes.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4 Recovery
children.description: This section details the PCIe dynamic link training and status state machine (LTSSM) Recovery substate, focusing on Receiver Lock, Transmitter Equalization phases for downstream and upstream lanes, and speed negotiation. It addresses technical queries regarding signal integrity, bit-error rate optimization, and the specific handshake sequences required to transition between link speeds and configurations.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4.1 Recovery.RcvrLock
children.description: This section defines **Recovery.RcvrLock** state requirements for PCIe links operating at or above 8.0 GT/s, focusing on **Block Alignment** prerequisites, **TS ordered set** reception, and **Transmitter preset** configuration protocols. It clarifies how ports must synchronize equalization settings and maintain signal integrity when transitioning into the Recovery substate from L0, L1, L0s, or Recovery.Speed.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4.2 Recovery.Equalization
children.description: This section defines the **Recovery.Equalization** state transitions and signaling requirements for PCIe links entering from **Recovery.RcvrLock** or **Loopback.Entry**. It details technical constraints concerning **TS0/TS1 Ordered Set** transmission, **Block Alignment**, and **Transmitter preset** configurations for lanes under test versus background noise lanes.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4.2.1 Downstream Lanes
children.description: This section dictates the mandatory architectural requirements for transitioning between TS0 and TS1 ordered sets during PCIe Link Equalization phases for data rates up to 64.0 GT/s and higher. It provides technical specifications for register configuration, flag initialization, and the signal processing logic required for successful negotiation between NRZ and PAM4 signaling domains.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4.2.1.1 Phase 1 of Transmitter Equalization
children.description: This section defines the reset and termination protocols for PCIe Transmitter Equalization across 32.0, 64.0, and 128.0 GT/s data rates. It clarifies the state of Status Register bits, the `equalization_done` variables, and the mandatory TS1 Ordered Set signaling required for Loopback Follower data rate advertisements during link training.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4.2.1.2 Phase 2 of Transmitter Equalization
children.description: This section defines the PCIe Link Equalization Phase 2 transition criteria, including specific register bit updates for 32.0, 64.0, and 128.0 GT/s data rates and the mandatory transmission of 24 TS0 Ordered Sets. It specifies timeout conditions and next-state transitions—Recovery.RcvrLock, Loopback.Entry, or Recovery.Speed—following completion of the equalization process.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4.2.1.3 Phase 3 of Transmitter Equalization
children.description: This section details the transitioning requirements from Phase 2 to Phase 3 of PCIe Transmitter Equalization, specifically defining coefficient validation, status register updates, and condition-based state transitions. It addresses how the transmitter handles illegal coefficient requests, updates success bits across various data rates (8.0 GT/s to 128.0 GT/s), and dictates when to advance to the next equalization state.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4.2.2 Upstream Lanes
children.description: This section details the entry-state configuration for PCIe Upstream Lanes, specifically documenting the reset of equalization status bits and the initialization of phase variables across data rates ranging from 8.0 GT/s to 128.0 GT/s. It addresses how Link Status and Equalization Complete registers are initialized to define proper state transitions during speed negotiation.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4.2.2.1 Phase 0 of Transmitter Equalization
children.description: This section outlines Phase 0 of PCIe Transmitter Equalization, detailing how transmitter preset values and coefficient fields are determined for TS0/TS1 Ordered Sets when entering `Recovery.Equalization` from `Loopback.Entry`. It specifies protocol behavior for 8.0 GT/s and 16.0 GT/s data rates, clarifying how devices must select and propagate equalization presets based on previous handshaking states.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4.2.2.2 Phase 1 of Transmitter Equalization
children.description: This section details the Phase 1 Transmitter Equalization procedure for PCIe data rates ranging from 8.0 GT/s to 128.0 GT/s. It defines mandatory TS0/TS1 Ordered Set configurations, status register updates, and transition conditions for entering Phase 2 or transitioning to the Loopback.Entry state under specific timeout and parameter constraints.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4.2.2.3 Phase 2 of Transmitter Equalization
children.description: This section details the Phase 2 PCIe transmitter equalization procedure, covering TS Ordered Set exchange, preset/coefficient request protocols, and signal evaluation requirements. It specifies the timing constraints (1 μs request duration, 500 ns plus roundtrip settling delay) and verification steps, such as achieving Block Alignment, necessary for optimizing link performance on a per-lane basis.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4.2.2.4 Phase 3 of Transmitter Equalization
children.description: This section defines the transition criteria for PCIe Transmitter Equalization Phase 3, specifying timeout durations and next-state logic for either `Loopback.Entry` or `Recovery.Speed`. It explains how system registers are updated and internal status bits are set based on operational data rates (ranging from 8.0 GT/s to 128.0 GT/s) upon equalization completion or timeout expiration.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4.3 Recovery.Speed
children.description: This section defines the transition logic for the **Recovery.Speed** state during PCIe equalization, specifying state-machine transitions to **Loopback.Entry** and **Recovery.RcvrLock** based on data rate-dependent equalization timeouts and **TS1 Ordered Set** reception. It answers how link timing parameters and status register updates—such as **Equalization Complete** and **Phase 3 Successful** bits—are managed across data rates ranging from 8.0 GT/s to 64.0 GT/s.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4.4 Recovery.RcvrCfg
children.description: This section defines the transition criteria for the `Recovery.RcvrCfg` state, including specific timeout conditions for 64.0 GT/s Flit Mode and rules for updating `Link Status Register` fields upon speed negotiations. It details how the transmitter handles `TS2 Ordered Sets` for Link width and rate changes, specifying the conditions for setting `Link Autonomous Bandwidth Status` versus `Link Bandwidth Management Status` bits.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.4.5 Recovery.Idle
children.description: This section defines state transition logic within PCI Express **Recovery.Idle**, specifically detailing timeout behaviors at varying data rates (2.5 GT/s to 8.0+ GT/s) and entry conditions for **Disabled** or **Hot Reset** states. It answers how hardware manages link-speed recovery failures, identifies **Flit Mode** signaling requirements, and explains the reset triggers for **changed_speed_recovery** and **directed_speed_change** variables.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.5 L0
children.description: This section specifies the transition requirements into the L0 link state for both Non-Flit and Flit Mode, detailing criteria such as consecutive Idle symbols/flits, de-skew completion, and link management register updates. It answers how a PCIe Link enters L0 while ensuring protocol-compliant data initialization and status reporting following Recovery or retrains.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.6 L0s
children.description: This section defines the L0s power-saving state within the PCIe physical layer, detailing the specific entry, idle, and fast training sequence (FTS) transitions for both transmitter and receiver logic. It addresses how link state machines manage power efficiency by enabling rapid recovery from L0s without requiring a full retraining process.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.6.1 Receiver L0s
children.description: This section defines the PCIe Link state transitions from **Receiver L0s**, detailing how the physical layer handles **EIOS (Electrical Idle Ordered Set)** and **EIOSQ** signaling to transition to **Recovery**, **L1**, or **L2** power management states. It specifies the hardware logic requirements for **ASPM/PCI-PM directed transitions** and the protocol for symmetric or asymmetric link-side signaling to reach electrical idle.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.6.1.3 Rx_L0s.FTS
children.description: This section details the state transition conditions for **Rx_L0s.FTS** within a PCIe link, specifying requirements for **Electrical Idle** exits, **SKP/SDS Ordered Set** detection, and **N_FTS timeout** calculations. It defines how a physical layer port manages transitions back to **L0** or **Recovery** status while ensuring proper **Lane-to-Lane de-skew** and data rate synchronization.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.6.2.3 Tx_L0s.FTS
children.description: This section defines PCIe Transmitter requirements for L0s state transitions, specifically detailing EIOSQ transmission, Electrical Idle entry, and the mandatory sequencing of Fast Training Sequences (FTS) and EIEOSQ symbols during recovery. It answers how a Port initiates L0s, enforces timing specifications, and manages FTS counts—including the role of the Extended Synch bit—to ensure successful signaling re-establishment.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.7 L1
children.description: This section details the operational transitions and state management for L1 components, specifically covering **L1.Entry initialization** and **L1.Idle power-save cycles**. It answers technical queries regarding entry-sequence requirements, low-power state maintenance, and transition logic within the L1 architectural framework.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.7.2 L1.Idle
children.description: This section defines the **L1.Idle** substate behavior for PCI Express links, specifying requirements for **Electrical Idle** entry, DC common-mode voltage compliance, and transition triggers to **Recovery**. It addresses technical questions regarding timing constraints, such as the 40 ns minimum stay requirement, and details the conditions for exiting L1 PM substates to resume link initialization.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.8 L2
children.description: This section defines the L2 layer architectural states, specifically covering Idle and TransmitWake operational modes. It details state transition logic, synchronization requirements, and signal behavior to address how the component manages power-efficient data transmission and link readiness.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.8.1 L2.Idle
children.description: This section defines the **L2.Idle** substate behavior for PCI Express, detailing **Electrical Idle** requirements, **TTX-IDLE-MIN** timing constraints, and **ZRX-DC** common-mode specifications. It specifies transitions to the **Detect** or **L2.TransmitWake** states by outlining conditions for **Beacon** detection and power restoration procedures.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.8.2 L2.TransmitWake
children.description: This section defines the L2.TransmitWake state machine transitions for PCIe Upstream Ports, detailing requirements for Electrical Idle exit detection, Beacon transmission protocols toward the Root Complex, and associated state transitions. It answers how an Upstream Port initiates wake-up signaling to restore power and manages the shift from L2 power management to the Detect state.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.9 Disabled
children.description: Section 4.2.7.9 defines the **Disabled** substate within the PCIe Link Training and Status State Machine (LTSSM), detailing the transition requirements for **Upstream Ports** and **Lanes**. The text specifies procedural mandates for **Electrical Idle exit** detection, **Beacon** transmission, and **TS1 Ordered Set** signaling required to effectively terminate a Link connection.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.10 Loopback
children.description: This section defines the loopback state machine lifecycle, covering transition procedures for **Loopback.Entry**, **Loopback.Active**, and **Loopback.Exit**. It addresses how signal integrity testing, diagnostic modes, and physical layer synchronization are initiated, maintained, and terminated within the communication interface.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.10.1 Loopback.Entry
children.description: This section defines the entry criteria and state transition requirements for the PCIe **Loopback.Entry** substate, focusing on **EIOS/EIOSQ** signaling, **LinkUp** status, and **Electrical Idle** recovery. It specifies technical conditions for **Upstream and Downstream Port** transitions to **Detect** and details 64.0/128.0 GT/s **equalization** procedures for the "Lane under test."

children.title: Section 4.2.7.10.2 Loopback.Active
children.description: This section defines the transition criteria and operational requirements for the **Loopback.Active** state within PCIe link training. It specifies conditions for **transmit_modified_compliance_pattern**, **Lane under test** determination, and the required **TS1 Ordered Set** signaling protocols for test equipment synchronization and state advancement.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.10.3 Loopback.Exit
children.description: This section details the **Loopback.Exit** state transition protocols, specifically covering **Electrical Idle** inference, **EIOS** (Electrical Idle Ordered Set) transmission requirements, and timing constraints for **Loopback Lead** and **Follower** synchronization. It specifies how PCIe devices handle sequence termination, exit latency, and the transition from active loopback to Electrical Idle states.

children.title: Section 4.2.7.11 Hot Reset
children.description: This section details the PCIe Hot Reset mechanism, specifically the state transitions triggered by TS1 Ordered Sets with the Hot_Reset_Request bit. It defines how Upstream and Downstream ports manage LinkUp status, timeout conditions, and propagation requirements to ensure synchronized system-wide or port-specific resets.

children.title: Section 4.2.8 Clock Tolerance Compensation
children.description: This section defines the Hot Reset signaling process, including requirements for transition triggers, timer management, and state machine behavior for Upstream and Downstream ports. Additionally, it introduces SKP Ordered Sets and elastic buffering mechanisms used to mitigate frequency discrepancies between Link partners during clock tolerance compensation.

children.title: Section 4.2.8.1 SKP Ordered Set for 8b/10b Encoding
children.description: This section defines the SKP (Skip) Ordered Set structure and transmission requirements for 8b/10b-encoded PCIe links used to compensate for clock frequency variances between components. It specifies symbol patterns, insertion rules, and frequency tolerances necessary for maintaining elastic buffer synchronization across high-speed serial lanes.

children.title: Section 4.2.8.2 SKP Ordered Set for 128b/130b Encoding
children.description: This section details technical specifications for SKP Ordered Set composition and scheduling under 128b/130b and 8b/10b encoding, contrasting SRNS and SRIS clocking architectures. It defines specific symbol lengths, frame structures (Standard vs. alternative formats), and buffer requirements for data transmission during non-flit mode operations.

children.title: Section 4.2.8.3 SKP Ordered Set for 1b/1b Encoding
children.description: This section defines the structural requirements and error-correction protocols for 1b/1b encoding SKP Ordered Sets, including symbol length, PHY Payload composition, and majority voting logic. It addresses how systems manage redundant control data, parity computation, and payload verification across transmit and receive operations.

children.title: Section 4.2.8.4 Rules for Transmitters
children.description: This section defines the PHY Payload structure for Control SKP Ordered Sets, detailing bit-level field definitions, parity calculation requirements, and error status mapping for the Polling.Compliance state. It specifies how Ports and Retimers manage data parity and payload transmission to ensure signal integrity across compliance and margin communication modes.

children.title: Section 4.2.8.5 Rules for Receivers
children.description: This section specifies receiver requirements for processing SKP Ordered Sets across diverse encoding schemes (8b/10b, 128b/130b, 1b/1b) and data rates (2.5 to 32.0 GT/s). It defines mandatory tolerance mandates for electrical idle recovery, multi-lane length consistency, and EDS token verification in Non-Flit Mode.

children.title: Section 4.2.8.6 SKP Ordered Set Usage with Optical Retimer Solutions
children.description: This section defines guidelines for SKP Ordered Set usage within PCIe Optical Retimer Solutions, specifically addressing SRIS/SRNS clocking operational requirements and protocols for transmitting sideband information at 8.0 GT/s or higher. It details implementation-specific methods for Sideband Payload communication, acknowledgement exchanges, and targeting Pseudo Ports during optical sub-link operations.

children.title: Section 4.2.9 Compliance Pattern in 8b/10b Encoding
children.description: This section defines the mandatory 8b/10b Compliance Pattern signaling requirements for the Polling.Compliance substate, detailing specific symbol sequences, negative running disparity alignment, and the deterministic four-symbol delay structure implemented on every eighth lane for multi-lane synchronization. It addresses how devices must structure bitstreams to ensure physical layer link testing and lane-to-lane deskew consistency.

children.title: Section 4.2.10 Modified Compliance Pattern in 8b/10b Encoding
children.description: This section specifies the 8b/10b Modified Compliance Pattern, detailing lane-by-lane sequences of K28.5, D21.5, D10.2, and out-of-phase symbols. It explains how these specific symbol patterns induce worst-case Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI) and crosstalk to facilitate rigorous physical layer compliance testing between adjacent lanes.

children.title: Section 4.2.11 Compliance Pattern in 128b/130b Encoding
children.description: This section specifies the structural requirements for the 128b/130b compliance pattern by defining mandatory Sync Headers, unscrambled data payloads, and lane-specific symbol values. It provides the technical implementation details necessary for verifying transmitter signal integrity and protocol conformance through standardized test sequences.

children.title: Section 4.2.12 Modified Compliance Pattern in 128b/130b Encoding
children.description: This section details the **Modified Compliance Pattern** used for **128b/130b encoding** to characterize **transmission line physical media attachments (PMA)**. Key technical topics include **PRBS31 patterns**, **scrambler bypass**, and **jitter test methodology**, addressing how to verify signal integrity and conformance testing requirements for high-speed serial links.

children.title: Section 4.2.13 Jitter Measurement Pattern in 128b/130b
children.description: This section defines the Modified Compliance Pattern and jitter measurement sequences required for 128b/130b encoding, specifically detailing block structures for non-SRIS and SRIS operational modes. It clarifies compliant stress patterns, scrambling requirements, and the specific unscrambled test payloads used to achieve alternating bit transitions for transmitter jitter analysis.

children.title: Section 4.2.14 Compliance Pattern in 1b/1b Encoding
children.description: This section specifies the 137-block compliance pattern for 1b/1b encoding in high-speed links, detailing unscrambled voltage levels, toggle patterns, and DC-balance-focused hex payloads per lane. It provides exact block sequences to verify signal integrity and physical layer transmitter compliance testing for multi-lane interfaces.

children.title: Section 4.2.15 Modified Compliance Pattern in 1b/1b Encoding
children.description: This section defines the Modified Compliance Pattern for 1b/1b encoding, detailing prohibited bit sequences, running disparity constraints, and strict transition requirements. It addresses how to maintain signal integrity and DC balance within high-speed data streams to ensure receiver synchronization and protocol-level compliance.

children.title: Section 4.2.16 Jitter Measurement Pattern in 1b/1b Encoding
children.description: This section specifies the **Modified Compliance Pattern** parameters for **jitter testing** within **1b/1b encoding**, detailing the structural sequences for **standard** versus **SRIS** operation. It defines technical requirements for **scrambler reset (EIEOSQ)**, **SKP Ordered Set insertion**, **scrambled payload generation**, and exemption status from standard **FEC/CRC/Flit** protocols.

children.title: Section 4.2.17 Toggle Patterns in 1b/1b encoding
children.description: This section specifies 1b/1b encoding toggle patterns—including High Swing, Low Swing, and LSTP0-4—required for high-accuracy single-edge jitter measurement at 64.0 GT/s and 128 GT/s data rates. It defines the mapping of binary symbols to voltage levels 0–3 and clarifies which patterns are DC-balanced versus those restricted to CLB/CBB compliance testing.

children.title: Section 4.2.18 Lane Margining at Receiver
children.description: Section 4.2.18 defines the protocols for Lane Margining at the Receiver, covering Command/Response flows, Flit Mode execution, and hardware testing requirements. It addresses how to configure margin types, manage payload fields, and validate signal integrity performance across PCIe high-speed data links.

children.title: Section 4.2.18.1 Receiver Number, Margin Type, Usage Model, and Margin Payload Fields
children.description: This section defines LSTP4 and SHSTP test patterns, mandatory Lane Margining at Receiver requirements for data rates of 16.0 GT/s and higher, and the operational constraints for performing voltage and timing margining in the L0 state. It addresses technical specifications for compliance testing, hardware support criteria for Retimers, and the conditions required to initiate or terminate margin commands.

children.title: Section 4.2.18.1.2 Margin Payload for Step Margin Commands
children.description: This section defines the margin payload structure and execution status codes for PCIe Step Margin commands targeting timing and voltage offsets. It specifies the 2-bit Step Margin Execution Status field, handling of MErrorCount, and the bit-level mapping for timing offset configurations, answering how receivers report process status and implement directional margin adjustments.

children.title: Section 4.2.18.2 Margin Command and Response Flow
children.description: This section defines the **Margin Payload** bit-field structure and coordinate logic for adjusting **Receiver voltage or timing offsets** relative to normal operating parameters. It details how the **MErrorCount** reports validation results from data/error samplers, addressing how to configure margining commands and interpret expected error reporting requirements for hardware-specific stress testing.

children.title: Section 4.2.18.3 Flit Mode 8.0 GT/s Margining Behavior
children.description: This section details the physical layer margining requirements for PCIe 8.0 GT/s Flit Mode, specifically addressing eye-width and eye-height electrical validation procedures. It defines mandated receiver-side test patterns, parameter constraints for error-rate measurement, and configuration procedures for compliance-directed margining cycles.

children.title: Section 4.2.18.4 Receiver Margin Testing Requirements
children.description: This section outlines protocols for PCI Express Receiver Margin Testing, covering implementation rules for Reserved bits, Reserved encodings, and mandatory Retimer register access requirements for 16.0 GT/s and higher data rates. It resolves operational questions regarding command status reporting, Retimer forwarding behaviors, and data rate limitations for Lane Margining.

children.title: Section 4.3 Retimers
children.description: This section defines operational requirements for protocol-aware PCIe Retimers, specifying a maximum limit of two standard Retimers or four Four Retimer Aware (FRA) devices per link. It addresses key technical constraints including equalization procedures, SKP Ordered Set modifications, and interoperability mandates for complex topologies involving active cables and various form factors.

children.title: Section 4.3.1 Retimer Requirements
children.description: This section defines operational requirements for PCIe Retimers, detailing independent Link equalization for 128b/130b encoding and dynamic Pseudo Port orientation. It specifically delineates architectural specifications for Optical Retimer Solutions and Optical Aware Retimers, addressing how optical technology-based extensions integrate with protocol-aware Pseudo Ports.

children.title: Section 4.3.2 Supported Retimer Topologies
children.description: This section details supported PCIe Retimer link topologies, defining the distinction between standard and Four Retimer Aware (FRA) configurations based on EFM_CNTL2 (N_FTS) decoding capabilities. It establishes technical constraints for maximum Retimer counts, pseudo-port orientation, and data rate alignment, while providing deployment recommendations for optical extension solutions.

children.title: Section 4.3.3 Variables
children.description: This section specifies the initialization criteria and default variable states for an Optical Retimer following a Fundamental Reset or specific TS2 Ordered Set configuration. It identifies the conditions and binary values required to reset functional variables, including link status, equalization completion flags, and port orientation, within a defined 1 μs timing window.

children.title: Section 4.3.4 Receiver Impedance Propagation Rules
children.description: This section defines the methodologies for propagating receiver impedance values across interconnect topologies, detailing requirements for impedance matching, signal integrity, and termination constraints. It answers how designers ensure electrical continuity and optimal impedance control between drivers and receivers during high-speed PCB routing.

children.title: Section 4.3.6 Activation Rules
children.description: Section 4.3.6 defines the **Activation Rules** for Retimers, specifying timing requirements for **Receiver impedance** determination, **Electrical Idle** conditions, and **Reset** transitions. It clarifies operational constraints for Retimer Transmitters, Receivers, and **Optical Aware Retimers** during the progression between **Activation, Forwarding, and Execution modes**.

children.title: Section 4.3.7 Forwarding Rules
children.description: This section defines the operational requirements for Retimers in Forwarding mode, covering state transitions, Electrical Idle thresholds, and data path integrity for Optical Aware configurations. It addresses how Retimers manage Symbol forwarding, lane-to-channel mapping, and signaling conditions for high-speed data rates up to and exceeding 64.0 GT/s.

children.title: Section 4.3.7.2 Orientation, Lane Numbers, and Data Stream Mode Rules
children.description: This section defines mechanisms for Retimers to identify data stream types—specifically distinguishing between training sets and non-training sets—and establishes dynamic determination logic for Port orientation and Lane configuration during link training. It addresses critical technical prerequisites for synchronization, including `RT_error_data_rate` settings, Electrical Idle inference, and the assignment of Upstream/Downstream Port roles based on TS1 Ordered Set detection.

children.title: Section 4.3.7.3 Electrical Idle Exit Rules
children.description: This section specifies mandatory Electrical Idle Exit (EIE) procedures for Retimers operating above 2.5 GT/s, detailing requirements for EIEOS transmission, 128b/130b Block Alignment, and optical link synchronization. It defines the technical rules for handling Electrical Idle exit detection, pseudo-port behavior, and specific timing constraints when lanes fail to meet ZRX-DC impedance requirements.

children.title: Section 4.3.7.4 Data Rate Change and Determination Rules
children.description: This section outlines the protocols for adaptive bitrate adjustments, covering mechanisms like dynamic rate control, threshold-based switching, and buffer-aware optimization. It provides technical specifications to resolve how network congestion, link quality degradation, and client-side feedback loops trigger rate adaptation in real-time transmission systems.

children.title: Section 4.3.7.5 Electrical Idle Entry Rules
children.description: This section defines the criteria and state-machine logic for Retimer data rate transitions upon Electrical Idle entry, governing the calculation of `RT_next_data_rate` and `RT_error_data_rate` variables. It specifies the conditions required for synchronization between Pseudo Ports when switching between 2.5 GT/s, 32.0 GT/s, or higher common data rates during L0-to-L1 or Recovery.Speed transitions.

children.title: Section 4.3.7.6 Transmitter Settings Determination Rules
children.description: This section defines the Retimer transmitter equalization (EQ) initialization process for 128.0 GT/s transitions, specifically detailing the logic for the `RT_128G_EQ_complete` variable. It mandates whether Retimers must utilize cached coefficient settings from previous 128 GT/s sessions or derived 1b/1b preset values from 64.0 GT/s signaling to ensure link compliance.

children.title: Section 4.3.7.7 Ordered Set Modification Rules
children.description: This section details the mandatory and implementation-specific modification rules for PCIe Ordered Sets (TS0, TS1, TS2) performed by a Retimer during signal transmission. It governs how Retimers update transmitter equalization parameters, including Precoding requests, LF/FS settings, and Pre-Cursor/Cursor coefficients, to ensure signal integrity across pseudo ports.

children.title: Section 4.3.7.8 DLLP, TLP, Logical Idle, and Flit Modification Rules
children.description: This section defines the structural requirements and precise bit-level modification rules for managing DLLPs, TLPs, and Logical Idle symbols within PCIe Flit-mode data streams. It clarifies how hardware logic must handle frame encapsulation, packet alignment, and payload integrity during transitions between control and data link layer transmissions.

children.title: Section 4.3.7.10 8b/10b Scrambling Rules
children.description: This section details Retimer protocols for handling 8b/10b scrambling, specifically regarding error mitigation and data transparency. It defines the replacement process for corrupted symbols using D21.3 codes and clarifies how Retimers manage 8b/10b decoding, running disparity violations, and scrambling state detection.

children.title: Section 4.3.7.13 Loopback
children.description: This section defines Retimer state machine transitions for Hot Reset and Disable Link requests, specifying how variable resets, transmitter states, and data rate configurations respond to TS1 Ordered Sets and Electrical Idle triggers. It establishes the mandatory behavior for managing link disconnection and re-initialization protocols across Upstream and Downstream Pseudo Ports, including specific provisions for Optical Retimer Solutions.

children.title: Section 4.3.7.14 Compliance Receive Rules
children.description: This section defines compliance receive behaviors for PCIe Retimers, detailing lane-level forwarding triggers based on TS1 Ordered Set detection and `Compliance_Receive_Request` status. It specifies the technical procedures for transitioning to Electrical Idle and resetting data rate variables when EIOS patterns are received on either the Upstream or Downstream path.

children.title: Section 4.3.7.15 Enter Compliance Rules
children.description: This section defines PCIe Retimer compliance testing requirements, specifically detailing Transmitter preset configuration for Pseudo Port A, equalization procedures for Pseudo Port B, and mandatory Modified Compliance Pattern forwarding. It addresses how Retimers must manage per-Lane Symbol lock, error counting, and error processing during 128b/130b or 1b/1b encoding states.

children.title: Section 4.3.8 Execution Mode Rules
children.description: This section defines the execution mode protocols for component loading, PCIe link equalization, and follower loopback testing. It addresses technical requirements for physical layer initialization and operational states during system hardware validation.

children.title: Section 4.3.8.1 CompLoadBoard Rules
children.description: Section 4.3.8.1 governs the CompLoadBoard logic, detailing operational requirements for entry triggers, pattern recognition, and exit criteria. This section defines the technical parameters, state-transition rules, and algorithmic constraints necessary to execute and finalize board loading processes.

children.title: Section 4.3.8.1.1 CompLoadBoard.Entry
children.description: This section defines the transition logic for Optical Retimers entering the CompLoadBoard state, specifically detailing variable resets, data rate downshifting to 2.5 GT/s, and independent Pseudo Port protocol execution. It outlines implementation requirements for the Retimer Compliance SOS register to govern SKP Ordered Set transmission during compliance pattern generation.

children.title: Section 4.3.8.1.3 CompLoadBoard.Exit
children.description: This section defines the exit and transition logic for the `CompLoadBoard` state in PCIe link training, specifically managing Electrical Idle, Receiver Detection, and Compliance Pattern transmission protocols across Pseudo Ports A and B. It establishes the mechanical requirements for Optical Retimer solutions and dictates the data rate, de-emphasis, and preset settings required for compliant signal transmission.

children.title: Section 4.3.8.2 Link Equalization Rules
children.description: This section defines the operational procedures for Retimer Link Equalization, specifically addressing Pseudo Port state transitions, data rate adjustments to 2.5 GT/s, and the transition from Forwarding to Execution mode. It details how Retimers manage Lane/Link number synchronization and timing mandates to ensure seamless participation in PCI Express equalization phases without requiring protocol-level timeout modifications.

children.title: Section 4.3.8.2.1.3 Phase 3 Active
children.description: This section specifies link equalization procedures for 64.0 GT/s and 16.0 GT/s operations, detailing Phase 3 Active requirements, Upstream/Downstream Pseudo Port synchronization, and Retimer Equalization Extend bit signaling. It defines state transition timing, EC field management for Optical Aware Retimers, and modified transmitter behavior protocols for traversing equalization phases.

children.title: Section 4.3.8.2.2.2 Phase 1
children.description: This section details PCIe Retimer Phase 1 equalization transitions, specifically governing the conditions and timers for moving to Phase 3 Passive or Force Timeout based on Retimer Equalization Extend bits. It specifies behavior for 64.0 GT/s operations, signaling requirements for TS1 Ordered Sets, and the storage of LF and FS values for Phase 2 transmitter adjustments.

children.title: Section 4.3.8.2.2.5 Phase 3
children.description: This section defines transmitter behavior during Phase 3 of Link Equalization, detailing specific timeout durations, bit settings (Retimer Equalization Extend), and transition criteria for Phase 2 Passive across varying PCIe data rates (8.0 to 64.0+ GT/s). It specifies the operative conditions required to exit Phase 3 or trigger a Force Timeout based on operational lane status and configuration variables.

children.title: Section 4.3.8.2.3 Force Timeout
children.description: This section specifies the Force Timeout recovery mechanism for PCIe Optical Retimer Solutions operating at 64.0 GT/s and above. It defines strict timing requirements, Electrical Idle Exit patterns, and recovery logic for transitioning between data rates and entering Forwarding mode when path errors occur.

children.title: Section 4.3.8.3 Follower Loopback
children.description: Section 4.3.8.3 details the state machine transitions for Follower Loopback, specifically covering entry conditions, active monitoring, and exit protocols. This section identifies operational requirements, signaling synchronization, and diagnostic trigger points to answer how the system handles circular data paths within a follower node.

children.title: Section 4.3.8.3.1 Follower Loopback.Entry
children.description: This section details the **Follower Loopback.Entry** transition process for PCIe Retimers, specifically governing state transitions when negotiating data rates of 64.0 GT/s and above. It defines the technical requirements for **Optical Retimer** synchronization, **Electrical Idle Exit Pattern** timing, **EIOS/EIOSQ** signaling protocols, and the subsequent fallback criteria to 2.5 GT/s or **Forwarding mode** upon timeout or successful idle synchronization.

children.title: Section 4.3.8.3.3 Follower Loopback.Exit
children.description: This section defines the transition behavior for Pseudo Ports during **Follower Loopback.Exit**, specifically governing **RT_LinkUp** status, **Electrical Idle** requirements, and **2.5 GT/s** data rate resets. It clarifies how a Loopback Follower and non-Loopback Pseudo Port restore connectivity and transition to **Forwarding mode** upon exiting the loopback state.

children.title: Section 4.3.9 Retimer Latency
children.description: This section outlines specifications for PCIe retimer latency, detailing standardized measurement methodologies, mandatory maximum duration limits, and operational impacts on upstream and downstream ports. It addresses requirements for signal integrity compliance and timing constraints necessary to maintain link communication standards within the PCIe architecture.

children.title: Section 4.3.9.1 Measurement
children.description: This section outlines the operational procedures for Pseudo Ports during Loopback Measurement, specifically detailing state transitions, transmitter Electrical Idle requirements, and data rate adjustments to 2.5 GT/s. It defines the behavioral exceptions for Loopback Follower synchronization and post-measurement state recovery to Forwarding mode.

children.title: Section 4.3.9.2 Maximum Limit on Retimer Latency
children.description: This section defines the mandatory timing constraints and buffer capacity thresholds for PCI Express retimers to ensure link-level interoperability. It specifies the maximum allowable latency bounds for packet forwarding and identifies the technical parameters required for protocol-compliant synchronization between upstream and downstream components.

children.title: Section 4.3.9.3 Impacts on Upstream and Downstream Ports
children.description: This section defines Retimer latency limits across PCIe data rates (2.5 GT/s to 128 GT/s) and specifies performance impacts on Data Link Layer buffers, Flow Control credits, and REPLAY_TIMER configurations. It addresses how Retimer-induced round trip delay necessitates increased port-level buffering and describes specific latency considerations for SRIS-enabled clock domain elastic stores.

children.title: Section 4.3.10 SRIS
children.description: Section 4.3.10 defines Retimer latency requirements, specifying maximum symbol time limits across data rates and the impact of SRIS (Separate Reference Independent SSC) on elastic store buffering. This section addresses how Retimers affect channel latency, buffer sizing, flow control, and the necessary configurations for SRIS-enabled clock domain synchronization.

children.title: Section 4.3.11 L1 PM Substates Support
children.description: This section defines PCI Express Retimer requirements for supporting L1 PM Substates, specifically detailing L1.1 state entry and exit procedures triggered by CLKREQ# signaling. It answers technical requirements for REFCLK management, Electrical Idle behavior, and limitations regarding L1.2 non-support within Retimer architectures.

children.title: Section 4.3.12 Retimer Configuration Parameters
children.description: Section 4.3.12 outlines required implementation-specific mechanisms for Retimer configuration parameters and details CLKREQ# signal connection topologies for L1 PM Substates. This section addresses how to manage reference clock distribution and power management synchronization across Retimers, Downstream Ports, and Upstream Ports within a PCIe link.

children.title: Section 4.3.12.2 Per Physical Pseudo Port Parameters
children.description: Section 4.3.12.2 defines configuration parameters for Retimer physical pseudo ports, detailing settings for Port Orientation Methods, Maximum Data Rate, and SRIS Enable. This section clarifies when retimers must implement dynamic versus static port configuration and specifies how these parameters apply to global retimer settings and optical deployments.

children.title: Section 4.3.13 In Band Register Access
children.description: This section defines the "In-Band Register Access" mechanism for PCIe Retimers operating at 16.0 GT/s or higher, detailing how to utilize Control SKP Ordered Sets and Margin Commands to retrieve Vendor Defined (A0h–FFh) or read-only status data (offsets 80h–84h) via the Link. It resolves requirements for retimer register accessibility, mandatory return values for capability verification, and reserved address space formatting.

children.title: Chapter 5. Power Management
children.description: This section details the power management architecture for PCI Express, covering topics such as link states (L0-L2), L1 PM substates, PCI-compatible mechanisms, AUX power support, and state transition recovery requirements. It answers how devices handle power state transitions, manage event signaling via DLLPs and messages, and maintain system compatibility during low-power modes.

children.title: Section 5.1 Overview
children.description: This section covers PCI Express power management (PM) architecture, detailing Function-based D-states (D0–D3) and Link-based L-states (L0–L1.2). It answers questions regarding system state transitions, native PM extensions, and the mechanisms used to identify capabilities, manage Link power, and support remote system wakeup functionality.

children.title: Section 5.2 Link State Power Management
children.description: This section details PCIe Link State Power Management, covering autonomous Active State Power Management (ASPM), L-state transitions, and resource reactivation protocols. It explains how hardware-directed link power states operate independently of D-state software to optimize power consumption during idle periods.

children.title: Section 5.3 PCI-PM Software Compatible Mechanisms
children.description: Section 5.3 details PCI-PM software-compatible power management, covering D-state transitions, Link Power Management state control, and Power Management Event (PME) mechanisms. This content addresses how operating system software manages function-level power states, coordinates link-level power transitions, and handles event-driven wake-up signaling within the PCI framework.

children.title: Section 5.3.1 Device Power Management States (D-States) of a Function
children.description: This section details PCIe Device Power Management (D-States) and Link (L-States), focusing on power-saving mechanisms like CLKREQ# clock gating, Vaux requirements, and L2/L3 state transitions. It defines the functional behavior of intermediate power states (D1, D2) and the operational requirements for transitioning components into low-power states while maintaining platform power-management compatibility.

children.title: Section 5.3.1.3 D2 State
children.description: This section defines the D0 and D1 power management states, detailing the transition requirements for a PCI Express Function between "uninitialized," "active," and D1 states. It answers how Functional Level Resets (FLR), configuration bits, and TLP handling protocols govern operational readiness and error reporting when exiting or entering specific power states.

children.title: Section 5.3.1.4 D3 State
children.description: This section details the D3 power management state, encompassing both D3Hot and D3Cold operational modes within the PCI Express architecture. It defines power consumption requirements, transition conditions, and the functional constraints necessary for maintaining device state during low-power suspension.

children.title: Section 5.3.1.4.1 D3Hot State
children.description: This section specifies PCIe power management requirements for the D3Hot state, covering recovery time latency, Functional Context retention, and the impact of the No_Soft_Reset bit. It defines operational prerequisites for System Software regarding re-initialization during D3Hot to D0 transitions and behavior upon entering L2/L3 Link states.

children.title: Section 5.3.1.4.2 D3Cold State
children.description: Section 5.3.1.4.2 defines the **D3Cold power state** transition mechanics, including the **Cold Reset** process, **D0uninitialized** state restoration, and **PME context** retention requirements. The text addresses how **Multi-Function Device (MFD)** configurations and **ARI** settings impact device-side control register consistency during reset sequences.

children.title: Section 5.3.2 PM Software Control of the Link Power Management State
children.description: This section outlines the software-driven mechanisms for PME event acknowledgment and Link power state transitions in PCI Express architectures. It defines the technical interdependencies between component D-states (D0–D3Cold) and corresponding Link states (L0–L3), specifying auxiliary power requirements for wake-up signaling and state preservation.

children.title: Section 5.3.2.1 Entry into the L1 State
children.description: This section defines the mandatory conditions for PCIe Link transitions into the L1 power state for ARI, SR-IOV, and SIOV devices. It details the technical sequence of hardware-level signaling—including PM_Request_Ack DLLPs and TLP scheduling protocols—required to move a Downstream component from D0 into a low-power state.

children.title: Section 5.3.2.2 Exit from L1 State
children.description: This section defines protocols for exiting the PCIe L1 low-power state to L0, covering initiation triggers, flow control update requirements, and upstream-to-downstream signaling procedures. It details state transition mechanisms, specifically addressing how components manage link reactivation to resume TLP transmission following power management configuration requests.

children.title: Section 5.3.2.3 Entry into the L2/L3 Ready State
children.description: This section defines the transition protocol to the **L2/L3 Ready state**, governing the handshake procedure between Upstream and Downstream ports via **PM_Enter_L23 DLLPs** and **PME_Turn_Off/PME_TO_Ack** sequences. It explains the requirements for achieving a power-down state and details the necessary operational readiness steps before loss of power or reference clock.

children.title: Section 5.3.3 Power Management Event Mechanisms
children.description: This section details PCI Express Power Management Event (PME) mechanisms, covering link wakeup procedures, PM_PME message packetization, and associated delivery state machine logic. It specifies technical rules for event signaling and resolves how devices trigger, route, and manage wake-up transitions from low-power states.

children.title: Section 5.3.3.1 Motivation
children.description: This section details the L2/L3 Ready state transition protocol for PCIe power management, focusing on the `PME_Turn_Off`/`PME_TO_Ack` handshake and the use of `PM_Enter_L23` DLLPs. It answers how downstream components coordinate their readiness for power and reference clock removal to ensure software-compatible PME signaling.

children.title: Section 5.3.3.2 Link Wakeup
