children.description: This section details PCI Express link wakeup mechanisms, covering in-band Beacon signaling and sideband WAKE# open-drain protocols. It explains how switches translate between these methods and defines functional requirements for power controllers and components requesting platform power restoration.

children.title: Section 5.3.3.2.1 PME Synchronization
children.description: This section details the PCIe PME synchronization protocol, specifically defining the sequence of the PME_Turn_Off broadcast message and the corresponding PME_TO_Ack TLP response. It clarifies how Root Complexes and Switches coordinate the cessation of PM_PME messages and manage acknowledgement aggregation to safely transition components to low-power states.

children.title: Section 5.3.3.3 PM_PME Messages
children.description: This section specifies the protocol for propagating PM_PME and PME_Turn_Off messages to safely transition hierarchical links into L2/L3 Ready power states. It defines mandated timeout mechanisms for deadlock avoidance and establishes critical timing requirements—including the 100 ns minimum wait—before the power delivery manager removes reference clocks and main power.

children.title: Section 5.3.3.3.1 PM_PME “Backpressure” Deadlock Avoidance
children.description: This section details PCIe deadlocks caused by limited Root Complex PM_PME buffer capacity and the resulting request/completion ordering conflicts. It specifies mandated forward-progress mechanisms, including credit-based flow control and PM_PME message discarding, to prevent system-wide stalls during PME handling.

children.title: Section 5.3.3.4 PME Rules
children.description: This section defines mandatory deadlock-avoidance requirements for Root Complex PM_PME Message buffering, addressing risks where simultaneous PME propagation and Configuration Read completion traffic create circular dependencies. It specifies that Root Complexes must ensure forward progress by managing flow control credits and discarding overflow PM_PME Messages to prevent system-level stalls.

children.title: Section 5.3.3.5 PM_PME Delivery State Machine
children.description: This section describes the PM_PME delivery state machine, governing how PCI Express Links transition between "Communicating" and "Non-communicating" states to manage power management events. It details technical requirements for PME assertion, PME_Turn_Off message handling, and wakeup signaling processes during power state transitions or resets.

children.title: Section 5.4 Native PCI Express Power Management Mechanisms
children.description: This section covers Native PCI Express Power Management, detailing L0s and L1 Active State Power Management (ASPM) link states and hardware-autonomous power transitions. It addresses how PCIe devices autonomously conserve power during idle periods while maintaining low-latency wake-up capabilities.

children.title: Section 5.4.1 Active State Power Management (ASPM)
children.description: This section details the **Active State Power Management (ASPM)** state machine transitions, specifically focusing on **PME Sent** and **Link Reactivation** protocol handling. It addresses how PCI Express functions manage **PME_Status** timeouts, **PME_Turn_Off** messages, and post-reset state recovery to ensure power management efficiency and legacy software compatibility.

children.title: Section 5.4.1.1 L0s ASPM State
children.description: This section defines the logic for enabling ASPM states (L0s and L1) within a PCI Express Multi-Function device based on the ASPM Control field settings of functions currently in the D0 state. It specifies how these dependencies dictate system behavior and clarifies that L0s is strictly incompatible with Flit Mode and Retimers.

children.title: Section 5.4.1.1.1 Entry into the L0s State
Section 5.4.1.1.2 Exit from the L0s State
children.description: This section delineates L0s state exit and entry requirements, focusing on transmission policies, idle condition definitions, and cross-device compatibility mandates. It addresses how devices manage power-saving transitions on transmitting lanes and specifies the criteria determining when a Port qualifies as idle.

children.title: Section 5.4.1.3 ASPM L1 State
children.description: This section defines the mandatory procedures for Upstream and Downstream Ports to exit the PCIe ASPM L0s low-power state and transition to L0. It addresses operational requirements for Switch-based topologies, specifically detailing how components trigger link-wide state transitions upon detecting upstream or downstream communication needs.

children.title: Section 5.4.1.3.1 ASPM Entry into the L1 State
children.description: This section details the configuration and initiation requirements for PCIe ASPM L1 state entry, including software sequencing protocols and the functional roles of PM_Active_State_Request_L1, PM_Request_Ack, and PM_Active_State_Nak messages. It addresses technical compatibility concerns regarding legacy software support for L1-only hardware and the mandatory Upstream/Downstream enable-disable sequencing logic.

children.title: Section 5.4.1.3.2 Exit from the L1 State
children.description: This section details the procedures for exiting the PCIe L1 Link state, covering initiation protocols for both upstream and downstream components and recommended post-exit flow control updates. It addresses how components transition to the L0 state, the specific timing requirements for sending flow control update DLLPs, and the mechanical sequence for restoring active communication.

children.title: Section 5.4.1.4 ASPM Configuration
children.description: This section details the PCIe ASPM (Active State Power Management) configuration requirements, specifying support encodings for L0s/L1 link power states and the role of the Slot Clock Configuration bit in reference clock synchronization. It answers how components report power management capabilities and how hardware identifies shared versus independent physical clocking mechanisms to ensure stable link transitions.

children.title: Section 5.4.1.4.1 Software Flow for Enabling or Disabling ASPM
children.description: This section defines the PCIe ASPM Control register bit-field configurations for managing L0s and L1 power-state entry. It specifies protocol requirements for Upstream and Downstream Port behavior, addressing how software flow controls link-state transitions, packet transmission inhibitors, and L1 request acknowledgments.

children.title: Section 5.5 L1 PM Substates
children.description: This section details the L1 PM Substate power management framework, covering exit latency calculations, ASPM configuration, and the operational requirements for L1.0 and L1.1 substates. It addresses how PCI Express system software determines acceptable latency thresholds and manages Link power through Electrical Idle detection and CLKREQ# signaling.

children.title: Section 5.5.1 Entry conditions for L1 PM Substates and L1.0 Requirements
children.description: This section defines entry criteria for PCIe L1 PM substates (L1.1 and L1.2), specifically detailing how CLKREQ# signaling, LTR latency thresholds, and configuration enable bits trigger transitions from L1.0. It addresses operational requirements for both PCI-PM and ASPM power management states, clarifying the conditions under which downstream and upstream ports must enter lower-power substates.

children.title: Section 5.5.2 L1.1 Requirements
children.description: This section defines the mandatory operational constraints and technical transition protocols for exiting the L1.1 power-saving state. It addresses critical questions regarding hardware-level wake-up sequences, signaling requirements, and state-machine transitions necessary to restore full interface functionality.

children.title: Section 5.5.2.1 Exit from L1.1
children.description: This section specifies the protocol for transitioning from PCI Express L1.1 or L1.2 sub-states to Recovery, focusing on `CLKREQ#` signaling requirements for Upstream and Downstream Ports. It delineates electrical idle exit mechanisms, Common Mode maintenance, and Refclk activation constraints defined by the PCI Express Mini CEM specification and LTR latency values.

children.title: Section 5.5.3 L1.2 Requirements
children.description: This section defines the L1.2 substate requirements for PCI Express links, mandating state restoration, LTSSM consistency, and PHY integrity during low-power transitions. It details the operational framework and transition substates (Entry, Idle, Exit) required to maintain functional indistinguishability from L1.0 states.

children.title: Section 5.5.3.1 L1.2.Entry
children.description: This section defines the L1.2.Entry transitional substate within the PCIe L1.2 power management architecture, specifically outlining requirements for Refclk shutdown, CLKREQ# deassertion monitoring, and electrical idle detect settings. The text provides technical specifications for state transition timing (TL1O_REFCLK_OFF, TPOWER_OFF) and governance for resolving boundary conditions occurring during simultaneous port signaling.

children.title: Section 5.5.3.3 L1.2.Exit
children.description: This section defines the L1.2.Idle and L1.2.Exit state protocols for PCIe link power management, focusing on CLKREQ# assertion requirements and PHY power-up sequences. It specifies the rules for Upstream and Downstream Port behavior during L1.2 transitions to ensure stable link recovery and signal synchronization.

children.title: Section 5.5.3.3.1 Exit from L1.2
children.description: This section defines the hardware signal sequence and timing constraints for transitioning from PCI Express L1.2 substate back to L1.0 using the CLKREQ# mechanism. It addresses requirements for power-up sequencing, Refclk stabilization, and mandatory training protocols (TS1) necessary to re-establish common mode signaling.

children.title: Section 5.5.5 L1 PM Substates Timing Parameters
children.description: This section specifies the configuration requirements and synchronization protocols for enabling ASPM and PCI-PM L1.1/L1.2 sublevels. It answers how to correctly sequence enable bits between Upstream and Downstream ports while defining mandatory prerequisites for timing parameters such as `TPOWER_ON`, `Common_Mode_Restore_Time`, and `LTR_L1.2_THRESHOLD`.

children.title: Section 5.5.6 Link Activation
children.description: Section 5.5.6 defines the **Link Activation** mechanism and **L1.2 timing parameters** (Table 5-11), specifying requirements for CLKREQ# deassertion, Refclk transitions, and LTR-based power-on delays. This section details how to manage L1.1/L1.2 state exits to avoid performance stalls during latency-sensitive operations, such as D3Hot to D0 transitions.

children.title: Section 5.6 Auxiliary Power Support
children.description: This section outlines the logic for INTx interrupt signaling during auxiliary power transitions and defines requirements for PCI Express auxiliary power (Vaux) management. It specifies conditions for Link Activation status triggers and clarifies the implementation of the Aux Power PM Enable bit within the Device Control Register.

children.title: Section 5.7 Power Management System Messages and DLLPs
children.description: This section details PCI Express power management mechanisms, specifically defining the configuration of auxiliary power via the Aux Power PM Enable bit, sticky status preservation, and firmware-based allocation. It provides technical specifications for link-level DLLPs and transaction layer messages, serving as the definitive reference for the packet types and structural rules governing power state transitions.

children.title: Section 5.8 PCI Function Power State Transitions
children.description: This section defines the mandatory protocols for PCI function power state transitions, detailing mechanisms for D0–D3 state progression, power management registers, and associated latency requirements. It addresses how PCI functions initialize wake-up events, manage state-specific power consumption constraints, and maintain configuration space integrity during power state switching.

children.title: Section 5.9 State Transition Recovery Time Requirements
children.description: This section defines compliance mandates for recovery latency during operational state transitions, focusing on recovery time objectives (RTO), fault-tolerant restoration, and system stabilization thresholds. It addresses technical requirements for minimizing downtime, validating state consistency, and meeting critical availability service-level agreements (SLAs) during hardware or software failover events.

children.title: Section 5.10 SR-IOV Power Management
children.description: This section outlines SR-IOV power management state transitions, recovery timing requirements, and PCI-PM configuration space rules. It addresses how software-controlled state changes, Fundamental Resets, and transition delays dictate Function accessibility and system power management for SR-IOV devices.

children.title: Section 5.10.1 VF Device Power Management States
children.description: This section details the power management states for Virtual Function (VF) devices, covering transitions between D0, D1, D2, and D3 power modes in compliance with PCI Express specifications. It defines architectural requirements for VF power state signaling, transitions, and register access rules to manage energy consumption and device availability within virtualized environments.

children.title: Section 5.10.2 PF Device Power Management States
children.description: This section details SR-IOV Virtual Function (VF) power management states relative to Physical Functions (PFs), addressing compliance with PCI Power Management Capability and D0active state transition requirements. It specifies operational dependencies, initialization criteria following Functional Level Resets (FLR), and necessary software procedures to prevent undefined device behavior during power transitions.

children.title: Section 5.11 PCI Bridges and Power Management
children.description: This section outlines the power management requirements for SR-IOV PCI bridges, specifically detailing state dependencies between Physical Functions (PFs) and Virtual Functions (VFs). It explains how PF power state transitions, the `No_Soft_Reset` bit, and the `D3Hot` to `D3Cold` states impact VF configuration, function availability, and context retention.

children.title: Section 5.11.1 Switches and PCI Express to PCI Bridges
children.description: This section defines the power management architecture for PCI Express Switches and Bridges, covering bus power/clock controls (BPCC_En, B2_B3#) and the shared policies dictated by the PMCSR_BSE register. It addresses technical requirements for managing secondary bus power states and clarifies the distinction between PME Generation and Wakeup Events within the legacy and PCIe bridge framework.

children.title: Section 5.12 Power Management Events
children.description: This section outlines Power Management Events for PCI Express bridges, covering Wakeup Events, PME generation mechanisms, and system software responsibilities for managing bus power state transitions. It defines the functional relationship between Bridge Power Management Control and Status Registers (PMCSR_BSE) and the PME# signal, answering how hardware signaling triggers system-level power state requests.

children.title: Chapter 6. System Architecture
children.description: Chapter 6 details the fundamental architectural framework of PCI Express, covering critical system-level mechanisms including interrupts, power management, hot-plug support, and error signaling. This section defines the protocols and rules for device synchronization, link speed management, Atomic Operations, and Root Complex topology, providing essential guidance for implementing reliable, power-efficient, and interoperable system interconnects.

children.title: Section 6.1 Interrupt and PME Support
children.description: This section defines the PCI Express interrupt and Power Management Event (PME) infrastructure, covering legacy INTx emulation, Message Signaled Interrupts (MSI/MSI-X), and native versus legacy PME software models. It addresses how PCIe devices signal system events and manage power transitions within both pure PCIe and mixed PCIe/PCI hierarchies.

children.title: Section 6.1.1 Rationale for PCI Express Interrupt Model
children.description: This section details the rationale for the PCI Express interrupt model, covering legacy INTx emulation, Message Signaled Interrupts (MSI/MSI-X), and their specific requirements for Endpoints, SR-IOV, and SIOV architectures. It addresses how PCIe maintains backward compatibility with legacy PCI software while utilizing in-band signaling and defining mandatory implementation standards for various device types.

children.title: Section 6.1.4 MSI and MSI-X Operation
children.description: This section details PCI Express legacy INTx emulation, explaining how Assert/Deassert Message packets function as virtual wires to replace physical interrupt pins. It addresses how PCIe devices maintain software-level backward compatibility, manage virtual-to-system interrupt mapping via the Root Complex, and utilize standard PCI Configuration Space registers.

children.title: Section 6.1.4.1 MSI Configuration
children.description: This section details PCIe interrupt requirements, mandating support for MSI or MSI-X protocols and specifying edge-triggered semantics over legacy level-triggered INTx. It clarifies technical implementation constraints, including Per-Vector Masking (PVM), 64-bit Message Address requirements for PCIe Endpoints, and strictly defined Transaction Descriptor attribute settings for memory-write interrupt transactions.

children.title: Section 6.1.4.2 MSI-X Configuration
children.description: This section outlines MSI-X configuration requirements, covering power-of-two vector alignment, per-vector masking, and 64-bit versus 32-bit message address initialization. It establishes technical protocols for programming the Message Address, Message Upper Address, and Message Data registers, specifically addressing how system software manages Extended Message Data capabilities.

children.title: Section 6.1.4.3 Enabling Operation
children.description: Section 6.1.4.3 outlines operational requirements for MSI and MSI-X configuration, covering bit preservation, vector masking, and the mutual exclusivity of interrupt mechanisms. This section details how software enables interrupts, handles backward compatibility, and manages potential conflicts when configuring MSI-X table entries.

children.title: Section 6.1.4.5 Per-vector Masking and Function Masking
children.description: This section outlines PCIe MSI/MSI-X message generation requirements, covering Message Control registers, Extended Message Data, and multi-vector formatting. It details how Functions construct Memory Writes and modify data bits to support vector-based interrupt signaling.

children.title: Section 6.1.4.6 Hardware/Software Synchronization
children.description: This section outlines synchronization requirements for MSI-X cached table entries, specifying that hardware must update masked entries upon unmasking to ensure proper interrupt delivery. It addresses critical questions regarding interrupt message persistence, vector-specific acknowledgement protocols, and the necessity of driver handshakes for reliable processing of multiple identical versus unique MSI-X vectors.

children.title: Section 6.1.4.7 Message Transaction Reception and Ordering Requirements
children.description: This section mandates adherence to PCIe transaction ordering rules, ensuring that MSI/MSI-X Posted Requests maintain strictly ordered delivery relative to preceding data writes to guarantee interrupt service routine (ISR) data consistency. It clarifies that interrupt receivers must immediately complete interrupt message transactions independently of CPU servicing, addressing how hardware architecture ensures sequential integrity and memory-mapped update visibility for device drivers.

children.title: Section 6.1.6 Native PME Software Model
children.description: This section explains the PCIe transaction ordering rules for MSI/MSI-X Posted Requests, ensuring data consistency between device updates and interrupt service routine (ISR) execution. It specifies technical requirements for interrupt receivers to process messages independently of CPU scheduling to maintain architectural compliance and system reliability.

children.title: Section 6.1.8 Operating System Power Management Notification
children.description: This section details the ACPI-defined protocols for Operating System Power Management (OSPM) notifications, covering device-specific interrupts, General Purpose Events (GPEs), and hardware-initiated wake events. It explains how the firmware communicates power state transitions and device status changes to the OS kernel through the ACPI namespace and SCI (System Control Interrupt) reporting mechanisms.

children.title: Section 6.1.9 PME Routing Between PCI Express and PCI Hierarchies
children.description: This section details the mechanism for propagating PME (Power Management Event) messages between PCI Express and PCI hierarchies using MSI/MSI-X signaling via the Root Port’s Root Status and Control Registers. It addresses how hardware triggers interrupt transitions, how the software handler identifies PME Requesters, and how Root Complex Event Collectors or legacy system-specific mechanisms manage these power events.

children.title: Section 6.2 Error Signaling and Logging
children.description: Section 6.2 details the protocols for error signaling and logging within PCI Express architectures, covering classification, hardware-specific reporting sequences, and error control mechanisms. This section defines the technical requirements for internal error handling, Downstream Port Containment (DPC), and SR-IOV baseline compliance to ensure system reliability and diagnostic traceability.

children.title: Section 6.2.2 Error Classification
children.description: Section 6.2.2 defines the framework for PCI Express error classification, signaling, and logging requirements, specifically addressing how errors associated with Ports and Functions are identified and reported. It details technical protocols for PME Requester ID handling, Root Complex Event Collector functionality, and the scope of permissible error tracking for PCI Express-related component interfaces.

children.title: Section 6.2.2.1 Correctable Errors
children.description: Section 6.2.2.1 defines "Correctable Errors" within the PCI Express error classification framework, detailing how hardware-level recovery mechanisms handle issues like LCRC errors without software intervention. It clarifies the distinction between Fatal, Uncorrectable, and Correctable categories while explaining how platform designers map these classifications to system responses and link integrity monitoring.

children.title: Section 6.2.2.2 Uncorrectable Errors
children.description: This section outlines the classification and handling protocols for PCIe uncorrectable errors, distinguishing between fatal and non-fatal error severity. It addresses technical requirements for error reporting, containment mechanisms, and system recovery procedures for hardware interface failures.

children.title: Section 6.2.2.2.2 Non-Fatal Errors
children.description: This section defines PCIe error classification, distinguishing between Fatal and Non-fatal uncorrectable errors to enable system recovery without Link resets. It details how agents report these errors using Completion Status, in-band error Messages, and associated recovery mechanisms for robust fault isolation.

children.title: Section 6.2.3 Error Signaling
children.description: This section defines PCI-related error signaling, distinguishing between fatal and non-fatal uncorrectable errors to determine necessity for link resets. It explains error reporting mechanisms, including completion status, in-band messages, and hardware signaling, to answer how interfaces handle and isolate functional faults.

children.title: Section 6.2.3.2 Error Messages
children.description: This section details error reporting mechanisms for PCI Express, distinguishing between Completions for non-posted requests and Error Messages routed to Root Ports or Root Complex Event Collectors. It addresses how hardware handles error severity, Requester ID association, and the merging of identical error messages to manage fault signaling throughout the PCIe hierarchy.

children.title: Section 6.2.3.2.1 Uncorrectable Error Severity Programming (Advanced Error Reporting)
children.description: This section details the **Uncorrectable Error Severity** register within the **PCIe Advanced Error Reporting (AER)** capability, focusing on configuring error bits as **Fatal** or **Non-Fatal**. It defines how system software manages hardware fault isolation and determines the appropriate recovery or system reset response for uncorrectable PCIe errors.

children.title: Section 6.2.3.2.2 Masking Individual Errors
children.description: This section details PCI Express Advanced Error Reporting (AER) mechanisms, specifically focusing on the `Uncorrectable Error Severity`, `Uncorrectable Error Mask`, and `Correctable Error Mask` registers. It explains how device drivers and platform software use these registers to customize error classification and selectively enable or suppress reporting messages for specific link or device failures.

children.title: Section 6.2.3.2.4 Advisory Non-Fatal Error Cases
children.description: This section defines the hierarchy and suppression logic for preventing "error pollution" when multiple PCIe layers detect faults for a single transaction. It specifies reporting precedence for uncorrectable errors, distinguishes between packet-associated faults and standalone internal errors, and establishes guidelines for isolating the root cause by limiting redundant reports.

children.title: Section 6.2.3.2.4.1 Completer Sending a Completion with UR/CA Status
children.description: This section details the mandatory and optional header fields for PCIe Completer-generated Completion packets signaling Unsupported Request (UR) or Completer Abort (CA) status. It addresses constraints on Completer Behavior (CB) and requester-side handling, specifically governing Payload presence, Length, and Completion Status bit definitions during error scenarios.

children.title: Section 6.2.3.2.4.2 Intermediate Receiver
children.description: This section defines Advisory Non-Fatal Error handling for PCIe agents with Advanced Error Reporting (AER), detailing when to promote ERR_COR messaging over ERR_NONFATAL. It clarifies error escalation procedures, TLP multi-error reporting strategies, and distinguishes AER-managed mechanisms from Root Port PIO error handling.

children.title: Section 6.2.3.2.4.3 Ultimate PCI Express Receiver of a Poisoned TLP or IDE TLP with PCRC Check Failed
children.description: This section defines the error-handling protocols for an Ultimate PCI Express Receiver when encountering a Poisoned TLP or a TLP with a failed PCRC check in an IDE stream. It details mandatory hardware reporting mechanisms, TLP handling behaviors, and the specific status bits utilized to signal link-level integrity violations.

children.title: Section 6.2.3.2.4.4 Requester with Completion Timeout
children.description: This section specifies error handling requirements for Root Complexes and Receivers encountering Poisoned TLPs or IDE PCRC failures. It defines mandatory signaling behaviors, such as ERR_NONFATAL or Advisory Non-Fatal Error handling, and clarifies conditions where internal fabric limitations or specific error severities necessitate dropping transactions and reporting errors to the PCI Express hierarchy.

children.title: Section 6.2.3.2.4.5 Receiver of an Unexpected Completion
children.description: This section defines PCIe protocol requirements for handling Unexpected Completions, specifically detailing transaction header processing, error reporting via the Error Message mechanism, and associated Advisory Non-Fatal Error handling. It addresses how a Receiver must respond when processing a Completer ID or Tag that does not correlate to an outstanding request.

children.title: Section 6.2.3.2.5 Requester Receiving a Completion with UR/CA Status
children.description: This section outlines PCI Express error handling protocols for Requesters receiving Completion with Unsupported Request (UR) or Completer Abort (CA) status, alongside procedures for managing unexpected Completions. It addresses critical questions regarding error recovery requirements, Advisory Non-Fatal Error reporting, and the behavioral expectations for AER-enabled versus non-AER devices.

children.title: Section 6.2.3.3 Error Forwarding (Data Poisoning)
children.description: This section defines error forwarding vulnerabilities occurring when malicious data inputs compromise downstream system components through improper sanitization. It addresses how data poisoning exploits error-handling pipelines and answers whether specific integration points facilitate the secondary injection of unauthorized payloads.

children.title: Section 6.2.3.4 Optional Error Checking
children.description: This section outlines protocols for handling Unexpected Completion errors and Requester-side error recovery in PCIe, specifically addressing Advisory Non-Fatal Error status, AER (Advanced Error Reporting) mechanisms, and the requirements for reporting Completion with UR/CA status. It explains how Requesters and Receivers coordinate error handling to ensure system stability while offloading specific recovery actions to device-specific mechanisms.

children.title: Section 6.2.4 Error Logging
children.description: This section defines error logging protocols for PCIe devices, detailing the use of Device Status registers and Advanced Error Reporting (AER) registers to record specific error events and TLP headers. It addresses how non-Function-specific errors are propagated across Multi-Function, SR-IOV, and SIOV devices.

children.title: Section 6.2.4.1 Root Complex Considerations (Advanced Error Reporting)
children.description: This section defines Root Complex (RC) requirements for Advanced Error Reporting (AER), focusing on error source identification mechanisms, interrupt generation protocols, and Extended Configuration Space (ECS) handling. It addresses how RCs detect, categorize, and report PCIe link errors to system software to ensure robust fault management.

children.title: Section 6.2.4.1.2 Interrupt Generation
children.description: This section outlines how Root Ports and Root Complex Event Collectors utilize the Advanced Error Reporting (AER) Extended Capability to implement the Error Source Identification and Root Error Status registers. It addresses how PCIe systems identify error originators, manage message transmission requirements, and configure reporting via the Root Error Command, Device Control, and Command registers.

children.title: Section 6.2.4.1.3 RC ECS Handling
children.description: This section details the **RC ECS Handling** mechanism, which allows **Root Ports (RP)** and **RCECs** to independently route **ERR_COR** error subclasses to either **System Firmware (SFW)** or the **OS**. It defines configuration enable bits for error subclass signaling and outlines the specific **AER registers** and status bits used to manage concurrent, independent error reporting.

children.title: Section 6.2.4.2 Multiple Error Handling (Advanced Error Reporting Extended Capability)
children.description: This section details the configuration of **RC ECS Handling** within **Advanced Error Reporting (AER)**, specifically explaining how **Root Ports (RP)** and **RCECs** independently route **ERR_COR** messages to either **System Firmware (SFW)** or the **Operating System (OS)**. It defines the enable bits and status registers required for concurrent error handling, addressing how system architects can manage discrete error-reporting paths for different **ECS (Error Correction Signaling) subclasses**.

children.title: Section 6.2.4.2.1 Multiple Error Handling in VFs
children.description: This section details requirements for Header Log space management and overflow handling when multiple Virtual Functions (VFs) share resources for Advanced Error Reporting (AER). It specifies how the First Error Pointer functions, defines the "all 1s" return mechanism for log overflows, and provides implementation guidance for maintaining log integrity during concurrent uncorrectable error events.

children.title: Section 6.2.4.3 Advisory Non-Fatal Error Logging
children.description: This section details the mechanism of PCIe Advisory Non-Fatal Error (ANFE) logging, focusing on the `Advanced Error Reporting (AER)` capability, `Error Status` registers, and the `Advisory Non-Fatal Error Mask` bit. It defines how compliant components handle reported errors versus those relegated to advisory status and clarifies when an error should be logged despite not triggering a fatal system event.

children.title: Section 6.2.4.4 End-End TLP Prefix Logging - Non-Flit Mode
children.description: This section defines the error reporting logic for PCIe Advisory Non-Fatal Error cases, specifically detailing the interaction between AER, uncorrectable error severity, and TLP header log locking mechanisms. It addresses how devices determine whether to signal errors via ERR_COR rather than ERR_NONFATAL and enforces strict logging protocols for VFs sharing header registers.

children.title: Section 6.2.5 Sequence of Device Error Signaling and Logging Operations
children.description: This section details the hierarchical sequence for signaling and logging PCIe device errors, integrating specific protocols for Correctable, Uncorrectable, and Advanced Error Reporting (AER). It addresses procedural questions regarding how hardware registers, status bits, and configuration masks determine error severity, logging requirements for TLP prefix overflows, and the conditional triggering of DPC and ERR messages.

children.title: Section 6.2.6 Error Message Controls
children.description: This section details the hierarchy and logic flow of PCI Express error message handling, covering control/status bits, AER reporting, and interrupt generation. It explains how device-level error conditions (ERR_COR, ERR_NONFATAL, ERR_FATAL) are logged, forwarded by bridges, and translated into system-level notifications via Root Ports and the Advanced Error Reporting (AER) framework.

children.title: Section 6.2.7 Error Listing and Rules
children.description: Section 6.2.7 facilitates systematic PCI Express fault management by defining error severity, hardware detection mechanisms, and required reporting actions. This section addresses how PCI Express agents handle corrected and uncorrectable internal errors, distinguishes between programmable Advanced Error Reporting (AER) configurations and non-reprogrammable defaults, and clarifies the relationship between error logging and triggered Downstream Port Containment (DPC).

children.title: Section 6.2.7.1 Conventional PCI Mapping
children.description: This section details the mandatory mapping of PCI Express error conditions to legacy PCI Status and Command registers to ensure compatibility with traditional driver software. It clarifies the operational independence between PCIe Uncorrectable/Correctable Error registers and legacy PCI registers, specifically addressing how error reporting synchronization functions across Virtual PCI Bridge configuration headers.

children.title: Section 6.2.8 Virtual PCI Bridge Error Handling
children.description: This section details the mapping of PCI Express error conditions to legacy PCI Status register bits within Virtual PCI Bridges to ensure compatibility with conventional PCI drivers. It clarifies the independence of PCI Express Error Status registers from legacy PCI Command and Status registers, specifically addressing how unsupported requests are reported and managed in a backward-compatible framework.

children.title: Section 6.2.8.1 Error Message Forwarding and PCI Mapping for Bridge - Rules
children.description: This section specifies PCI Express bridge behavior for Unsupported Requests and TLP poisoning, detailing status register updates during upstream and downstream forwarding. It answers how bridges process malformed packets and when to set error bits in the Primary and Secondary Status registers.

children.title: Section 6.2.9 SR-IOV Baseline Error Handling
children.description: This section defines error handling protocols for SR-IOV Virtual PCI Bridges, specifically addressing TLP routing, Unsupported Request (UR) termination, and status register updates for Poisoned TLPs. It provides technical requirements for managing data parity errors and forwarded completions when internal bridge mapping rules are triggered.

children.title: Section 6.2.10 Internal Errors
children.description: This section defines configuration register requirements and status reporting mechanisms for Virtual Functions (VFs) to ensure PCI Express error isolation. It specifies mandatory error-handling status bits and classifies Internal Errors, addressing how VFs independently manage and signal component-level faults.

children.title: Section 6.2.11 Downstream Port Containment (DPC)
children.description: This section details Downstream Port Containment (DPC), covering header logging for Uncorrectable Internal Errors and the mechanism for halting PCIe traffic below a Downstream Port to prevent data corruption. It defines the reporting requirements for internal errors and explains how software enables this optional capability via the DPC Extended Capability structure and DPC Trigger Enable field.

children.title: Section 6.2.11.2 DPC ERR_COR Signaling
children.description: This section specifies the DPC (Downstream Port Containment) interrupt signaling requirements for PCI Express Ports, covering configurations for both level-triggered (INTx) and edge-triggered (MSI/MSI-X) mechanisms. It details the operational prerequisites, status bit registers, and conditions necessary for a Port to successfully generate and transition DPC ERR_COR error alerts.

children.title: Section 6.2.11.3 Root Port Programmed I/O (RP PIO) Error Controls
children.description: This section details Root Port Programmed I/O (RP PIO) error controls, specifically governing DPC trigger events, error signaling order (ERR_COR, MSI/MSI-X), and granular tracking of Unsupported Requests, Completer Aborts, and Completion Timeouts. It defines how Root Ports manage and report non-posted request errors across Configuration, I/O, and Memory cycles to ensure precise fault handling and protocol adherence.

children.title: Section 6.2.11.5 DL_Active ERR_COR Signaling
children.description: This section defines the hierarchical handling of DL_Active ERR_COR signaling by categorizing AER-managed errors into uncorrectable, Advisory Non-Fatal, or masked states to determine if DPC is triggered. It specifies the functional relationship between RP PIO SysError/Exception bits and DPC, while outlining the requirements for software-triggered DPC via the DPC Control and Status registers.

children.title: Section 6.3 Virtual Channel Support
children.description: This section covers Virtual Channel (VC) architecture, including Traffic Class (TC) mapping, arbitration algorithms, isochronous data support, and SVC/MFVC interoperability. It addresses how PCIe traffic is prioritized, scheduled across multiple logical queues, and managed to maintain strict Quality of Service (QoS) requirements.

children.title: Section 6.3.1 Introduction and Scope
children.description: This section defines the mandatory signaling requirements for `DL_ACTIVE` `ERR_COR` messages, specifically addressing conditions for the DPC `SIG_SFW` status bit and message ordering protocols involving MSI/MSI-X. It clarifies the operational relationship between Link state transitions, PCI Express error reporting interfaces, and the differentiation from standard Data Link Layer State Changed interrupts.

children.title: Section 6.3.2 TC/VC Mapping and Example Usage
children.description: This section outlines the mechanism for mapping Traffic Classes (TCs) to Virtual Channels (VCs) within a PCIe fabric, emphasizing the mandatory TC0/VC0 "hardwired" requirement and optional software-defined configurations. It defines the implementation of VC resources, addressing how system software manages TC/VC associations across diverse components, hot-plug scenarios, and varied fabric topologies.

children.title: Section 6.3.3 VC Arbitration
children.description: This section outlines PCIe Virtual Channel (VC) arbitration, detailing TC-to-VC mappings, link bidirectional traffic flow, and configuration objectives. It explains how to manage traffic prioritization and resource allocation to ensure guaranteed bandwidth, deterministic latency, and forward progress across fabric bottlenecks.

children.title: Section 6.3.3.1 Traffic Flow and Switch Arbitration Model
children.description: This section outlines PCI Express switch architecture, detailing traffic flow mechanisms, Virtual Channel (VC) prioritization, and internal arbitration policies. It specifies how ingress-to-egress routing models and data scheduling infrastructure manage differentiated packet streams within multi-port PCIe switch fabrics.

children.title: Section 6.3.3.2 VC Arbitration - Arbitration Between VCs
children.description: This section details PCIe switch packet routing through two-stage arbitration: Port Arbitration between ingress sources at a specific Virtual Channel (VC) and VC Arbitration for egress link access. It addresses how TLP headers and TC/VC mapping facilitate resource contention management and egress traffic multiplexing.

children.title: Section 6.3.3.2.1 Strict Priority Arbitration Model
children.description: This section details the Strict Priority Arbitration Model for network traffic scheduling, focusing on priority-level determination, queue-servicing protocols, and latency bounds. It addresses how the scheduler evaluates packet precedence and maintains deterministic performance under congestion.

children.title: Section 6.3.3.2.2 Round Robin Arbitration Model
children.description: This section details the **Round Robin Arbitration Model** for **Virtual Channel (VC) resource management**, covering **Strict Priority**, **Round Robin (RR)**, and **Weighted Round Robin (WRR)** algorithms. It defines how **Port VC capability structures** prioritize traffic and how hardware groups VCs to manage **Quality of Service (QoS)** through configurable priority tiers and weighting factors.

children.title: Section 6.3.3.3 Port Arbitration - Arbitration Within VC
children.description: This section defines the mechanisms for **Port Arbitration** and **Virtual Channel (VC) bandwidth allocation** within fabric interconnects. It addresses how hardware-level arbiter logic manages **service priority**, **weighted round-robin scheduling**, and **flow control credits** to resolve contention between multiple traffic streams sharing a single physical port.

children.title: Section 6.3.3.4 Multi-Function Devices and Function Arbitration
children.description: This section details Weighted Round Robin (WRR) arbitration for multi-function devices, focusing on Quality of Service (QoS), bandwidth allocation, and latency management. It defines port arbitration mechanisms for switches, root ports, and RCRBs while specifying requirements for programmable hardware configurations.

children.title: Section 6.3.4 Isochronous Support
children.description: This section defines the architectural requirements and traffic management protocols for PCIe isochronous support, covering configuration, requester/completer behavior, and switch handling. It answers how systems maintain Quality of Service (QoS) and latency guarantees for time-sensitive data transfers across multi-function devices and complex topologies.

children.title: Section 6.3.4.1 Rules for Software Configuration
children.description: This section defines software configuration requirements for PCI Express isochronous data transfer, specifically covering TC transaction labeling, VC data-transfer protocols, and TC-to-VC mapping. It answers how system software must configure fabric resources to ensure guaranteed bandwidth, deterministic latency, and end-to-end quality-of-service for isochronous traffic.

children.title: Section 6.3.4.4 Rules for Switches and Root Complexes
children.description: Section 6.3.4.4 mandates strict packet forwarding behavior for PCIe Switches and Root Complexes, focusing on Address Routing, ID Routing, and Message Routing protocols. This section defines essential traffic handling rules, compliance requirements for TLP (Transaction Layer Packet) routing, and specific procedures for managing internal routing tables and port-to-port path selection.

children.title: Section 6.3.4.5 Rules for Multi-Function Devices
children.description: This section establishes compliance requirements for PCIe multi-function and endpoint devices handling isochronous traffic, specifically focusing on `Max_Payload_Size` constraints, `No Snoop` TLP attributes, and Root Complex/Switch bandwidth arbitration. It defines the operational mandates for Requesters and Completers regarding performance, latency, and flow control to ensure deterministic data transfer.

children.title: Section 6.3.5 SVC and VC/MFVC Capability Coexistence
children.description: This section details the architectural coexistence and mutual exclusivity of Streamlined Virtual Channel (SVC), Virtual Channel (VC), and Multi-Function Virtual Channel (MFVC) capabilities within PCIe ports. It defines initialization requirements for VC0, clarifies the necessity of time-based Function Arbitration for isochronous upstream traffic, and specifies software configuration procedures for transitioning between SVC and VC/MFVC modes.

children.title: Section 6.4 Device Synchronization
children.description: This section details the MFVC Extended Capability requirements for time-based Function Arbitration and isochronous traffic support in Multi-Function Devices. It clarifies the configuration hierarchy, interoperability, and mutual exclusivity between SVC and VC/MFVC capabilities during link initialization.

children.title: Section 6.5 Locked Transactions
children.description: Section 6.5 details the architectural requirements for supporting atomic "Locked Transaction" sequences across the PCI Express fabric, including propagation rules for Switches, Bridges, and the Root Complex. This section defines the specific compliance mandates and operational constraints for Legacy Endpoints versus PCI Express Endpoints to ensure legacy compatibility and signal atomic memory access.

children.title: Section 6.5.1 Introduction
children.description: This section defines mechanisms for managing PCIe transaction completion and synchronization, specifically addressing the **Transactions Pending bit**, **flushing protocols** for posted writes across different Traffic Classes (TCs), and **ordering rules** (Relaxed/ID-Based). It explains how system software determines if a Function is expecting completions and identifies requirements for ensuring device-to-device memory consistency.

children.title: Section 6.5.2 Initiation and Propagation of Locked Transactions - Rules
children.description: This section defines the architectural protocols for initiating and propagating locked transactions, focusing on atomicity, consensus mechanisms, and cross-shard communication. It addresses how distributed systems validate transaction states and handle message locking to ensure consistency during synchronization.

children.title: Section 6.5.3 Switches and Lock - Rules
children.description: This section details protocol requirements for PCI Express locked transaction sequences, including MRdLk/CplDLk signaling, mandatory Unlock Message termination, and TC0 traffic constraints. It defines how Switches and Bridges maintain exclusive paths between the Root Complex and Legacy Endpoints while preventing traffic congestion via hierarchy-wide lock limitations.

children.title: Section 6.5.4 PCI Express/PCI Bridges and Lock - Rules
children.description: This section defines the mandatory protocols for managing atomic lock operations and resource access across PCI Express-to-PCI/PCI-X bridges. It clarifies bridge behavior regarding Legacy Lock signaling, write posting requirements, and transaction ordering rules to ensure architectural compatibility between PCIe and legacy PCI fabrics.

children.title: Section 6.5.5 Root Complex and Lock - Rules
Section 6.5.6 Legacy Endpoints
children.description: This section details mandatory PCIe switch traffic management during legacy Locked Memory Read (MRdLk) transactions, specifically focusing on VC0 blocking rules, port isolation protocols, and Unlock Message propagation. It addresses how switches maintain lock atomicity, prevent system deadlocks, and handle undefined behaviors during split-lock operations.

children.title: Section 6.5.7 PCI Express Endpoints
children.description: This section defines PCI Express endpoint handling of locked memory requests and establishes mandatory reset architectures, specifically distinguishing between Conventional Resets and Fundamental Resets. It addresses technical requirements for the PERST# auxiliary signal and specifies how components must initialize port states to comply with the PCIe specification.

children.title: Section 6.6 PCI Express Reset - Rules
children.description: This section details PCI Express reset mechanisms, specifically covering Fundamental Reset, Conventional Reset, and Function Level Reset (FLR) protocols. These rules define how components transition through power states, handle configuration space initialization, and recover from functional errors during system resets.

children.title: Section 6.6.1 Conventional Reset
children.description: Section 6.6.1 defines PCI Express Conventional Reset, distinguishing between Fundamental Reset (mandating the PERST# signal) and non-fundamental reset mechanisms. It addresses how components return ports to initial states, operational requirements for auxiliary signals, and constraints regarding MRdLk Request handling.

children.title: Section 6.6.2 Function Level Reset (FLR)
children.description: This section defines mandatory power-on and reference clock timing parameters for PCIe devices, including *Tpvperl*, *Tperst*, and *Tperstslew* requirements. It further details the Function Level Reset (FLR) mechanism and its utility in quiescing hardware during software faults or partition migrations to prevent data corruption.

children.title: Section 6.7 PCI Express Native Hot-Plug
children.description: This section details PCI Express native hot-plug mechanisms, covering Request Retry Status (RRS) handling during Function Level Resets (FLR), Unexpected Completion management, and the architectural support for orderly versus asynchronous component removal. It answers how systems maintain configuration stability during hardware transitions and specifies the protocols for device initialization and error handling during hot-add and hot-removal events.

children.title: Section 6.7.1 Elements of Hot-Plug
children.description: This section details the mechanical and logical components required for PCIe hot-plug functionality, covering indicators, MRL sensors, electromechanical interlocks, power controllers, and software interfaces. It addresses how hardware state changes are detected, how devices are safely isolated during removal, and how user-initiated slot management is executed.

children.title: Section 6.7.1.1 Indicators
children.description: This section defines technical requirements for hot-plug physical elements, including MRL sensors, power controllers, and indicators, to guide form factor specifications. It addresses which components are mandatory for hot-plug support and how system software interacts with hardware to manage slot power, adapter retention, and user request interfaces.

children.title: Section 6.7.1.1.2 Power Indicator
children.description: This section details the functional specifications for the "Attention Indicator," covering its visual status states (Off, On, Blinking) and their corresponding meanings regarding operational health and locate-mode identification. It specifically addresses how system software leverages this indicator to signal hardware or driver malfunctions versus user-initiated identification requests.

children.title: Section 6.7.1.2 Manually-operated Retention Latch (MRL)
children.description: This section defines the mechanical specifications, engagement requirements, and manual release procedures for Manually-operated Retention Latches (MRL). It details structural locking mechanisms, verification protocols, and safety constraints to address functional integration and secure equipment deployment.

children.title: Section 6.7.1.3 MRL Sensor
children.description: This section defines the operational states of a Peripheral Component Interconnect (PCI) MRL Sensor and Power Indicator during hot-plugging procedures. It details how indicator states (Off, On, Blinking) govern safe adapter removal, signal state management (Vaux), and user interaction protocols for form factors like CEM.

children.title: Section 6.7.1.6 Software User Interface
children.description: This section specifies requirements for PCIe hot-plug electromechanical interlocks, including persistent state maintenance, status reporting via the Slot Status register, timing constraints, and the operational integration of user-accessible Attention Buttons. It addresses critical questions regarding hardware-software synchronization, interlock power-loss behaviors, and the functional protocols for initiating hot-add or hot-remove procedures.

children.title: Section 6.7.1.8 Power Controller
children.description: This section defines the functionality of the Power Controller and the Physical Slot Identifier within hot-plug operations. It clarifies requirements for slot identification, power state management, fault monitoring, and behavior when a power controller is absent.

children.title: Section 6.7.2 Registers Grouped by Hot-Plug Element Association
children.description: This section details the hardware register interfaces for PCIe hot-plug components, including attention buttons, power controllers, indicators, sensors, and interlock mechanisms. It specifies control and status bit definitions, interrupt management, and slot-level capabilities necessary for managing device insertion and removal events within a PCIe hierarchy.

children.title: Section 6.7.2.4 Power Controller Registers
children.description: This section defines PCI Express Power Controller registers and hot-plug signaling requirements, specifically detailing the 1-second power-removal wait mandate and associated control registers. It identifies key technical terms including Slot Capabilities, Slot Control, and Status registers, while specifying how software manages Attention Button and Indicator states for hot-plug capable slots.

children.title: Section 6.7.2.7 Electromechanical Interlock Registers
children.description: This section defines the configuration and operational logic of Electromechanical Interlock Registers within system control hierarchies. It details memory-mapped register structures, safety-critical interlocking protocols, and synchronization requirements to resolve system-level access conflicts.

children.title: Section 6.7.2.8 Command Completed Registers
children.description: This section details PCIe slot management registers governing Presence Detect (PD) and MRL Sensor mechanisms, including capabilities, control bits, and status reporting. It addresses how systems track physical adapter insertion, MRL sensor state changes, and the configuration of in-band versus out-of-band presence detection reporting.

children.title: Section 6.7.2.9 Port Capabilities and Slot Information Registers
children.description: This section defines the PCIe Port Capabilities and Slot Information registers, detailing fields for physical slot power limits, hot-plug mechanisms, and attention indicators. It addresses how software queries a port’s hardware characteristics and physical slot configuration to manage adapter power and status functionality.

children.title: Section 6.7.2.10 Hot-Plug Interrupt Control Register
children.description: This section defines the configuration and control mechanisms for PCIe hot-plug operations, detailing status, enable, and capability fields for slot, command-completed, and Data Link Layer state events. It identifies how hardware-initialized registers—including Slot Implemented, Physical Slot Number, and Hot-Plug Interrupt Enable—govern system responses to asynchronous device insertion, removal, and attention button requests.

children.title: Section 6.7.3 PCI Express Hot-Plug Events
children.description: This section details PCI Express Hot-Plug event signaling, covering mechanism-specific triggers including Slot Status updates, Command Completion interrupts, and Data Link Layer state transitions. It defines the technical requirements for hardware-to-software notification processes, addressing how systems identify, track, and manage dynamic device additions and removals.

children.title: Section 6.7.3.1 Slot Events
children.description: This section details the PCIe slot-based hardware configuration, focusing on physical slot identification, hot-plug capabilities, and the interrupt management of asynchronous slot events. It answers how a system identifies physical ports, enables hot-plug support, and manages hardware-to-software event reporting for states like presence detection and power faults.

children.title: Section 6.7.3.3 Data Link Layer State Changed Events
children.description: This section defines PCIe hot-plug command execution protocols, including synchronization requirements for the Slot Control register and the mandatory 1-second completion threshold. It specifies how Downstream Ports handle command processing, event reporting via the Command Completed status, and operational constraints for hardware lacking command completion support.

children.title: Section 6.7.3.4 Software Notification of Hot-Plug Events
children.description: This section details the hardware and software configuration requirements for PCIe hot-plug interrupt signaling, defining the operational logic for level-triggered (INTx) and edge-triggered (MSI/MSI-X) notification mechanisms. It specifies the necessary Slot Control and Status register bit states required to enable interrupt generation and resolves how software is notified of hot-plug events within a Function.

children.title: Section 6.7.4 System Firmware Intermediary (SFI) Support
children.description: This section defines System Firmware Intermediary (SFI) protocols for mediated PCI Express device management, covering mechanisms such as Downstream Port Filtering (DPF), Quarantine Mode, and In-Band capability hiding. It addresses how firmware intercepts and regulates hardware events, error signaling, readiness notifications, and hot-plug surprise behavior to maintain system integrity.

children.title: Section 6.7.4.2 SFI Downstream Port Filtering (DPF)
children.description: This section defines the System Firmware Intermediary (SFI) and Enhanced SFI (eSFI) capability requirements for PCIe Downstream Ports, detailing prerequisite Link and Device Capability bit settings and mandatory ERR_COR signaling. It addresses questions regarding SFI hardware/firmware dependencies, the distinctions between standard SFI and eSFI, and limitations concerning interrupt generation and Root Port configurations.

children.title: Section 6.7.4.3 SFI CAM
children.description: This section defines Downstream Port Containment (DPF) filtering modes, specifically the SFI CAM mechanism’s role in regulating TLP traffic and facilitating firmware-first operations. It addresses how PCIe ports manage asynchronous hot-add scenarios and prevent undefined hardware behavior during post-reset bus scans by selectively blocking or allowing Configuration Requests and MCTP VDMs.

children.title: Section 6.7.4.4 SFI Interactions with Readiness Notifications
children.description: This section defines SFI Extended Capability mechanisms for masking and emulating Device Readiness Status (DRS) messages during Downstream Port Filtering. It establishes mandatory register bit behavior for DRS state reporting and provides implementation guidance for the serialized synchronization of SFI CAM Address and Data registers.

children.title: Section 6.7.4.5 SFI Suppression of Hot-Plug Surprise Functionality
children.description: This section defines the use of the SFI HPS Suppress bit to disable Hot-Plug Surprise (HPS) functionality in favor of Downstream Port Containment (DPC). It additionally details how system firmware utilizes SFI DPF and DRS Trigger mechanisms to manage device readiness, bridge configuration delays, and control operating system visibility during hot-add operations.

children.title: Section 6.7.4.6 SFI Quarantine Mode
children.description: This section defines SFI Quarantine Mode and the associated SFI Quarantine bit, detailing how BMCs can isolate eSFI-capable PCIe ports by forcing the LTSSM to a Disabled state and masking presence detect signals. It specifies functional triggers for port isolation—including hot removal and DL_Down status—to mitigate security risks and manage hardware resets when presence detect states deassert.

children.title: Section 6.7.4.7 SFI Extended Capability In-Band Hiding
children.description: This section details the SFI Extended Capability In-Band Hiding mechanism, focusing on steganographic data concealment protocols, sub-frame multiplexing, and header-field obfuscation. It addresses how stealth communication channels are established within standard data packet architectures without violating integrity constraints.

children.title: Section 6.7.4.8 SFI Extended Capability In-Band Read-Only
children.description: This section defines access constraints for the SFI Extended Capability structure, specifically detailing "SFI Hidden In-Band" and "SFI RO In-Band" bits to manage visibility for in-band versus out-of-band management traffic. It addresses how to secure configuration space against host-based system software exploits while ensuring BMCs retain administrative control via PCIe-MI or out-of-band protocols.

children.title: Section 6.7.6 Async Removal
children.description: Section 6.7.6 defines the handling of SFI CAM Data access based on the **SFI RO In-Band** bit and clarifies the interplay between platform **ACPI firmware** and **OS-native hot-plug** control. It addresses how configuration requests are processed when **Async removal** occurs and specifies the requirements for transferring **PCIe hot-plug** management between firmware and the operating system.

children.title: Section 6.8 Power Budgeting Mechanism
children.description: This section details the **PCI Express asynchronous removal** process, addressing error handling for **Correctable Errors**, **Completion Timeouts**, and **DL_Active to DL_Down** state transitions. It defines the mechanism for managing expected system side effects when a hardware adapter is removed unexpectedly before operating system quiescence.

children.title: Section 6.8.1 System Power Budgeting Process Recommendations
children.description: This section outlines systematic power budgeting methodologies, covering technical parameters like peak-load allocation, thermal dissipation constraints, and power conversion efficiency. It addresses how to establish a robust power hierarchy, estimate component-level consumption, and mitigate potential system-wide energy bottlenecks.

children.title: Section 6.8.3 Power Limit Mechanisms
children.description: This section details the power management infrastructure for PCI Express systems, covering responsibilities between system firmware and the power budget manager, including the use of the Power Budgeting Extended Capability register and the System Allocated bit. It defines how total power budgets are calculated and communicated to ensure hot-plugged devices and system board components remain within defined operational limits.

children.title: Section 6.9 Slot Power Limit Control
children.description: This section details the PCIe Slot Power Limit Control mechanism, defining how software utilizes the Slot Capabilities and Device Capabilities registers to manage power consumption via Set_Slot_Power_Limit messages. It addresses how platform firmware enforces hardware limits based on thermal and power delivery constraints for adapters connected to Downstream Ports.

children.title: Section 6.10 Root Complex Topology Discovery
children.description: This section defines Root Complex topologies, contrasting opaque configurations with those featuring Root Complex Register Blocks (RCRBs) for internal traffic management and Virtual Channel control. It addresses how PCIe software identifies system architecture and manages power scaling for adapters exceeding slot power limits.

children.title: Section 6.12 Access Control Services (ACS)
children.description: This section governs PCIe Link speed management by coordinating LTSSM operations with software-based control registers. Using technical terms like **Target Link Speed**, **Hardware Autonomous Speed Disable**, and **Link reliability**, it defines the conditions under which components autonomously shift operational speeds or negotiate speed caps during training.

children.title: Section 6.12.1 ACS Component Capability Requirements
children.description: This section details Access Control Services (ACS) capability requirements for PCIe components, covering downstream port configurations, multi-function device virtualization (SR-IOV/SIOV), and single-function device specifications. It answers questions regarding mandatory security and routing architecture constraints for hardware compliance and inter-device transaction isolation.

children.title: Section 6.12.1.1 ACS Downstream Ports
children.description: Section 6.12.1.1 defines the Access Control Services (ACS) framework for Root Complex Integrated Endpoints (RCiEPs) and Downstream Ports, detailing mechanism-specific controls such as P2P Request Redirect, Translation Blocking, and Source Validation. It answers how ACS-capable hardware manages request routing, security, and access granularity for PCI Express functions via ACS Extended Capability structures.

children.title: Section 6.12.1.2 ACS Functions in SR-IOV, SIOV, and Multi-Function Devices
children.description: This section details Access Control Services (ACS) enforcement mechanisms for PCIe topologies, specifically defining requirements for ACS I/O Request Blocking and Memory Target Access controls for Root Ports and Switch Downstream Ports. It provides technical specifications for managing peer-to-peer Request redirection, egress control, and address-space protection in SR-IOV and Multi-Function device environments.

children.title: Section 6.12.1.3 Functions in Single-Function Devices
children.description: This section defines Access Control Services (ACS) requirements for Single-Function Devices, SIOV-capable PFs, and SDIs regarding peer-to-peer (P2P) traffic routing. It addresses how ACS Direct Translated P2P, Request Redirect, and Egress Control policies govern Memory Request handling and component interoperability within PCIe topologies.

children.title: Section 6.12.3 ACS Peer-to-Peer Control Interactions
children.description: This section defines the routing and policy enforcement constraints for peer-to-peer (P2P) traffic in SIOV and Multi-Function devices, specifically governing Access Control Services (ACS) Request Redirect, Egress Control, and Direct Translated P2P interactions. It answers how SIOV Functions inherit PF policies, when translated memory requests bypass redirection, and how ACS interoperates with non-ACS components within a PCIe topology.

children.title: Section 6.12.4 ACS Enhanced Capability
children.description: This section details **ACS Enhanced Capability** mechanisms, including **I/O Request Blocking**, **DSP/USP Memory Target Access**, and **Unclaimed Request Redirect**, to improve PCIe function isolation. It defines how these controls manage **untranslated memory requests** and **MMIO transactions** within environments supporting **direct assignment** and multiple system address spaces.

children.title: Section 6.12.5 ACS Violation Error Handling
children.description: This section details requirements for handling PCIe ACS Violation errors, covering hardware/software fault isolation, AER diagnostic procedures, and specific Completer Abort (CA) signaling protocols. It defines how components must log violations, manage Advisory Non-Fatal Error status, and distinguish these events from standard Completer Abort errors.

children.title: Section 6.12.6 ACS Redirection Impacts on Ordering Rules
children.description: This section defines Access Control Services (ACS) redirection impacts on PCI Express transaction ordering rules, specifically addressing how Completions and subsequent Requests interact with Posted Requests. It clarifies whether these transactions can bypass pending traffic when ACS redirection is active, providing the technical basis for maintaining consistency in redirected data streams.

children.title: Section 6.12.6.1 Completions Passing Posted Requests
children.description: This section addresses PCIe ordering rule violations between redirected peer-to-peer (P2P) Posted Requests and non-RO Completions. It explains how ACS (Access Control Services) P2P Completion Redirect ensures consistent routing paths to maintain transaction ordering compliance.

children.title: Section 6.12.6.2 Requests Passing Posted Requests
children.description: This section details the risks of violating PCIe ordering rules, specifically when ACS P2P Request Redirect and ACS Direct Translated P2P features are enabled simultaneously. It addresses software configuration requirements to prevent ordering conflicts and highlights the performance trade-offs associated with Redirecting Peer-to-Peer Completions versus utilizing Relaxed Ordering.

children.title: Section 6.13 Alternative Routing-ID Interpretation (ARI)
children.description: Section 6.13 details Alternative Routing-ID Interpretation (ARI), which replaces the traditional 8-bit Bus, 5-bit Device, and 3-bit Function identifier structure with an 8-bit Bus and 8-bit Function format to support up to 256 functions. This section addresses how software detects and configures ARI-capable components, handles Function Groups for arbitration, and modifies Type 1 to Type 0 Configuration Request translation logic.

children.title: Section 6.14 Multicast Operations
children.description: Section 6.14 defines PCIe Multicast operations, covering TLP forwarding, ordering rules, Capability Structure updates, MC-Blocked conditions, and the MC-Overlay mechanism. This section addresses how to implement efficient one-to-many data distribution within a PCIe fabric while ensuring packet ordering integrity and managing Multicast Group (MCG) address space.

children.title: Section 6.14.1 Multicast TLP Processing
children.description: This section outlines PCI Express Multicast architecture, detailing how the Multicast Capability structure facilitates routing and permission checks by segmenting memory address ranges into specific Multicast Groups (MCGs). It defines the criteria for a "Multicast Hit," including address range validation and TLP type requirements, to explain how switches and functions process multicast memory write requests.

children.title: Section 6.14.2 Multicast Ordering
children.description: This section defines PCIe multicast TLP routing by specifying how MC_Receive register states and Multicast Group (MCG) lookups override default address decoding to determine egress forwarding or silent drops. It addresses loop prevention, RC reflection exceptions, and the inheritance of standard ordering rules to clarify how switches and endpoints manage non-unicast traffic flows.

children.title: Section 6.14.3 Multicast Capability Structure Field Updates
children.description: This section details field-level updates to the Multicast Capability Structure, covering protocol-specific configuration parameters and TLV (Type-Length-Value) definitions for multicast routing. It addresses how devices negotiate membership capabilities, synchronize multicast group state, and manage control-plane delivery requirements within the network architecture.

children.title: Section 6.14.5 MC_Overlay Mechanism
children.description: This section details the **MC_Overlay mechanism** for PCIe Multicast Capability, governing the dynamic update requirements for structure fields like **MC_Base_Address** and **MC_Enable**. It addresses how software updates must maintain **TLP ordering** consistency and defines error-handling protocols, specifically **Completer** actions, for **MC_Block_All** and **MC_Block_Untranslated** TLP drops.

children.title: Section 6.15 Atomic Operations (AtomicOps)
children.description: This section details PCIe Atomic Operations—specifically **FetchAdd** and **Swap**—defining their **read-modify-write** mechanics within **Memory Space** transactions. It also provides implementation guidelines for managing **network congestion** through **rate limiting** and **wire-speed consumption** of **Multicast TLPs**.

children.title: Section 6.15.1 AtomicOp Use Models and Benefits
children.description: This section outlines the operational models for **AtomicOp** transactions—specifically **FetchAdd, Swap, and CAS**—including operand size specifications and mandatory PCIe fabric support. It defines the technical requirements for **Requesters, Completers, and routing elements** to successfully execute atomic memory operations across device-to-host, device-to-device, and host-to-device topologies.

children.title: Section 6.15.2 AtomicOp Transaction Protocol Summary
children.description: This section defines the PCI Express AtomicOp transaction protocol, detailing the FetchAdd, Swap, and CAS mechanisms used for low-latency synchronization and lock-free statistics. It addresses technical requirements, performance benefits over locked transactions, and operational characteristics such as transaction addressing, completeness, and concurrency.

children.title: Section 6.15.3 Root Complex Support for AtomicOps
children.description: This section details PCIe Root Complex requirements for AtomicOp transactions, focusing on Completer, Requester, and Routing capabilities. It addresses technical specifications for verifying device compliance, routing logic, and hardware support for atomic operations within a root complex architecture.

children.title: Section 6.15.3.1 Root Ports with AtomicOp Completer Capabilities
children.description: This section defines the compliance requirements for AtomicOp-aware Completers and Root Ports regarding the handling of AtomicOp Request types, operand sizes, and routing. It specifies the necessary error handling protocols—differentiating between Unsupported Requests (UR) and Malformed TLPs—for multi-function devices and Root Complex Integrated Endpoints (RCiEPs).

children.title: Section 6.15.3.2 Root Ports with AtomicOp Routing Capability
children.description: This section specifies requirements for Root Ports to support AtomicOp routing, detailing the "AtomicOp Routing Enable" control bit within the Device Control 2 register and associated TLP handling procedures. It defines mandated behavior for processing AtomicOp requests to ensure proper upstream routing and completion signaling in PCIe hierarchies.

children.title: Section 6.15.3.3 RCs with AtomicOp Requester Capabilities
children.description: This section defines Root Complex (RC) requirements for AtomicOp request routing between Root Ports, covering the AtomicOp Routing Supported bit, Unsupported Request (UR) handling, and RCiEP/host-initiated transaction capabilities. It clarifies implementation dependencies for peer-to-peer routing, error reporting protocols, and the scope of software-based requester support within the PCIe architecture.

children.title: Section 6.15.4 Switch Support for AtomicOps
children.description: This section specifies that if a Switch supports AtomicOp routing, it must apply to all Ports, and outlines the Dynamic Power Allocation (DPA) capability for Endpoint Functions. It clarifies DPA’s role as an optional, independent mechanism for optimizing power usage in the D0 state by defining up to 32 numbered power substates.

children.title: Section 6.16 Dynamic Power Allocation (DPA) Capability
children.description: This section specifies the Dynamic Power Allocation (DPA) capability, defining how Endpoint Functions manage power substates [0-31] independently of PCI-PM to optimize consumption during the D0 state. It addresses how devices without dedicated drivers perform power negotiation and establishes the normative rules for using DPA versus the Emergency Power Reduction State.

children.title: Section 6.16.1 DPA Capability with Multi-Function Devices
children.description: This section details Dynamic Power Allocation (DPA) requirements for multi-function PCI Express devices, focusing on substate transition behaviors, power consumption constraints, and transition latency reporting. It defines the technical rules for power reporting during state changes and explains how devices signal maximum transition latency to facilitate platform power management policies.

children.title: Section 6.17 TLP Processing Hints (TPH)
children.description: Section 6.17 details TLP Processing Hints (TPH) for PCIe data optimization, covering Steering Tags (ST), ST modes of operation, and TPH capability structures. This section defines how TPH mechanisms improve cache locality and memory latency by providing metadata hints for transaction layer packet routing and processing.

children.title: Section 6.17.1 Processing Hints
children.description: This section outlines PCI Express Function Power Management state transition requirements, detailing Substate Status reporting, power draw constraints, and Transition Latency Value definitions. It addresses how a Function manages power consumption during state changes and provides the timing metrics necessary for software to implement effective power management policies.

children.title: Section 6.17.3 ST Modes of Operation
children.description: This section defines PCIe Steering Tag (ST) modes of operation, detailing Processing Hint (PH) mappings for cache-targeted data structures and the configuration of hardware-specific ST Tables within TPH Requester or MSI-X structures. It answers how topological cache steering is implemented and where software programs these system-specific metadata tags to optimize TLP routing between host and device.

children.title: Section 6.17.4 TPH Capability
children.description: This section defines TPH (Transaction Processing Hints) ST (Steering Tag) Operating Modes, specifically Interrupt Vector and Device Specific modes, to optimize PCI Express performance. It details how the Function maps Steering Tags using MSI/MSI-X vector indices and clarifies requirements for ST Table size constraints relative to interrupt message ranges.

children.title: Section 6.18 Latency Tolerance Reporting (LTR) Mechanism
