children.description: This section details the Latency Tolerance Reporting (LTR) mechanism, covering its integration with TPH Requester capabilities, configuration through the Device Capabilities 2 Register, and its role in optimizing Root Complex power management based on Endpoint service latency requirements. It addresses how software enables the mechanism and explains the functional distinction between latency requirements affecting system performance versus operational correctness.

children.title: Section 6.19 Optimized Buffer Flush/Fill (OBFF) Mechanism
children.description: This section details the Optimized Buffer Flush/Fill (OBFF) mechanism and Latency Tolerance Reporting (LTR), explaining how Endpoints communicate latency requirements to the Root Complex to synchronize data transfers with low-power states. It addresses how devices manage link power states, resume DMA activity, and utilize LTR messages to balance power efficiency with sustained throughput performance.

children.title: Section 6.20 PASID
children.description: Section 6.20 covers Process Address Space ID (PASID) management, detailing the hardware-software governance of PASID allocation, capacity, and state tracking. It provides the technical specifications required to answer questions regarding how system software enables, registers, and manages granular process-level address space isolation within PCIe devices.

children.title: Section 6.20.1 Managing PASID Usage
children.description: This section outlines OBFF message transmission protocols for power management and defines the technical implementation of PASID TLP Prefixes for both Non-Flit and Flit modes. It details configuration sequences for PCIe Ports, PASID address space identification, and the functional relationship between Requester IDs and process-specific address spaces.

children.title: Section 6.20.2 PASID Information Layout
children.description: This section details the PCIe PASID TLP Prefix architecture, covering binary layouts for both Flit and Non-Flit modes. It defines specific bit-field behaviors for Execute Requested and Privileged Mode Requested attributes to clarify how host-side process address spaces are signaled in transaction layer packets.

children.title: Section 6.20.2.1 PASID TLP Prefix - Non-Flit Mode
children.description: This section defines requirements for PASID TLP Prefix usage in Non-Flit Mode, detailing the mandatory "PASID Enable" and "Translated Requests with PASID Enable" configuration bits. It specifies the operational constraints and required completion behaviors for device-specific PASID stop mechanisms, ensuring that stopping one PASID does not disrupt concurrent Function operations or non-PASID transactions.

children.title: Section 6.20.2.2 PASID field (Flit Mode and Non-Flit Mode)
children.description: This section defines the 4-byte PASID TLP Prefix structure, detailing bit-level requirements for Privileged Mode, Execute Requested, and the 20-bit Process Address Space ID field. The text specifies how these fields identify user processes in PCIe transactions and references supporting capabilities like Max PASID Width to clarify implementation compliance for Endpoints and Root Complexes.

children.title: Section 6.20.2.3 Execute Requested
children.description: Section 6.20.2.3 defines Protocol-Specific PASID constraints and Execute Requested bit requirements for PCIe Endpoints and Root Complexes. It specifies rules for PASID value limits, Unsupported Request error handling, and the mandatory prerequisite of Execute Permission Supported for requesting instruction execution privileges.

children.title: Section 6.20.2.4 Privileged Mode Requested
children.description: This section defines the "Execute Requested" bit in PCIe transaction layer protocols, covering implementation requirements, enablement mechanisms for Root Complexes, and protection check logic for Completers. It answers how systems securely manage execution permissions for memory requests and specifies when this bit is reserved versus enforced during address translation.

children.title: Section 6.21 Precision Time Measurement (PTM) Mechanism
children.description: This section details the Precision Time Measurement (PTM) mechanism, covering timing synchronization protocols, hardware configuration requirements, and link-level operational procedures. It addresses how PCIe devices achieve sub-microsecond time-stamping accuracy and manage clock synchronization across a fabric for latency-sensitive applications.

children.title: Section 6.21.1 Introduction
children.description: This section introduces Precision Time Measurement (PTM) and Enhanced PTM (ePTM) for synchronizing independent hardware clocks across a PCIe hierarchy. It defines key architectural roles—including PTM Root, Requester, Responder, and Time Source—while explaining how components calculate offsets relative to a unified PTM Master Time domain.

children.title: Section 6.21.2 PTM Link Protocol
children.description: Section 6.21.2 details the Precision Time Measurement (PTM) link protocol, covering hierarchy topologies, master time synchronization, and the roles of PTM Roots, Requesters, and Responders. It answers how PTM architectures are configured across Root Complexes and switches while providing essential implementation guidance on mitigating timing accuracy degradation caused by PCIe Retimer asymmetric link delays.

children.title: Section 6.21.3 Configuration and Operational Requirements
children.description: This section details the Precision Time Measurement (PTM) configuration requirements, establishing the hierarchical enablement rules for PTM Control bits across Upstream and Downstream Ports. It defines error-handling protocols for Unsupported Requests, behavioral constraints for PTM Master Time consistency, and technical specifications for timestamp capture.

children.title: Section 6.21.3.1 PTM Requester Role
children.description: This section defines the mandatory Precision Time Measurement (PTM) Requester Role, specifically resolving ambiguity between propagation delay byte-order interpretations (A vs. B). It specifies how the PTM Propagation Delay Adaptation Capability—via the PTM Capability and Link Control registers—enables interoperation between devices using conflicting field-ordering standards.

children.title: Section 6.21.3.2 PTM Responder Role
children.description: This section details the operational requirements for PCIe components acting as Precision Time Measurement (PTM) Responders, focusing on the PTM Capability register, message field population, and PTM Master Time synchronization. It addresses technical specifications for PTM Responder message construction, propagation delay measurement, and multi-port orchestration in switches and Root Complexes.

children.title: Section 6.21.3.3 PTM Time Source Role - Rules Specific to Switches
children.description: This section specifies PCIe Precision Time Measurement (PTM) operational requirements for switches, covering PTM Master/Responder roles, timestamp granularity constraints, and implementation rules for multi-function device architectures. It addresses how switches synchronize internal PTM contexts, manage upstream port Extended Capability structures, and account for internal delays when acting as PTM Roots or intermediaries.

children.title: Section 6.22 Readiness Notifications (RN)
children.description: Section 6.22 defines Readiness Notifications (RN), including Device Readiness Status (DRS) and Function Readiness Status (FRS), to optimize timing for Configuration Requests. Additionally, the provided excerpt details PTM Timestamp Capture mechanisms, addressing how Physical Layer proximity and Transaction/Data Link layer determinism impact timestamp accuracy for PTM message protocols.

children.title: Section 6.22.1 Device Readiness Status (DRS)
children.description: This section defines the Device Readiness Status (DRS) protocol, outlining mandatory implementation requirements for Downstream and Upstream Ports, specific triggering "DRS Events," and the criteria for Configuration-Ready signaling across Type 0 and Type 1 Functions. It answers when DRS Messages must be transmitted, how Port-level readiness is determined following link transitions, and the operational expectations for PCIe Devices in Flit Mode.

children.title: Section 6.22.3 FRS Queuing
children.description: This section defines the Functional Readiness Status (FRS) queuing protocol, specifying the event-driven triggers—Function Level Reset (FLR), D3Hot to D0 transitions, and SR-IOV VF enabling—that mandate the transmission of FRS Messages to indicate configuration readiness. It details protocol requirements for Requesters, Switches, and Root Ports, ultimately answering when and how a PCIe function signals to the fabric that it is prepared to process Configuration Requests.

children.title: Section 6.23 Enhanced Allocation
children.description: Section 6.23 defines the operational requirements for the FRS Message Queue within a Root Complex Event Collector, specifying mechanisms for message queuing, overflow handling, and status interrupt generation. Additionally, it introduces the Enhanced Allocation (EA) Capability as an optional feature for flexible I/O, Memory, and Bus Number resource management beyond standard BAR constraints.

children.title: Section 6.24 Emergency Power Reduction State
children.description: This section defines PCIe Emergency Power Reduction State requirements, specifically mandating that functions using Extended Allocation (EA) hardwire equivalent BARs and Expansion ROM registers to zero to prevent resource conflicts. It clarifies protocol for bridges utilizing EA mechanisms and prescribes how system firmware must traverse device hierarchies to identify complete resource allocations.

children.title: Section 6.25 Hierarchy ID Message
children.description: Section 6.25 describes the **Hierarchy ID Message** mechanism used to establish unique **Routing IDs** for PCI Express Functions in multi-hierarchy and clustered systems. It details how augmenting local IDs with a **System Identifier** and **Hierarchy Identifier** enables global uniqueness and explains how these messages are generated and reported via the **Hierarchy ID Extended Capability**.

children.title: Section 6.26 Flattening Portal Bridge (FPB)
children.description: This section covers the architecture and implementation of the Flattening Portal Bridge (FPB), detailing essential hardware specifications, software dependencies, and integration protocols. It answers questions regarding system requirements, technical deployment prerequisites, and the operational workflow necessary to establish a functional bridge connection.

children.title: Section 6.26.1 Introduction
children.description: This section defines the Flattening Portal Bridge (FPB) mechanism for optimizing Routing ID and memory resource scalability across non-ARI and ARI PCIe functions. It details architecture-specific Routing ID bit-field allocations and provides implementation guidance for managing Virtual Function (VF) Hierarchy ID capabilities during VM migration and configuration.

children.title: Section 6.26.2 Hardware and Software Requirements
children.description: This section details Fabric-based Programmable BAR (FPB) implementation requirements for Routing IDs, MEM Low, and MEM High resource management within PCI bridges. It addresses how bit-vector mechanisms, configurable offsets, and granularity settings facilitate flexible resource decoding between primary and secondary bridge interfaces while defining hardware-software responsibilities.

children.title: Section 6.27 Vital Product Data (VPD)
children.description: This section defines Vital Product Data (VPD) as a repository for hardware, software, and microcode component identification and performance monitoring, while outlining Flexible PCI/PCIe Bus (FPB) resource allocation strategies for static and dynamic system configurations. It addresses how systems store Field Replaceable Unit (FRU) metadata and how implementation-level FPB architectures resolve PCI/PCIe hierarchy limitations and resource rebalancing inefficiencies.

children.title: Section 6.27.1 VPD Format
children.description: Section 6.27.1 defines the structure of Vital Product Data (VPD) resources, specifying a three-byte header format comprised of an ASCII keyword and binary length. It addresses how to encode and interpret hardware identifiers—such as serial numbers—by detailing data field mapping and standardized ASCII representation.

children.title: Section 6.27.2 VPD Definitions
children.description: Section 6.27.2 defines Vital Product Data (VPD) structures, covering resource data tags, read-only fields, and read/write fields. This section specifies how to format and organize device identification metadata to address requirements for hardware configuration and inventory tracking.

children.title: Section 6.27.2.1 VPD Large and Small Resource Data Tags
children.description: This section defines the structure and byte-level encoding of Vital Product Data (VPD) Small and Large Resource Data Tags used for identifying hardware configuration data. It details tag-specific header formats and payload lengths, providing the technical specifications necessary to parse and validate resource record descriptors within a VPD data structure.

children.title: Section 6.27.2.2 Read-Only Fields
children.description: This section details the structure of PCI Vital Product Data (VPD) using Large and Small Resource Data Tags and read-only keywords. It defines technical specifications for identifying add-in cards, including Part Number (PN), Engineering Change (EC) level, and manufacturing metadata, answering how device identification is formatted within the VPD storage component.

children.title: Section 6.27.2.4 VPD Example
children.description: This section details VPD (Vital Product Data) structure, specifying rules for add-in card form factor conformance, vendor-specific (Vx), and system-specific (Yx) data fields. It addresses how to identify hardware form factors via wildcards and defines the management of Asset Tags and Remaining Read/Write (RW) space allocations.

children.title: Section 6.28 Native PCIe Enclosure Management
children.description: This section details the Native PCIe Enclosure Management (NPEM) Extended Capability, covering Vital Product Data (VPD) structures, LED status control, and configuration mechanisms for PCIe SSD enclosures. It answers how NPEM integrates into Downstream Ports or Endpoints to provide standardized storage-related enclosure management consistent with existing storage ecosystems.

children.title: Section 6.29 Conventional PCI Advanced Features Operation
children.description: This section defines the Conventional PCI Advanced Features (AF) Capability, focusing on Function Level Reset (FLR) and Transactions Pending (TP) mechanisms for Root Complex-integrated devices. It details how these features enable granular hardware resets and pending completion tracking while specifying which configuration registers remain immutable during an FLR operation.

children.title: Section 6.30 Data Object Exchange (DOE)
children.description: This section defines the Data Object Exchange (DOE) Extended Capability, covering its role as a prerequisite for Component Measurement and Authentication (CMA) and SPDM-based in-band firmware communication. It outlines implementation rules for Functions and RCRBs, specifying how vendor-defined data object types and features are structured and routed via Configuration Requests.

children.title: Section 6.30.1 Data Objects and Features
children.description: This section details the Data Object Exchange (DOE) structural specifications, covering the required 2 DW to 256K DW object length and the specific bit-field formatting for the Vendor ID and Data Object Type headers. It defines the hierarchical relationship between applications, sessions, and the PCI device mailbox to explain how software manages concurrent data object transfers.

children.title: Section 6.30.1.1 DOE Discovery Feature
children.description: This section details the PCIe DOE (Data Object Exchange) Discovery feature, specifying requirements for handling unsupported data objects, expected length variances, and the implementation of discovery requests across device power states. It defines the structure of the DOE Discovery Request object, including bit-field mappings for Index, Version, and Vendor ID, to clarify how software enumerates supported data object types.

children.title: Section 6.30.1.2 DOE Async Message
children.description: Section 6.30.1.2 defines the architectural requirements for **DOE (Data Object Exchange) Async Messages**, specifically detailing the implementation protocols for **CMA/SPDM**, **Secured CMA-SPDM**, and **Connection ID** support within DOE Discovery Responses. It answers how to structure the **4th DW (Data Word)** and **Additional Info** fields to ensure compatibility and manage **Connection ID ranges** for secure SPDM session establishment.

children.title: Section 6.30.2 Operation
children.description: This section defines the operational protocol for Data Object Exchange (DOE) Async Messages, specifying requirements for message varieties, encapsulated data objects, and the handshaking process between a DOE instance and system software. It addresses key technical criteria including DOE Async Message Support/Enable configuration, status bit polling, and permitted message types like CMA/SPDM.

children.title: Section 6.30.3 Interrupt Generation
children.description: This section defines hardware requirements for Data Object Exchange (DOE) interrupt generation, specifying mandatory MSI/MSI-X support, register control mechanisms (DOE Control/Status/Capabilities), and conditions for interrupt triggering. It clarifies how DOE instances manage configuration-based signaling and mailbox-level flow control to ensure robust data object exchange.

children.title: Section 6.31 Component Measurement and Authentication (CMA-SPDM)
children.description: This section details the Component Measurement and Authentication (CMA-SPDM) architecture, covering SPDM data object integration, interrupt vector configuration via DOE, and security evolution strategies. It addresses how the specification facilitates secure component authentication, balances protocol interoperability with security updates, and utilizes PCIe-layered architecture to manage extensible security exchanges.

children.title: Section 6.31.3 CMA-SPDM Rules
children.description: This section details CMA-SPDM implementation requirements for PCIe device functions, switches, and sideband communication channels. It addresses whether SPDM responder support is mandatory for specific PCIe components and mandates certificate consistency when multiple access mechanisms like DOE and MCTP are utilized.

children.title: Section 6.31.4 Secured CMA-SPDM
children.description: This section defines the technical requirements for implementing Secured CMA-SPDM, including mandated cryptographic algorithms (SHA-256/384), Cryptographic Timeout (CT) constraints, and session persistence during Function Level Resets (FLR). It specifies protocols for secure data object transport, session establishment per SPDM 1.1+, and the integration of IDE_KM protocols.

children.title: Section 6.32 Deferrable Memory Write
children.description: This section defines mandatory cryptographic requirements for Deferrable Memory Writes, specifically covering SPDM protocol negotiation, AEAD structures, DHE groups, and Secured Message formats. It answers questions regarding implementation standards for mutual authentication, KeySchedule compatibility, and the version-specific Opaque Data formats required to ensure secure transport and interoperability.

children.title: Section 6.33 Integrity & Data Encryption (IDE)
children.description: Section 6.33 defines Integrity & Data Encryption (IDE), covering cryptographic protocols for TLP confidentiality, integrity, and replay protection across PCIe Links and Switches. This section clarifies how IDE establishes Selective and Link IDE Streams to mitigate physical and hardware-level security threats in trusted execution environments.

children.title: Section 6.33.1 IDE Stream and TEE State Machines
children.description: This section outlines the lifecycle of IDE Streams, specifically detailing the multi-step initialization process involving authentication, IDE_KM key exchanges, and Secure Connection configurations. It addresses how IDE state machines and Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) requirements ensure secure data isolation from the Port-to-Port boundary through on-die implementations.

children.title: Section 6.33.2 IDE Stream Establishment
children.description: This section outlines the state machine requirements for IDE stream establishment, focusing on the transition between "Not Locked" and "Locked" operational conditions based on stream security status. It defines mandatory protections against adversary-in-the-middle (AITM) attacks by requiring that essential configuration information be securely established, confirmed, and maintained within the TEE environment.

children.title: Section 6.33.3 IDE Key Management (IDE_KM)
children.description: This section defines the mandatory architectural placement of the IDE Extended Capability for Endpoints, Switches, and Bridge Functions, while establishing requirements for IDE Key Management (IDE_KM) protocol support. It addresses the implementation of DOE/CMA-SPDM responders, allowed use of MCTP, and authorization for host firmware-directed IDE_KM pass-through communication between Partner Ports.

children.title: Section 6.33.4 IDE TLPs
children.description: This section details the formal requirements for IDE TLP formatting in Non-Flit and Flit modes, complemented by an implementation guide for the IDE Key Management (IDE_KM) protocol. It defines the structural TLP constraints and the step-by-step SPDM-based authentication and key distribution workflow between Host and Endpoint.

children.title: Section 6.33.5 IDE TLP Sub-Streams
children.description: This section defines **IDE TLP Sub-Streams** and their implementation within PCIe, focusing on **XT bit** processing, **AES-GCM** cryptographic synchronization, and the maintenance of in-order TLP delivery across **Flow Control Credit** types. It addresses how IDE partners manage packet ordering and security limitations to prevent deadlocks and mitigate specific security attack vectors.

children.title: Section 6.33.6 IDE TLP Aggregation
children.description: This section defines rules for Integrity and Data Encryption (IDE) TLP aggregation, covering constraints on Sub-Stream grouping, Data Link Layer payload limits (256 DW), and software configuration requirements. It answers how Transmitters and Receivers must manage buffered TLP sequences, Flow Control credit alignment, and the integration of IDE Sync Messages during aggregated transmissions.

children.title: Section 6.33.7 Flow-Through Selective IDE Streams
children.description: This section defines the routing and ordering constraints for Flow-Through Selective IDE Streams within PCIe Switches and Root Complexes, where IDE Terminus functionality is decoupled from pass-through stream handling. It details specific modifications to TLP ordering rules—forbidding reordering within Sub-Streams—to ensure data integrity for IDE-protected traffic traversing multiple ports.

children.title: Section 6.33.8 Other IDE Rules
children.description: This section details PCI Express IDE (Integrity and Data Encryption) stream ordering rules, specifying transaction traffic requirements for Flow-Through non-UIO and UIO IDE streams. It defines technical constraints for Switches and Root Complexes, including TLP routing prohibitions, handling of Misrouted IDE TLP errors, and mandatory adherence to stream-specific ordering matrices.

children.title: Section 6.34 Unordered I/O (UIO)
children.description: This section defines Unordered I/O (UIO) protocol requirements, governing transaction ordering constraints, completion policies, and adherence to PCI Express ordering rules. It addresses how systems manage out-of-order data delivery and the technical mandates for maintaining architectural consistency during non-ordered memory and I/O operations.

children.title: Section 6.34.1 UIO Rules
children.description: This section establishes mandatory Power Management and security requirements for UIO/IDE implementations, specifically detailing state retention, registers, and transition protocols for the Insecure state. It addresses how IDE hardware must maintain key/counter integrity across power states (D0-D3hot) and validates security event handling during configuration modifications.

children.title: Section 6.35 MMIO Register Blocks
children.description: This section defines the constraints and requirements for UIO (Upstream IO) traffic within non-tree and multi-path PCIe topologies, specifically addressing credit-based flow control, SVC VC protocol support, and configuration synchronization. It clarifies the scope of UIO implementation regarding deadlock prevention, TLP routing limitations outside the enumeration tree, and the absence of hardware-level address hazard protection.

children.title: Section 6.35.1 MMIO Capabilities Register Block (MCAP)
children.description: This section defines the MMIO Capabilities Register Block (MCAP) architecture, detailing the organizational structure of the MCAP Array, header registers, and associated functional blocks like MMB and MMPT. It explains how PCIe devices use the MMIO Register Block Locator (MRBL) and mapped Memory Space (BARx) to facilitate vendor-specific and standardized register communication.

children.title: Section 6.35.1.1 MCAP Array Register (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section defines the MCAP Array Register block, specifically detailing its discovery via the MMIO Register Block Locator (MRBL) and the bit-level structure of the MCAP Array Register 1. It answers how to identify the hardware's MCAP capabilities and provides the specific register offsets, field allocations, and attributes required for firmware to interact with the MCAP interface.

children.title: Section 6.35.1.2 MCAP Header Register Block (Offset Varies)
children.description: This section defines the structural architecture of the MCAP Header Register Block, covering the MCAP Count, ID, and Version fields within the PCI Express® configuration space. It specifies the layout and addressing of contiguous MCAP Header Registers to facilitate capability discovery and version management for hardware extensions.

children.title: Section 6.35.1.3 MMIO Mailbox Capability (MMB) (Offset: Varies)
children.description: This section defines the MMIO Mailbox Capability (MMB) structure, detailing the MCAP Header Register 4 specifications, PCI-SIG Vendor ID assignments, and the MCAP ID hierarchy. It addresses how to identify, categorize, and verify the MMIO mailbox interface while establishing requirements for single-threaded command execution within a CXL function.

children.title: Section 6.35.1.3.1 MMB Operation
children.description: This section defines the operational states and interrupt mechanisms for the Memory-Mapped Block (MMB) interface, focusing on power-management responsiveness via the MMB Attention Mechanism. It details hardware-software synchronization, MSI/MSI-X interrupt configuration, and the command execution flow for version-controlled MMB registers.

children.title: Section 6.35.1.3.2.1 MMB Capabilities Register (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section defines the MMB Capabilities Register structure and bit-level specifications within the MMB register map. It details technical parameters including payload size limits, interrupt capabilities, and timing requirements, answering how software queries hardware functionality for MMB interface management.

children.title: Section 6.35.1.3.2.2 MMB Control Register (Offset 04h)
children.description: This section defines the MMB Control Register (Offset 04h) bit fields, including the Doorbell mechanism for command execution management and the Command Ready Interrupt Enable for MSI/MSI-X signaling. It specifies how callers trigger command processing, monitors Function status availability, and details MMB Attention Mechanism capabilities.

children.title: Section 6.35.1.3.2.3 MMB Command Register (Offset 08h)
children.description: This section defines the structural layout and operational requirements of the MMB Command Register (Offset 08h), including the MMB Command Opcode, MMB Payload Length, and Vendor ID fields. It specifies the necessary synchronization protocols, such as ensuring the MMB Control Register Doorbell is cleared before use, and details how callers and functions manage command input/output sizes.

children.title: Section 6.35.1.3.2.4 MMB Status Register (Offset 10h)
children.description: This section details the **MMB Status Register (Offset 10h)**, covering bit definitions, error reporting mechanisms, and operational state indicators for the Memory Management Bridge. It clarifies how software can monitor bridge health, identify hardware faults, and troubleshoot system-level communication errors.

children.title: Section 6.35.1.3.2.4.1 MMB Command Return Codes
children.description: This section defines the MMB Status Register architecture, including bit-field definitions for readiness, attention signaling, and command return codes. It specifies the functional requirements for CXL-compliant interfaces, identifying how the Function communicates operational status and command execution results following a Conventional Reset.

children.title: Section 6.35.1.3.2.5 MMB Payload Registers (Offset 20h)
children.description: This section defines MMB Payload Register specifications, including valid PCI-SIG command return codes (0000h–FFFFh) and payload sizing constraints ranging from 256 bytes to 1 MB. It serves as a technical reference for interpreting CXL-compliant command status codes and enforcing proper data transfer protocols during MMB register interactions.

children.title: Section 6.35.1.4 Management Message Passthrough (MMPT) Capability (Offset: Varies)
children.description: This section details the Management Message Passthrough (MMPT) capability, covering the functional architecture of MMPT registers, including capabilities, control, and receive message notification. It specifies the technical interface requirements and register offsets necessary to implement and manage asynchronous message routing between components via the MMPT protocol.

children.title: Section 6.35.1.4.1.1 MMPT Capabilities Register (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section defines the MMPT Capabilities Register (Offset 00h) and the architectural layout of the Management Message Passthrough (MMPT) register block, including associated control and notification registers. It specifies the configuration and versioning requirements for Functions implementing the MMPT command set as outlined in the PCI Express Base Specification.

children.title: Section 6.35.1.4.1.2 MMPT Control Register (Offset 04h)
children.description: This section details the **MMPT Capabilities Register (Offset 04h)**, covering **MSI/MSI-X interrupt signaling** and **vector mapping** for the MMPT Receive Message Ready state. It specifies hardware requirements for interrupt message number calculation and defines constraints for MSI-X table entry selection.

children.title: Section 6.35.1.4.1.3 MMPT Receive Message Notification Register (Offset 08h)
children.description: This section details the **MMPT Receive Message Notification Register (Offset 08h)**, defining the control and status bits for management message signaling, including the **MMPT Receive Message Ready** status and **Message Type** field. It provides technical specifications for **MSI/MSI-X interrupt enablement** and the mechanism governing how a function alerts the host to new management messages ready for transfer.

children.title: Section 6.35.2 MMIO Designated Vendor-Specific Register Block (MDVS)
children.description: This section details the MMIO Designated Vendor-Specific (MDVS) register interface, specifically defining the MMPT Control Register and MMPT Receive Message Notification Register. It addresses how the Function signals management message availability and identifies the configuration and status mechanisms required to retrieve messages via the MMPT command interface.

children.title: Section 6.35.2.1 MDVS Register Block Header Register 1 (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section defines the **MDVS Register Block Header Register 1 (Offset 00h)** within the **PCI Express MDVS architecture**, detailing the **MDVS Vendor ID** and **MDVS Register Block ID** fields. It provides technical specifications for identifying the organization that defined the register block and the specific format of the subsequent **MMIO Designated Vendor-Specific Registers**.

children.title: Section 6.35.3 MDVS Register Block Header Register 2 (Offset 04h)
children.description: This section details the configuration and functional definition of the MDVS Register Block Header Register 2 at memory offset 04h. It specifies bit-field assignments, read/write access protocols, and architectural attributes necessary for proper MDVS hardware communication and register management.

children.title: Section 6.35.4 MDVS Register Block Header Register 3 (Offset 08h)
children.description: This section defines the MDVS Register Block Header Register 3 (offset 08h), specifically detailing the **MDVS Register Block Length** field within the PCI Express® Base Specification. It explains how this 32-bit read-only register identifies the total byte count of the register block, including header and vendor-specific fields.

children.title: Section 6.36 MMB Command Interface
children.description: This section details the MMB Command Interface and the Management Message Passthrough (MMPT) protocol for facilitating OOB communication. It covers command encapsulation, message routing, and packet handling protocols to resolve questions regarding inter-component signaling and firmware-level management interactions.

children.title: Section 6.36.1 Management Message Passthrough (MMPT)
children.description: This section specifies the Management Message Passthrough (MMPT) protocol within the MMB command structure, detailing the opcode versioning strategy, specific command codes (0100h for Send, 0101h for Receive), and associated payload size requirements. It defines how MMB command opcodes ensure backward compatibility and provides the normative framework for managing message transmission and reception between components.

children.title: Section 6.36.1.1 MMPT Send Message (Opcode 0100h)
children.description: This section defines the MMPT "Send Message" protocol for handling management message transfers, detailing the usage of action opcodes for full versus segmented data transmission. It clarifies requirements for data ordering, offset management, and error handling during message fragmentation or interruption.

children.title: Section 6.36.1.1.1 MMPT Send Message Operation
children.description: This section defines the **MMPT Send Message Operation** protocol, detailing the **Action field encodings** (Full, Initiate, Continue, Finish, and Abort), **input/output payload structures**, and **data transfer flow control**. It addresses how a host manages fragmentation and retransmission when the total **management message** size exceeds the **MMB Payload Registers Size**.

children.title: Section 6.36.1.2 MMPT Receive Message (Opcode 0101h)
children.description: This section defines the MMPT Receive Message (Opcode 0101h) command, detailing the input payload structure for managing message transfers—including Message Type, flow control Flags, offset, and length—and the corresponding management data output. It specifies valid MMB command return codes and the protocol for segmenting or completing message transfers between the PCI Express Function and the host.

children.title: Section 6.36.1.2.1 MMPT Receive Message Operation
children.description: This section details the asynchronous management message transfer protocol (MMPT) from Function to Host, covering status notification, polling versus interrupt handling, and data retrieval via Receive Message commands. It specifies procedures for handling payloads via full, initiated, or segmented transfers, answering how the Host retrieves messages of varying sizes using payload registers.

children.title: Section 6.37 Debug Over Link
children.description: Section 6.37 details the Debug Over Link protocol, specifically addressing NOP Flit mechanisms for link-level state management and maintenance. It explains how these control flits facilitate non-intrusive diagnostic data transmission and link synchronization during active traffic.

children.title: Section 6.37.1 NOP Flit
children.description: This section details the asynchronous management message transfer protocol between Function and Host, covering the MMPT Receive Message Notification mechanism, data transfer actions (Full, Initiate, Continue, Finish), and partial-message retransmission logic. It defines the specific register workflows for notification signaling and multi-part payload retrieval when message lengths exceed the MMB Payload Registers capacity.

children.title: Chapter 7. Software Initialization and Configuration
children.description: This section details the PCIe configuration architecture, including topology, register access mechanisms, and mandatory functional capabilities spanning legacy PCI and extended PCIe standards. It addresses inquiries regarding how software discovers devices, manages register-level initialization, and implements required feature sets across varying system configurations.

children.title: Section 7.1 Configuration Topology
children.description: This section details PCIe configuration hierarchy using standard (CAM) and enhanced (ECAM) access mechanisms to ensure software compatibility and expanded address space. It addresses how Root Ports and Switch internal architectures utilize logical PCI-PCI Bridge structures to define bus enumeration and mapping within a PCIe topology.

children.title: Section 7.2 PCI Express Configuration Mechanisms
children.description: Section 7.2 defines PCI Express configuration mechanisms, contrasting the 4096-byte PCIe configuration space with the 256-byte PCI-compatible legacy region. It addresses how software alters device OS driver bindings, class codes, and function availability through architected register modifications and implementation-specific firmware updates.

children.title: Section 7.2.1 PCI-compatible Configuration Mechanism
children.description: This section details the PCI-compatible Configuration Mechanism, which maintains backward compatibility with conventional PCI bus enumeration and initialization for PCI Express Functions. It specifies the configuration space header format, clarifies the independence of the legacy mechanism from the Enhanced Configuration Access Mechanism (ECAM), and mandates that PCI-compatible requests utilize a zeroed Extended Register Address field.

children.title: Section 7.2.2 PCI Express Enhanced Configuration Access Mechanism (ECAM)
children.description: This section details the Enhanced Configuration Access Mechanism (ECAM), defining the memory-mapped architecture used to access PCI Express Configuration Space registers. It specifies the conditions for ECAM implementation, the role of host bridge firmware in reporting memory ranges, and the address translation mapping required for system-level configuration access.

children.title: Section 7.2.2.2 PCI Express Device Requirements
children.description: This section outlines PCI Express Host Bridge requirements for ECAM-based configuration space access and mandates that PCIe devices decode the Extended Register Address[3:0] field for extended configuration access. It addresses how bridges translate memory-mapped configuration requests and provides implementation guidelines for placing device-specific registers within architected Vendor-Specific Capability structures.

children.title: Section 7.2.3 Root Complex Register Block (RCRB)
children.description: This section defines the Root Complex Register Block (RCRB) as an optional memory-mapped 4096-byte register space for Root Ports and RCiEPs, detailing architectural constraints regarding address space isolation and alignment requirements. It addresses how RCRBs complement PCI Express Extended Capabilities and specifies the routing restrictions and functional addressing rules for Downstream Ports lacking ARI Forwarding.

children.title: Section 7.3 Configuration Transaction Rules
children.description: Section 7.3 details the fundamental mechanics of PCI configuration transactions, covering device addressing, request routing, and legacy support for special cycles. It provides technical specifications for identifying, targeting, and orchestrating communication across the PCI device hierarchy.

children.title: Section 7.3.1 Device Number
children.description: This section defines the architectural requirements for **Root Complex Register Blocks (RCRB)** and the **Device Number** constraints for PCI Express components. It specifies memory-mapping rules for RCRB access, limitations on non-ARI device configurations, and the mandatory association of Device 0 for downstream ports lacking ARI Forwarding.

children.title: Section 7.3.2 Configuration Transaction Addressing
children.description: This section details PCI Express Configuration Transaction addressing, specifying how Type 0 and Type 1 messages utilize Bus, Device, and Function (BDF) identifiers to route packets through a hierarchy. It defines the hierarchical discovery process and answers how Requester IDs and Completion IDs ensure correct configuration space access across multi-switch topologies.

children.title: Section 7.3.3 Configuration Request Routing Rules
children.description: This section details PCIe configuration request routing by specifying requirements for Non-ARI device identification, virtual switch topology, and Flattening Portal Bridge (FPB) bus flattening mechanisms. It resolves how switches handle multi-function addressing, ID-based routing for ARI versus legacy devices, and the mandatory return of Unsupported Request (UR) status for unimplemented configuration space addresses.

children.title: Section 7.3.4 PCI Special Cycles
children.description: This section outlines the PCI Express handling of unimplemented Configuration Space register accesses and clarifies that PCI Special Cycles are unsupported except when tunneled through PCI Express-to-PCI Bridges using Type 1 configuration cycles. It defines the "HwInit" register attribute and specifies mandatory error-handling behavior, such as returning Successful Completion for unimplemented register requests.

children.title: Section 7.4 Configuration Register Types
children.description: This section defines PCI Express configuration register behaviors, covering architectural rules for unimplemented register access, requirements for PCI Special Cycle support, and register attribute definitions. It specifically addresses how devices handle invalid register addresses, specifies how components initialize hardware-defined fields, and functions as a reference for handling Configuration Space read/write completions.

children.title: Section 7.5 PCI and PCIe Capabilities Required by the Base Spec for all Ports
children.description: This section defines mandatory PCI and PCIe configuration registers, Power Management structures, and Express Capability requirements for all device ports. It answers questions regarding standard header register compliance, power state transitions, and the foundational architecture necessary for system-level PCI Express interoperability.

children.title: Section 7.5.1 PCI-Compatible Configuration Registers
children.description: This section details the organization of PCI-Compatible Configuration Registers, covering standard Type 0 and Type 1 configuration space headers and common header fields. It addresses how PCI-compliant devices manage identification, base address register (BAR) mapping, and bus-level routing configurations.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1 Type 0/1 Common Configuration Space
children.description: This section defines the structural requirements for Type 0/1 PCI Express Configuration Space headers, focusing on VF-specific attribute encoding (ROZ, RsvdP, RsvdZ) and PCI-compatible aliasing. It answers how configuration registers are architected for PFs and VFs and defines standard field attributes for little-endian PCI register access.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1.1 Vendor ID Register (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section specifies the **Vendor ID Register** functionality, addressing bit-level mapping within the **PCI Express Configuration Space Header** for both physical and virtual functions. It defines the implementation-specific requirement of returning **FFFFh** for non-existent functions or **Virtual Functions (VFs)** to ensure standard hardware discovery and manufacturer identification.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1.2 Device ID Register (Offset 02h)
children.description: This section specifies the 16-bit **Device ID Register** located at configuration offset `02h`, defining its role in identifying unique **PCIe device functions** for system enumeration. It details the **read-only register format**, bit-mapping specifications, and hardware identification protocols necessary for **driver-to-device matching** and system-level peripheral configuration.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1.3 Command Register (Offset 04h)
children.description: This section defines the PCIe Command Register (Offset 04h) layout and bit-level implementation requirements, including specific behaviors for Virtual Functions (VFs) versus non-VFs. It addresses technical specifications for Device ID reporting and legacy software compatibility, clarifying how the Command Register enables or disables fundamental device features like I/O and Memory Space.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1.4 Status Register (Offset 06h)
children.description: This section defines the PCIe Status Register (Offset 06h) layout and bit-level implementation requirements, including the optional Immediate Readiness bit. It details operational triggers for parity errors, aborts, and capability signaling, answering how software determines function readiness and identifies hardware-level transaction status.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1.5 Revision ID Register (Offset 08h)
children.description: This section defines the 8-bit **Revision ID Register (Offset 08h)**, detailing register fields, read/write access attributes, and default values. It specifies the hardware versioning identifier, helping developers verify device stepping and compatibility during system initialization.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1.6 Class Code Register (Offset 09h)
children.description: This section defines bits 13-15 of the PCI Configuration Space Status Register, detailing error reporting mechanisms including Received Master Abort, Signaled System Error, and Detected Parity Error. It specifies triggering conditions, RW1C (Read-Write-1-to-Clear) attributes, and hardware requirements for Type 0 and Type 1 headers during TLP processing or error message transmission.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1.7 Cache Line Size Register (Offset 0Ch)
children.description: This section defines the Cache Line Size register (offset 0Ch), detailing its 8-bit read/write field used by the system to configure cache line granularity. It explains how this register informs master devices of the cache stripe system architecture to optimize burst-mode data transfers and memory access efficiency within the PCIe configuration space.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1.8 Latency Timer Register (Offset 0Dh)
children.description: This section defines the 8-bit Latency Timer register, detailing its function in establishing the maximum duration a PCI master can hold the bus after bus grant. It explains how this value determines bus tenure limits and burst termination to ensure equitable bandwidth sharing among PCI devices.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1.9 Header Type Register (Offset 0Eh)
children.description: This section defines the **Class Code Register** (bits 0–23) within the PCI Configuration Header, detailing the **Programming Interface**, **Sub-Class Code**, and **Base Class Code** descriptors used to identify device functionality. It further clarifies that the **Cache Line Size register** is a legacy-only, read-write field that carries no functional impact on standard PCI Express device behavior.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1.10 BIST Register (Offset 0Fh)
children.description: This section details the header layout encoding and the Multi-Function Device bit within the PCI Configuration Space Header Type register. It defines how software identifies header types (Type 0 vs. Type 1) and determines whether to probe for additional device functions.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1.11 Capabilities Pointer (Offset 34h)
children.description: This section defines the 8-bit Capabilities Pointer field in the PCI Configuration Space header, which specifies the offset for the first entry in the linked list of extended capability structures. It explains how system software traverses this pointer to discover and initialize optional hardware features, such as Power Management or PCI Express specifications, ensuring compatibility and configuration compliance.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1.12 Interrupt Line Register (Offset 3Ch)
children.description: This section defines the BIST (Built-In Self-Test) Register, detailing Completion Code, Start BIST, and BIST Capable bit fields and their respective hardware attributes. It explains how software initiates device self-testing and interprets failure status within the PCI Express configuration space.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1.13 Interrupt Pin Register (Offset 3Dh)
children.description: This section details the 8-bit **Interrupt Pin Register (Offset 3Dh)** within the PCI configuration space, specifying how hardware identifies the **legacy interrupt pin (INTA#–INTD#)** used by a device. It answers how a device maps its interrupt signal to the system’s interrupt controller and defines the read-only constraints for this field during software initialization.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.1.14 Error Registers
children.description: This section delineates the configuration of the **Interrupt Pin register** within PCIe architecture, detailing legacy interrupt assignment rules (INTA-INTD) for Single-Function and Multi-Function Devices. It specifies handling requirements for **Virtual Functions (VFs)** and identifies relevant **Error Control/Status register** references, clarifying how functions map legacy interrupts and apply architectural compliance constraints.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.2 Type 0 Configuration Space Header
children.description: This section details the Type 0 PCI Configuration Space header, specifically covering Base Address Registers (BARs), Cardbus CIS Pointers, Subsystem IDs, Expansion ROM, and latency timing registers. It answers technical specifications regarding how system software identifies, maps, and configures device-specific resources and memory-mapped I/O apertures for endpoint functions.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.2.1 Base Address Registers (Offset 10h - 24h)
children.description: This section defines the Type 0 Configuration Space Header Base Address Registers (BARs) located at offsets 10h–24h within the PCI Express configuration space. It specifies the mechanism system software uses to determine memory resource requirements and map device functions into the system address space to ensure consistent boot-time configuration.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.2.2 Cardbus CIS Pointer Register (Offset 28h)
children.description: This section defines the CardBus CIS Pointer Register at offset 28h, detailing the memory address space mapping used to identify Card Information Structure (CIS) metadata. It explains how system firmware and drivers access device-specific configuration pointers to facilitate proper Plug and Play resource allocation for CardBus controllers.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.2.3 Subsystem Vendor ID Register/Subsystem ID Register (Offset 2Ch/2Eh)
children.description: This section defines the mandatory architectural requirement that PCI Express components must hardwire Subsystem Vendor ID and Subsystem ID registers (Offset 2Ch/2Eh) to zero. It additionally provides implementation guidance for organizing MMIO space attributes to optimize TLP generation, memory operation performance, and hardware interoperability.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.2.4 Expansion ROM Base Address Register (Offset 30h)
children.description: This section defines the implementation requirements and initialization procedures for the **Expansion ROM Base Address Register**, **Subsystem Vendor ID (SVID)**, and **Subsystem ID (SID)**. It clarifies that these registers uniquely identify PCI product assemblies when Vendor and Device IDs are identical, while specifying that values must be pre-loaded before a Function becomes **Configuration-Ready**.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.2.5 Min_Gnt Register/Max_Lat Register (Offset 3Eh/3Fh)
children.description: This section defines the **Min_Gnt** and **Max_Lat** registers (Offset 3Eh/3Fh), detailing their status as obsolete for **PCI Express** devices and their historical role in configuring **Expansion ROM Base Address** space. It specifies implementation requirements for address mapping, size discovery via **all-1s write-back** mechanisms, and conditions for **hardwiring to zero**.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.3 Type 1 Configuration Space Header
children.description: This section defines the Type 1 Configuration Space header’s **Expansion ROM Base Address register**, detailing its memory-mapping mechanics, alignment requirements, and software-based size negotiation. It specifies implementation constraints for PCI versus PCI Express, architectural dependencies, and protocols for determining address space requests via read-write mask probing.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.3.1 Type 1 Base Address Registers (Offset 10h-14h)
children.description: This section details the specification and functional behavior of Type 1 Configuration Space Base Address Registers (BARs), covering BAR assignment, address range mapping, and type identification. It answers how PCI-to-PCI bridges configure memory and I/O resource windows through address decoding and bit-field management.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.3.2 Primary Bus Number Register (Offset 18h)
children.description: This section specifies the 8-bit **Primary Bus Number Register** (offset 18h) within the PCI Configuration Space, which identifies the bus number of the path leading to the root complex or host bus. It addresses how the system enumerates hierarchical topologies and determines the identification number assigned to the primary interface of a PCI-to-PCI bridge.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.3.3 Secondary Bus Number Register (Offset 19h)
children.description: This section defines the 8-bit **Secondary Bus Number Register** (Offset 19h) within the **PCI-to-PCI Bridge configuration space**, functioning as a critical identifier for the subordinate PCIe bus segment. It specifies how the register initializes the **bus numbering hierarchy** and answers questions regarding how a bridge identifies its downstream interface during **PCI enumeration and transaction routing**.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.3.4 Subordinate Bus Number Register (Offset 1Ah)
children.description: This section defines the Subordinate Bus Number Register, which specifies the highest bus number subordinate to a PCI-to-PCI bridge. It details hardware configuration requirements and answers how the system software determines the architectural range of secondary buses managed by a specific bridge device.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.3.5 Secondary Latency Timer (Offset 1Bh)
children.description: This section details the configuration registers for PCI Express-to-PCI bridge Type 1 headers, specifically addressing the **Secondary Latency Timer**, **Secondary Bus Number**, and **Subordinate Bus Number**. It defines how these registers facilitate ID-routed TLP forwarding and bridge interface connectivity while maintaining compatibility with legacy PCI software requirements.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.3.6 I/O Base/I/O Limit Registers(Offset 1Ch/1Dh)
children.description: This section defines the I/O Base and I/O Limit registers, specifying how PCI-to-PCI bridges configure address ranges for I/O transactions. It details bit-field mappings and alignment requirements, explaining how the hardware determines whether a specific I/O address falls within the bridge's designated upstream or downstream forwarding paths.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.3.7 Secondary Status Register (Offset 1Eh)
children.description: This section defines the functionality and address decoding logic for PCI-to-PCI bridge I/O Base and Limit registers. It specifies 4 KB boundary alignment requirements, read-only implementation criteria for optional ranges, and how configuration software manages address forwarding across a bridge.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.3.8 Memory Base Register/Memory Limit Register(Offset 20h/22h)
children.description: This section defines the 16-bit Memory Base and Memory Limit registers used to program non-prefetchable memory-mapped I/O windows within PCI-to-PCI Bridge architectures. It specifies how these registers establish address decoding ranges for downstream devices and addresses configuration requirements for managing system memory resource allocation.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.3.9 64-bit Memory Base/64-bit Memory Limit Registers (Offset 24h/26h) and 64-bit Base Upper 32 Bits/64-bit Limit Upper 32 Bits Registers (Offset 28h/2Ch)
children.description: This section defines the 64-bit Memory Base and Limit registers, including their upper 32-bit extensions, used by PCI-to-PCI bridges to manage memory-mapped address ranges for transaction forwarding. It provides technical specifications for status register bits—specifically Received Master Abort, Received System Error, and Detected Parity Error—to explain how bridges handle error conditions and TLP processing.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.3.10 64-bit Base Upper 32 Bits/64-bit Limit Upper 32 Bits Registers (Offset 28h/2Ch)
children.description: This section details the 64-bit base and limit upper address registers (offsets 28h and 2Ch), providing specifications for extended memory mapping in 64-bit architectures. It answers how software configures high-order memory boundary constraints and address space upper bounds for system resources.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.3.11 I/O Base Upper 16 Bits/I/O Limit Upper 16 Bits Registers (Offset 30h/32h)
children.description: This section details the I/O Base Upper 16 Bits and I/O Limit Upper 16 Bits registers located at offsets 30h and 32h, which facilitate 32-bit I/O addressing within PCI bridge configurations. It explains how these registers extend I/O address ranges beyond the standard 16-bit limit, answering questions regarding the software-defined mapping, upper-limit configuration, and address range allocation for downstream I/O devices.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.3.12 Expansion ROM Base Address Register (Offset 38h)
children.description: This section specifies the configuration and address decoding requirements for the Expansion ROM Base Address Register (offset 38h), detailing 1 MB alignment rules and the implementation of 64-bit memory mapping. It addresses how bridge hardware and system software manage memory-mapped secondary side ranges and register support for 64-bit addressing.

children.title: Section 7.5.1.3.13 Bridge Control Register (Offset 3Eh)
children.description: This section details the Bridge Control Register (offset 3Eh) for Type 1 Configuration Space headers, defining control bits associated with secondary interface parity, error signaling, and bridge-specific decoding. It addresses how to manage bus-level bridge behavior, including ISA/VGA enable features, discard timers, and secondary bus resets within PCI/PCIe architectures.

children.title: Section 7.5.2 PCI Power Management Capability Structure
children.description: This section details the PCI Power Management Capability structure for non-Virtual Function (VF) PCI Express devices, covering mandatory architectural requirements and register definitions. It addresses the functionality of legacy PCI-to-PCI Bridge registers, specifically clarifying that bits 7–11 are reserved or hardwired to 0b as they do not apply to the PCIe specification.

children.title: Section 7.5.2.1 Power Management Capabilities Register (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section specifies the Power Management Capabilities Register architecture, including bit-level definitions for `Capability_ID`, `Next Capability Pointer`, and power state transition features like `Immediate_Readiness_on_Return_to_D0`. It answers how PCI Express functions identify, navigate, and report their specific power management attributes within the configuration space.

children.title: Section 7.5.2.2 Power Management Control/Status Register (Offset 04h)
children.description: This section details the Power Management Control/Status Register (PMCSR) at offset 04h, defining bit-fields for `PowerState`, `PME_En`, and `PME_Status`. It specifies technical requirements for preserving PME context during D3Cold transitions and explains how system software manages PCI function power states and wakeup event signaling.

children.title: Section 7.5.2.3 Power Management Data Register (Offset 07h)
children.description: This section details the Power Management Data Register (Offset 07h), covering the functional behavior of the `No_Soft_Reset` bit, `PME_En` state persistence, and `Data_Select` indexing. It addresses how transitions from D3Hot to D0 affect internal state, PME generation requirements, and the reporting of device-specific power indicators.

children.title: Section 7.5.3 PCI Express Capability Structure
children.description: This section specifies procedures for reporting power consumption and heat dissipation within the PCI Express Capability Structure. It clarifies how to calculate power metrics for multi-function devices and external power sources to facilitate system-level thermal and power management.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.1 PCI Express Capability List Register (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section defines the implementation requirements and hardwiring rules for PCIe capability registers, specifically covering Device, Link, and Slot configuration spaces for Root Ports, Switches, and Endpoints. It clarifies when registers such as Device Control 2, Link Capabilities 2, and Slot Status must be active or hardwired to zero based on function type and capability reporting requirements.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.2 PCI Express Capabilities Register (Offset 02h)
children.description: This section defines the PCI Express Capabilities Register, detailing the structure and bit-level implementation of the Capability ID, Next Capability Pointer, Capability Version, and device-specific flags. It answers questions regarding how the PCI Express capability structure is identified within the configuration space, how capabilities are linked, and how specific hardware features like port types and flit modes are signaled to the system.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.3 Device Capabilities Register (Offset 04h)
children.description: Section 7.5.3.3 defines the PCI Express Device Capabilities Register, focusing on hardware-specific parameters such as **Max_Payload_Size**, **Flit Mode**, **Endpoint Latency**, and **Function Level Reset**. This section answers questions regarding a device's architectural limitations and functional support capabilities established during the PCI Express configuration process.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.4 Device Control Register (Offset 08h)
children.description: This section details the PCIe Device Control Register (offset 08h), covering operational parameters like error reporting enables, Max_Payload_Size, and Function Level Reset. It provides bit-level definitions and attributes to explain how these settings regulate device-specific PCI Express behavior and error messaging.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.5 Device Status Register (Offset 0Ah)
children.description: This section details the **Device Status Register (Offset 0Ah)**, covering bit-level definitions for PCIe error reporting, including **Correctable, Non-Fatal, and Fatal Error Detected** flags. It specifies the **RW1C** (Read-Write-1-to-Clear) attributes and behavioral requirements for functions regarding hardware error logging and **Advanced Error Handling** compliance.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.6 Link Capabilities Register (Offset 0Ch)
children.description: This section defines the **Link Capabilities Register** by detailing status bits for **Fatal Error Detection**, **Unsupported Request (UR)** status, **AUX Power** detection, and **Transactions Pending** states. It specifies **RW1C** and **RO** access attributes, clarifies error logging behavior for **Advanced Error Handling**, and explains how Functions identify incomplete **Non-Posted Requests**.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.7 Link Control Register (Offset 10h)
children.description: This section defines the PCIe Link Control Register configuration and mandates the ASPM Optionality Compliance bit for verifying hardware adherence to power management specifications. It addresses the technical requirements for ASPM implementation, explains how system software validates compliance, and outlines legacy hardware treatment standards.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.8 Link Status Register (Offset 12h)
children.description: This section defines the PCI Express Link Status Register (Offset 12h) and the mechanism for determining common clocking configurations for Link-connected components. It details the Slot Clock Configuration bit and the interaction between system-side hardware and adapter-level clock sourcing to establish synchronous timing across a PCIe connector.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.9 Slot Capabilities Register (Offset 14h)
children.description: This section defines the **Slot Capabilities Register** within the **PCI Express** architecture, detailing bit-field specifications for hardware features like **Hot-Plug**, **Power Indicators**, and **Electromechanical Interlocks**. It establishes configuration requirements and register behaviors for downstream ports, specifically addressing how identifying physical slot properties and autonomous link bandwidth changes are managed in compliance with PCIe standards.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.10 Slot Control Register (Offset 18h)
children.description: This section details the configuration and operational fields of the PCIe Slot Control Register, including Slot Power Limit Scale, Electromechanical Interlock status, and Physical Slot Number assignment. It defines the implementation requirements and hardware initialization behaviors for managing hot-plug capable ports and slot-specific power messaging.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.11 Slot Status Register (Offset 1Ah)
children.description: This section specifies the Slot Status Register (Offset 1Ah) configuration, covering Power Controller operations, Electromechanical Interlock control, Data Link Layer state reporting, and Auto Slot Power Limit management. It defines the register's bit-level functionality, including read/write attributes and implementation requirements for presence detection and status notification mechanisms.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.12 Root Control Register (Offset 1Ch)
children.description: This section defines the **Root Control Register (Offset 1Ch)** fields specifically for **Electromechanical Interlock Status** and **Data Link Layer State Changed** events in PCI Express. It addresses how software monitors hot-plug slot presence, assesses link state transitions, and manages hardware-level connectivity through status bits.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.13 Root Capabilities Register (Offset 1Eh)
children.description: This section specifies the bit-level architecture of the Root Capabilities Register, detailing mandatory power management and messaging support fields within the PCIe configuration space. It defines the implementation requirements for hardware-level capabilities, addressing how a Root Complex signals and manages system-level error and event reporting.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.14 Root Status Register (Offset 20h)
children.description: This section defines the "No NFM Subtree Below This Root Port" bit within the Root Status Register and the "Configuration RRS Software Visibility" capability in the Root Capabilities Register. It specifies the operational requirements for handling non-posted requests passing through a Root Port and outlines how software identifies support for Request Retry Status (RRS) in response to configuration requests.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.15 Device Capabilities 2 Register (Offset 24h)
children.description: This section defines the PCIe Device Capabilities 2 Register (offset 24h), detailing hardware support for features such as Completion Timeout programmability, AtomicOp routing, LTR, TPH, and 10-Bit Tag mechanisms. It specifies bit-level assignments and functional applicability to help engineers determine valid configuration settings for Root Ports, Endpoints, and Bridges.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.16 Device Control 2 Register (Offset 28h)
children.description: This section defines the **Device Control 2 Register (Offset 28h)** in **PCI Express** architectures, detailing bit-level configuration for **Completion Timeout**, **AtomicOp** operations, **LTR**, and **OBFF** functionalities. It specifies hardware requirements for these control bits, including **RW** (Read/Write) and **RsvdP** (Reserved and Preserved) attributes, to clarify how system software manages device behavior and request handling.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.17 Device Status 2 Register (Offset 2Ah)
children.description: This section details the **Device Status 2 Register** (Offset 2Ah), specifically defining **10-Bit Tag Requester Enable** requirements for non-UIO requests and **OBFF (Optimized Buffer Flush/Fill) Enable** signaling configurations. It provides technical specifications on bit-level attributes, hardware implementation defaults, and multi-function device behavior regarding these power management and requester capability features.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.18 Link Capabilities 2 Register (Offset 2Ch)
children.description: This section defines the **Link Capabilities 2 Register**, detailing technical specifications for **PCIe Link speeds** (2.5 GT/s to 128.0 GT/s+), **Crosslink support**, and **Retimer detection** mechanisms. It answers queries regarding physical layer architectural support, multi-function device compliance, and signaling requirements for higher-generation data rates.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.19 Link Control 2 Register (Offset 30h)
children.description: This section details the **Link Control 2 Register (Offset 30h)**, specifically defining the **Target Link Speed** field used to restrict PCIe link operational speeds. It provides technical specifications for managing hardware-based link behavior, including **Compliance** modes, **De-emphasis**, and **Transmit Margin** settings, explaining how software should utilize **Supported Link Speeds Vectors** for future-proof hardware interoperability.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.20 Link Status 2 Register (Offset 32h)
children.description: This section defines the **Link Status 2 Register (Offset 32h)**, encompassing bit-level indicators for **PCIe 8.0 GT/s equalization progress**, **Retimer presence/detection**, and **Link speed de-emphasis levels**. It clarifies implementation requirements for multi-function devices, **Virtual Functions (VFs)**, and crosslink status to facilitate link initialization and maintenance diagnostics.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.21 Slot Capabilities 2 Register (Offset 34h)
children.description: This section defines the **Component Present** state logic for the **Slot Capabilities 2 Register** (Offset 34h), detailing the integration of **in-band** and **out-of-band (OOB) presence detect (PD)** mechanisms. It addresses technical requirements for handling signaling race conditions between the **LTSSM Link Up state** and **async removal or hot-add** events.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.22 Slot Control 2 Register (Offset 38h)
children.description: This section defines the Slot Control 2 Register (offset 38h) within the PCI Express capability structure, detailing bit-level specifications for managing slot power, link speeds, and emergency power reduction. It answers operational questions regarding how system software controls physical slot configuration, hardware notification signals, and power management states for downstream ports.

children.title: Section 7.5.3.23 Slot Status 2 Register (Offset 3Ah)
children.description: This section defines the **Slot Status 2 Register** (Offset 3Ah) within the **PCI Express** configuration space, detailing the **In-Band PD Disable** support and **SCap2 OOB PD Supported** encodings. It specifies the hardware initialization requirements for presence detect mechanisms and clarifies that, when no capabilities are implemented, the register must be treated as **RsvdP** or **RsvdZ**.

children.title: Section 7.6 PCI Express Extended Capabilities
children.description: Section 7.6 details the **PCI Express Extended Capabilities** architecture, specifically defining the **Slot Capabilities 2 Register** and its bit-level attributes for **In-Band PD Disable** and **OOB PD** (Out-of-Band Presence Detect) support. This section clarifies hardware implementation requirements, register access constraints, and status reporting mechanisms for PCIe physical slots.

children.title: Section 7.6.1 Extended Capabilities in Configuration Space
children.description: This section defines the PCIe Extended Configuration Space, detailing the linked-list structure of Extended Capability structures and their 4KB memory-mapped access mechanism. It explains how to traverse capability registers, identify specific hardware features, and manage extended configuration offsets beyond the legacy 256-byte limit.

children.title: Section 7.6.2 Extended Capabilities in the Root Complex Register Block
children.description: This section details the implementation of PCI Express Extended Capabilities within the Root Complex Register Block, focusing on structure, mapping, and header requirements. It addresses how the Root Complex organizes capability structures, defines register accessibility, and identifies the specific mandatory and optional capabilities supported within the Root Complex Register Block address space.

children.title: Section 7.6.3 PCI Express Extended Capability Header
children.description: This section defines the structural requirements for the PCI Express Extended Capability Header, detailing register layouts for the Extended Configuration Space and Root Complex Register Block. It specifies bit-field definitions—including Capability ID, Version, and Next Capability Offset—and establishes the signaling conventions for indicating the absence of Extended Capabilities.

children.title: Section 7.7 PCI and PCIe Capabilities Required by the Base Spec in Some Situations
children.description: This section details mandatory PCI and PCIe capability structures, including MSI/MSI-X interrupt mechanisms, Link speed-specific physical layer enhancements (up to 128.0 GT/s), and advanced features like Flit logging, Lane Margining, and ACS. It defines the architectural requirements for implementing these extended capabilities to ensure compliance with the PCI Express Base Specification in specific system configurations.

children.title: Section 7.7.1 MSI Capability Structures
children.description: This section defines the MSI Capability structure, focusing on the Next Capability Offset field, versioning rules for software compatibility, and mandatory interrupt implementation requirements for PCI Express Endpoints. It specifies technical constraints for the linked list of capabilities, including register bit-field definitions, offset alignment, and the necessity of MSI or MSI-X support for generating interrupts.

children.title: Section 7.7.1.1 MSI Capability Header (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section defines the Message Signaled Interrupt (MSI) Capability Header structure, detailing fields like the Capability ID, Next Pointer, and Message Control register. It explains how PCI devices identify MSI support and manage interrupt configuration through memory-mapped signaling.

children.title: Section 7.7.1.2 Message Control Register for MSI (Offset 02h)
children.description: This section defines the MSI Capability structure, focusing on the Message Control Register, 32-bit versus 64-bit address requirements for Legacy and PCI Express Endpoints, and the MSI Capability Header layout. It specifies the configuration space requirements, capability IDs, and pointer mechanisms necessary for system software to identify and control Message Signaled Interrupts.

children.title: Section 7.7.1.3 Message Address Register for MSI (Offset 04h)
children.description: This section defines the Message Control Register fields for MSI configuration, specifically detailing vector allocation, 64-bit address support, and per-vector masking capabilities. It establishes technical requirements for SR-IOV and PCIe endpoints, explaining how these parameters determine a function's interrupt, address, and extended message data settings.

children.title: Section 7.7.1.4 Message Upper Address Register for MSI (Offset 08h)
children.description: This section defines the 32-bit Message Upper Address Register, providing the high-order bits for 64-bit PCI Express Message Signaled Interrupt (MSI) addressing. It details memory-mapped register functionality, bit definitions, and host system address space requirements for configuring interrupt delivery.

children.title: Section 7.7.1.5 Message Data Register for MSI (Offset 08h or 0Ch)
children.description: This section defines the **Message Address** and **Message Data Registers** for **Message Signaled Interrupts (MSI)**, detailing how devices initiate **DWORD Memory Write transactions**. It specifies the configuration for 32-bit versus 64-bit address formats and clarifies how system-level parameters dictate address alignment and data payload requirements for peripheral interrupts.

children.title: Section 7.7.1.6 Extended Message Data Register for MSI (Optional)
children.description: This section defines the Extended Message Data Register for MSI, detailing its bit-field architecture, support for 32-bit data incrementing, and configuration requirements within the PCI Express capability structure. It answers how systems manage extended interrupt messaging for devices requiring more than 32 message vectors.

children.title: Section 7.7.1.7 Mask Bits Register for MSI (Offset 0Ch or 10h
children.description: This section defines the Mask Bits and Extended Message Data registers within the PCI Express MSI Capability structure.
