children.description: This section defines the architectural structure and bit-level configuration of the Selective IDE Stream Capability Register, detailing the "Number of Address Association Register Blocks" and "Received IDE Fail Message" status bit. It provides technical specifications for hardware implementation and stream management within the Selective IDE (Integrity and Data Encryption) framework.

children.title: Section 7.9.26.5.2 Selective IDE Stream Control Register
children.description: This section defines the **Selective IDE Stream Control Register**, detailing configuration requirements and functional bits such as **Selective IDE Stream Enable**, **PCRC Enable**, and **Stream_ID**. It specifies the initialization sequence, security transitions to **Insecure state**, and protocol parameters involved in managing **IDE Stream** operation and **IDE_KM** triggering.

children.title: Section 7.9.26.5.3 Selective IDE Stream Status Register
children.description: This section defines the **Selective IDE Stream Status Register**, detailing the configuration of **Selected Algorithm**, **Traffic Class (TC)** mapping, and **Default Stream** designation for IDE TLP security. It answers how system software must manage stream-specific encryption parameters, TC association, and hardware-level hierarchy requirements for Endpoint Upstream Ports.

children.title: Section 7.9.26.5.4 Selective IDE RID Association Register Block
children.description: This section defines the Selective IDE RID Association Register Block, detailing the configuration of Resource Identifier (RID) associations for Integrated Device Encryption (IDE) streams. It specifies register offsets, field definitions, and procedural requirements for mapping PCI Express TLP traffic to specific IDE security associations.

children.title: Section 7.9.26.5.4.1 IDE RID Association Register 1
children.description: This section defines the **IDE RID Association Register 1** within the **Selective IDE RID Association Register Block**, detailing the configuration of **RID Limit** values and the association of **Stream_IDs** with **IDE Partner Ports**. It specifies the register layout, bit-level attributes for status and control—including **Selective IDE Stream State** and **IDE Fail Message** reporting—to facilitate secure PCIe stream management.

children.title: Section 7.9.26.5.4.2 IDE RID Association Register 2
children.description: This section specifies the IDE RID Association Register 2, detailing bit-field definitions for mapping Requestor IDs (RID) to Integrity and Data Encryption (IDE) streams. It defines the hardware-level mechanisms for associating PCI Express requester identifiers with security policies and encryption contexts during TLP processing.

children.title: Section 7.9.26.5.5 Selective IDE Address Association Register Block
children.description: This section details the Selective IDE (Integrity and Data Encryption) Address Association Register Block, covering register bit definitions, programming requirements for secure communication, and address filtering configurations. It answers how to implement selective hardware-based encryption by defining specific address ranges and identity-based association rules for IDE-compliant interfaces.

children.title: Section 7.9.26.5.5.1 IDE Address Association Register 1
children.description: This section defines the **IDE Address Association Register 1** and **IDE RID Association Register 2** within the **Selective IDE Address Association Register Block** of a PCIe interface. It specifies bit-level field definitions, including **Segment Base**, **RID Base**, **Memory Base Lower**, and **Memory Limit Lower**, to explain how software configures memory-mapped and requester ID (RID) ranges for IDE stream associations.

children.title: Section 7.9.26.5.5.2 IDE Address Association Register 2
children.description: This section defines the bit-fields and configuration parameters for the **IDE Address Association Register 2**, detailing how it manages memory-mapped I/O addressing and bus communication within the **Integrated Drive Electronics (IDE)** interface. It addresses how the system maps device-specific addresses and manages register-level association for proper hardware signal routing and data transfer initialization.

children.title: Section 7.9.26.5.5.3 IDE Address Association Register 3
children.description: This section defines the IDE Address Association Registers, specifically detailing the configuration of 64-bit Memory Base and Limit fields used to map Transmitted Address Routed TLPs to specific IDE Streams. It addresses how hardware validates address ranges and establishes association criteria for IDE Partner Port memory assignments.

children.title: Section 7.9.28 Null Extended Capability
children.description: This section defines the Null Capability and Null Extended Capability structures within PCI Express configuration spaces. It specifies their register layouts—including Capability ID 00h and Next Capability Pointers—and clarifies their role as passive, ignorable placeholders within linked capability lists.

children.title: Section 7.9.29 Streamlined Virtual Channel Extended Capability (SVC)
children.description: This section details the Streamlined Virtual Channel (SVC) Extended Capability, covering its register structure, optional implementation requirements, and mandatory role for supporting UIO-based devices. It defines the configuration space offsets, Capability IDs, and protocol constraints, specifically addressing how the SVC distinguishes itself from legacy VC or MFVC capabilities in Flit Mode environments.

children.title: Section 7.9.29.1 Streamlined Virtual Channel Extended Capability Header (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section defines the **Streamlined Virtual Channel Extended Capability Header** structure, detailing the register layout for the **PCI Express Extended Capability ID (0035h)**, **Capability Version**, and **Next Capability Offset**. It specifies how these read-only (RO) fields function within the **PCI Configuration Space** to enable linked-list traversal of PCIe Extended Capabilities.

children.title: Section 7.9.29.2 SVC Port Capability Register 1 (Offset 04h)
children.description: This section defines the configuration and status bits for the SVC Port Capability Register 1, detailing port-specific speed capabilities, link width, and protocol feature support. It addresses technical requirements for initializing port hardware and identifies which operational modes and traffic priorities are supported by the SVC interface.

children.title: Section 7.9.29.3 SVC Port Capability Register 2 (Offset 08h)
children.description: This section defines the **SVC Port Capability Register 2**, detailing bit-field mappings for **vendor-specific SVC port attributes** and **negotiation parameters**. It explains how to interpret **port-to-port communication capabilities**, hardware-defined **protocol limits**, and administrative state configurations within the **PCI Express** management hierarchy.

children.title: Section 7.9.29.4 SVC Port Control Register (Offset 0Ch)
children.description: This section defines the **SVC Port Control Register** and **SVC Port Capability Register 1** within the PCIe specification, detailing the allocation of **Virtual Channel (VC)** configuration bits. It answers how many additional VC resource registers are supported beyond the default VC0 and identifies the status of **VC Enablement** completion via specific offset-based register fields.

children.title: Section 7.9.29.5 SVC Port Status Register (Offset 10h)
children.description: This section defines the bit-fields and functional status indicators of the SVC Port Status Register (offset 10h) within the SVC interface architecture. It provides technical specifications for monitoring SVC link connectivity, error conditions, and operational state transitions to determine port health and communication readiness.

children.title: Section 7.9.29.6 SVC Resource Capability Register
children.description: This section defines the **SVC Port Control** and **SVC Port Status** registers, focusing on **VC enablement** procedures and the **Use VC/MFVC** bit configuration. It addresses how software orchestrates resource activation and interdependencies between **VC/MFVC capability** registers and **SVC resource control** registers.

children.title: Section 7.9.29.7 SVC Resource Control Register
children.description: This section defines the **SVC Resource Capability Register** architecture, specifically detailing **SVC VC Protocols Supported** (UIO vs. non-UIO) and **SVC VC ID** allocation. It answers how hardware enforces protocol-specific TLP restrictions and unique VC resource identification within the PCIe configuration space.

children.title: Section 7.9.29.8 SVC Resource Status Register
children.description: This section defines the control mechanisms for SVC (Shared Virtual Channel) resource management, specifically covering the **SVC Shared Flow Control Usage Limit Enable** and **SVC VC Enable** bits. It addresses operational prerequisites for enabling/disabling Virtual Channels, hardware-specific attribute requirements (RW/RO/RsvdP), and the necessary synchronization protocol for cross-link negotiation.

children.title: Section 7.9.30 MMIO Register Block Locator Extended Capability (MRBL)
children.description: This section details the MMIO Register Block Locator (MRBL) Extended Capability, covering the functional structure of the Header, Capabilities, and Locator registers. It defines the technical implementation for discovering memory-mapped I/O register blocks within a device, providing the necessary specifications for system software to locate and map these ranges.

children.title: Section 7.9.30.1 MRBL Extended Capability Header (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section defines the MMIO Register Block Locator (MRBL) Extended Capability, outlining technical specifications for discovering and mapping register blocks within PCIe Memory Space using the MRBL Extended Capability Header, Capabilities Register, and Locator Registers. It addresses how system software identifies custom register structures and validates their association with specific Base Address Registers (BARs).

children.title: Section 7.9.30.2 MRBL Capabilities Register (Offset 04h)
children.description: This section defines the MMIO Register Block Locator (MRBL) Extended Capability header and structure configuration within PCI Express space. It specifies bit-level requirements for the Capability ID (0036h), versioning, next-pointer offsets, and the MRBL structure length field, answering how to identify and navigate the MRBL capability structure in device configuration memory.

children.title: Section 7.9.30.3 MRBL Locator Register (Offset Varies)
children.description: This section defines the **MRBL Locator Register**, detailing the **Base Address Register (BIR)** index, **Register Block ID**, and **Register Block Offset** fields. It provides the technical specification for identifying and locating functional register blocks—such as **MCAP** or **MDVS**—within a device's memory-mapped space.

children.title: Chapter 8. Electrical Sub-Block
children.description: This section defines the electrical physical layer requirements, covering transmitter/receiver characteristics, channel tolerancing, and reference clock specifications for high-speed interoperability. It establishes technical criteria for signal integrity, jitter, and compliance testing to ensure robust data transmission across electrical sub-block interfaces.

children.title: Section 8.1 Electrical Specification Introduction
children.description: This section details absolute maximum ratings, DC characteristics, and AC timing parameters essential for hardware integration. It addresses operational limits, power consumption metrics, and signal integrity requirements for the device.

children.title: Section 8.2 Interoperability Criteria
children.description: This section details interoperability requirements for high-speed serial links, specifically covering signaling data rates and reference clock (Refclk) architectures. It defines industry-standard compliance parameters to ensure physical layer compatibility and system-level synchronization between interconnected components.

children.title: Section 8.2.2 Refclk Architectures
children.description: This section defines PCIe reference clock architectures and electrical specifications, covering NRZ/PAM4 signaling data rates, Nyquist frequencies, Spread Spectrum Clocking, and Bit Error Rate (BER/FBER) requirements. It addresses technical requirements for link operation, including channel compliance, transmitter/receiver equalization, and lane margining thresholds for 2.5 GT/s to 128.0 GT/s data rates.

children.title: Section 8.3 Transmitter Specification
children.description: Section 8.3 defines physical layer requirements for high-speed PCIe transmitters, covering voltage levels, jitter budgets, return loss, and PLL characteristics across data rates from 2.5 to 128 GT/s. This section answers technical specifications for signal integrity, transmitter margining, and compliance compliance testing protocols necessary for hardware validation.

children.title: Section 8.3.1 Measurement Setup for Characterizing Transmitters
children.description: This section defines the measurement setup for characterizing PCI Express transmitters, specifically addressing the physical limitations of probing DUT pins via breakout channels. It covers the use of low-jitter Refclk sources and multi-lane test environments to accurately simulate real-system signal integrity conditions at defined test points.

children.title: Section 8.3.1.1 Breakout and Replica Channels
children.description: This section defines the use of breakout and replica channels for de-embedding transmitter (Tx) voltage and jitter measurements to the DUT pins at TP1. It addresses design requirements for channel length matching, layer transitions, and specified insertion/return loss limits to ensure accurate signal integrity characterization at high signaling rates.

children.title: Section 8.3.2 Voltage Level Definitions
children.description: This section defines transmitter voltage and jitter measurement methodology through the use of breakout and replica channels to de-embed signals to the DUT pin. It addresses technical requirements for test board design, specifically channel impedance, insertion loss, return loss, and layer-matching guidelines to ensure consistent signaling measurements.

children.title: Section 8.3.3 Tx Voltage Parameters
children.description: Section 8.3.3 details Transmitter (Tx) Voltage Parameters, covering equalization specifications, preset definitions, and precise measurement methodologies for PCIe data rates ranging from 2.5 to 128.0 GT/s. It defines critical electrical requirements including VTX-DIFF-PP, coefficient tolerances, and EIEOS signal limits to ensure standardized physical layer compliance across varied signaling speeds.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.1 2.5 and 5.0 GT/s Transmitter Equalization
children.description: This section details PCIe transmitter equalization requirements for 2.5 and 5.0 GT/s data rates, focusing on coefficient settings, de-emphasis, and preshoot parameters. It defines the electrical specifications and control mechanisms necessary to maintain signal integrity and manage inter-symbol interference across high-speed serial links.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.2 8.0, 16.0, 32.0, 64.0, and 128.0 GT/s Transmitter Equalization
children.description: This section details PCIe transmitter (Tx) equalization requirements, defining voltage measurement methods for data rates from 2.5 to 128.0 GT/s, including de-emphasis, presets, and specific low-frequency compliance patterns. It answers how designers verify signal steady-state voltage swing and implement coefficient-based equalization to achieve fine-grained resolution control across high-speed link speeds.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.3 Tx Equalization Presets for 8.0, 16.0, 32.0, 64.0, and 128.0 GT/s
children.description: This section defines PCIe transmitter (Tx) equalization FIR filter architectures, coefficient constraints, and preset requirements for data rates ranging from 8.0 GT/s to 128.0 GT/s. It details the mathematical relationships between pre-shoots ($V_{c1}$, $V_{c2}$) and de-emphasis ($V_b$), establishing the architectural differences between 3-tap (8–32 GT/s) and 4-tap (64–128 GT/s) equalization models.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.4 Measuring Tx Equalization for 2.5 GT/s and 5.0 GT/s
children.description: This section defines transmitter (Tx) equalization requirements, focusing on coefficient ratios (c-2, c-1, c+1) and voltage swing parameters (Va/Vd, Vb/Vd, Vc/Vd) for PCIe presets at 2.5/5.0 GT/s and 64.0/128.0 GT/s. It provides normative signaling specifications and preset definitions to answer how transmitters must configure preshoot and de-emphasis to comply with full-swing and reduced-swing testing standards.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.5 Measuring Presets at 8.0, 16.0, 32.0, 64.0, and 128.0 GT/s
children.description: This section details the methodologies for measuring PCIe Tx equalization presets and coefficients at data rates from 8.0 to 128.0 GT/s using real-time oscilloscope analysis. It defines the Mean Square Error (MSE) minimization process for validating TxEQ coefficients ($c_{-2}, c_{-1}, c_0, c_{+1}$) by comparing measured versus reconstructed equalized step response waveforms.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.6 Method for Measuring VTX-DIFF-PP at 2.5 GT/s and 5.0 GT/s
children.description: This section details the oscilloscope-based test methodology for verifying **$V_{TX-DIFF-PP}$** (transmitter differential peak-to-peak voltage) at **2.5 GT/s and 5.0 GT/s** data rates. It provides standardized setup requirements, signal capture procedures, and compliance criteria necessary to ensure physical layer transmitter signal integrity within PCI Express specifications.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.7 Method for Measuring VTX-DIFF-PP at 8.0, 16.0, 32.0, 64.0, and 128.0 GT/s
children.description: This section details the oscilloscope-based characterization methodology for transmitter differential peak-to-peak voltage ($V_{TX-DIFF-PP}$) across PCIe data rates from 8.0 to 128.0 GT/s. It defines mandatory test setups, signal conditioning requirements, and measurement protocols to ensure compliance with architectural electrical specifications.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.8 Coefficient Range and Tolerance for 8.0, 16.0, 32.0, 64.0, and 128.0 GT/s
children.description: This section specifies the measurement requirements for $V_{TX-DIFF-PP}$ and $V_{TX-DIFF-PP-LOW}$ across 8.0 to 128.0 GT/s, detailing the calibration of PAM4 voltage levels and coefficient tolerances for transmitter presets. It provides technical definitions for signal swing measurements and compliance testing, addressing how transmitter output is validated and how coefficient ranges are communicated to the receiver.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.9 EIEOS and VTX-EIEOS-FS and VTX-EIEOS-RS Limits
children.description: This section defines specifications and voltage limits for Electrical Idle Exit Ordered Sets (EIEOS) and I-EIEOS used during PCIe data rate transitions. It provides technical requirements for $V_{TX-EIEOS-FS}$ and $V_{TX-EIEOS-RS}$ parameters, explaining how these signaling patterns ensure receiver squelch exit detection across full and reduced swing configurations.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.10 Reduced Swing Signaling at 8.0 GT/s and Higher Data Rates
children.description: This section defines the electrical requirements and physical layer specifications for Reduced Swing Signaling (RSS) used to improve power efficiency at data rates of 8.0 GT/s and beyond. It addresses how transmitter voltage levels are calibrated and constrained to maintain signal integrity while minimizing energy consumption within PCIe operational environments.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.11 Effective Tx Package Loss at 8.0, 16.0, 32.0, 64.0, and 128.0 GT/s
children.description: This section defines PCIe transmitter signal integrity requirements, focusing on the `ps21TX` parameter for measuring combined package insertion loss and driver bandwidth at test point TP1. It addresses implementation criteria for Reduced Swing Signaling (VTX-EIEOS-RS) and specifies how form factors must manage interoperability and voltage constraints across data rates from 8.0 to 128.0 GT/s.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.12 Linear Fit Pulse Response
children.description: This section outlines the test methodology for calculating the $ps21TX$ linear fit pulse response parameters $V101$ and $V111$ at 32.0 GT/s. It defines mandatory analog and digital filtering requirements for waveform conditioning and specifies compliance criteria for PCIe 6.0 and 64.0 GT/s signaling environments.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.13 Transmitter Signal-to Noise and Distortion Ratio (SNDRTX) for 64.0 and 128.0 GT/s
children.description: This section specifies the measurement procedure for Transmitter Signal-to-Noise and Distortion Ratio (SNDRTX) at 64.0 and 128.0 GT/s, detailing the use of Compliance Patterns, Multi Pulse Response Fit (MPRF) methodology, and RMS fitting error ($\sigma_e$) calculations. It addresses technical requirements for oscilloscope bandwidth and Bessel-Thomson filter roll-off settings necessary to mitigate high-frequency noise during transmitter characterization.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.14 Transmitter Ratio of Level Mismatch (RLM-TX) for 64.0 and 128.0 GT/s
children.description: This section defines the metrics for assessing PAM4 transmitter signal integrity, specifically outlining the mathematical frameworks for Signal-to-Noise-and-Distortion Ratio (SNDR) and the Ratio of Level Mismatch (RLM-TX) via pulse response peak analysis and voltage level calculations. It provides the necessary methodology—including multi-UI offset set definitions and specific algebraic formulas—to determine if a 64.0 or 128.0 GT/s transmitter meets linearity requirements for PCI Express 7.0 compliance.

children.title: Section 8.3.3.14.1 Multi Pulse Response Fit (MPRF) – PAM4 Voltage Variant
children.description: This section defines the Multi Pulse Response Fit (MPRF) method for PAM4 voltage linearity (RLM-TX) via specific pulse extraction and matrix-based weighting algorithms. It addresses how to quantify symbol level deviation through the normalization of pulse responses ($W_{sym0\_3}$, $W_{sym1}$, $W_{sym2}$) derived from the Linear Fit Pulse Response methodology.

children.title: Section 8.3.5 Tx Jitter Parameters
children.description: This section defines the technical procedures for calculating transmitter jitter parameters, including un-normalized pulse peaks and RLM-TX, while outlining constraints and encoding requirements for Tx voltage margining at high data rates up to 128.0 GT/s. It addresses how to compute critical signal metrics and specifies compliance mandates for transmitter voltage adjustment ranges and register programming.

children.title: Section 8.3.5.1 Post Processing Steps to Extract Jitter
children.description: This section details post-processing algorithms for extracting jitter from time-domain waveforms, focusing on signal conditioning, reference clock synchronization, and statistical decomposition. It addresses how to compute peak-to-peak and RMS jitter metrics while isolating deterministic, random, and periodic components from captured waveform data.

children.title: Section 8.3.5.2 Applying CTLE or De-embedding
children.description: This section specifies requirements for measuring PCI Express Tx jitter at 32.0, 64.0, and 128.0 GT/s, focusing on oscilloscope bandwidth, Bessel-Thomson filter characteristics, and crosstalk conditions. It establishes protocols for obtaining accurate jitter measurements referenced to the Tx pin, including essential filtering criteria to mitigate high-frequency noise during signal post-processing.

children.title: Section 8.3.5.3 Independent Refclk Measurement and Post Processing
children.description: This section details methodologies for independent reference clock (Refclk) frequency measurement, jitter analysis, and post-processing algorithms. It defines signal acquisition requirements, clock recovery techniques, and statistical data validation processes necessary to ensure timing compliance and signal integrity.

children.title: Section 8.3.5.4 Embedded and Non-Embedded Refclk Measurement and Post Processing
children.description: This section details the methodology for measuring uncorrelated jitter parameters at 64.0 GT/s using the Jitter Measurement and High Swing Toggle patterns. It covers technical requirements for data averaging, CTLE application for ISI mitigation, scope noise correction, and Q-scale application to confirm compliance with PAM4 transition jitter specifications.

children.title: Section 8.3.5.5 Behavioral CDR Characteristics
children.description: This section defines behavioral CDR testing protocols and post-processing requirements for various Refclk configurations, including embedded, non-embedded, and SRIS modes. It specifies measurement methodologies—such as 1-port versus 2-port analysis and CC vs. SRIS CDR filters—to determine how to characterize transmitter jitter when reference clock access varies.

children.title: Section 8.3.5.6 Data Dependent and Uncorrelated Jitter
children.description: This section defines Data Dependent Jitter (DDJ) and Uncorrelated Jitter (UJ), analyzing their distinct physical origins, measurement methodologies, and signal integrity impacts. It addresses how to differentiate between deterministic and random jitter components to optimize high-speed serial link performance, timing budgets, and bit error rate (BER) predictions.

children.title: Section 8.3.5.7 Data Dependent Jitter
children.description: This section defines the mathematical transfer function $H(s)$ and parameters—including damping ratios ($\zeta$) and corner frequencies ($\omega$)—required to characterize Data Dependent Jitter (DDJ) and Clock Data Recovery (CDR) behavior for 32.0, 64.0, and 128.0 GT/s signals. It details the methodology for partitioning transmitter jitter into recoverable data-dependent components and non-recoverable uncorrelated sources to ensure accurate timing margin analysis using the Dual Dirac Model.

children.title: Section 8.3.5.8 Uncorrelated Total Jitter and Deterministic Jitter (Dual Dirac Model) (TTX-UTJ and TTX-UDJDD)
children.description: This section defines the Dual Dirac model parameters TTX-UTJ (Uncorrelated Total Jitter) and TTX-UDJDD (Uncorrelated Deterministic Jitter) for quantifying high-speed signal integrity. It explains the decomposition of jitter distributions into random and deterministic components to answer how to characterize and budget link-level jitter performance accurately.

children.title: Section 8.3.5.9 Random Jitter (TTX-RJ) (informative)
children.description: This section defines Random Jitter (TTX-RJ) within high-speed signal integrity analysis, detailing statistical modeling, Gaussian distribution characteristics, and decomposition methodologies. It answers how to quantify unbounded stochastic timing variations and differentiate uncorrelated noise components from deterministic jitter sources.

children.title: Section 8.3.5.10 Uncorrelated Total and Deterministic PWJ (TTX-UPW-TJ and TTX-UPW-DJDD)
children.description: This section defines the derivation of Uncorrelated Total Jitter (TTX-UTJ) and Uncorrelated Deterministic Jitter (TTX-UDJDD) using Dual Dirac models and CDR-recovered data clocks. It explains how to distinguish random jitter (TTX-RJ) from data-dependent jitter (DDJ) by analyzing Q-scale PDF slopes and clarifies the methodology for calculating pulse width jitter (PWJ) across consecutive UI edges.

children.title: Section 8.3.6 Data Rate Dependent Parameters
children.description: This section defines the data rate-dependent transmitter specifications for PCI Express, detailing technical procedures for jitter decomposition and the conversion of legacy jitter metrics for 2.5 GT/s and 5.0 GT/s signaling. It explicitly defines Unit Interval (UI) tolerances across generations up to 128 GT/s while clarifying voltage swing definitions for PAM4 modulation.

children.title: Section 8.3.7 Tx and Rx Return Loss for 2.5, 5.0, 8.0, 16.0, and 32.0 GT/s
children.description: This section defines electrical signal integrity specifications for PCIe Transmitter (Tx) and Receiver (Rx) components, specifically covering AC common mode voltage, lane-to-lane output skew, and differential/common mode return loss across data rates from 2.5 to 128.0 GT/s. It establishes the mandatory technical thresholds and design constraints required to maintain signal impedance matching and timing synchronization within the Tx package and die.

children.title: Section 8.3.8 Tx and Rx Return Loss for 64.0 and 128.0 GT/s
children.description: This section defines the mandatory physical layer compliance masks for 64.0 and 128.0 GT/s PCI Express operations. It specifies technical requirements for Tx and Rx differential and common mode return loss, detailing pass/fail criteria relative to a 50-ohm reference impedance.

children.title: Section 8.3.9 Transmitter PLL Bandwidth and Peaking
children.description: This section defines transmitter PLL bandwidth and peaking transfer function requirements across PCIe data rates from 2.5 GT/s to 128 GT/s. It establishes critical jitter tolerance and clocking specifications, addressing how series capacitor integration and PLL loop design impact signal integrity and compliance.

children.title: Section 8.3.9.1 2.5 GT/s and 5.0 GT/s Tx PLL Bandwidth and Peaking
children.description: This section specifies the required Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) bandwidth and peaking transfer function constraints for 2.5 GT/s and 5.0 GT/s PCIe transmitter signaling. It establishes compliance boundaries to ensure jitter tolerance and signal integrity by defining frequency-domain characteristics and filter response parameters.

children.title: Section 8.3.9.2 8.0, 16.0, 32.0, 64.0, and 128.0 GT/s Tx PLL Bandwidth and Peaking
children.description: This section specifies Transmitter and Receiver PLL bandwidth and peaking requirements across PCIe data rates from 2.5 to 128.0 GT/s to constrain Refclk jitter propagation and ensure jitter tracking. It defines specific frequency ranges and decibel peaking limits to provide designers with architectural trade-offs between bandwidth and jitter amplification.

children.title: Section 8.3.9.3 Series Capacitors
children.description: This section outlines technical specifications for PCI Express series capacitors and PLL bandwidth requirements, specifically addressing 64.0 GT/s and 128.0 GT/s signaling. It explains how updated capacitance ranges mitigate DC wander and Refclk jitter, while providing standardized Tx parameters like common-mode voltage and electrical idle delta.

children.title: Section 8.3.10 Data Rate Independent Tx Parameters
children.description: This section defines data rate independent transmitter specifications for PCI Express, covering PLL bandwidth requirements for 64.0 and 128.0 GT/s, DC blocking capacitor constraints, and common-mode voltage parameters. It addresses technical requirements for managing Refclk jitter, mitigating DC wander in scrambled data streams, and establishing safe operational limits for Tx DC common-mode voltages.

children.title: Section 8.4 Receiver Specifications
children.description: Section 8.4 defines electrical compliance metrics for high-speed serial receivers, covering stressed eye calibration, jitter tolerance, lane margining, and low-frequency signaling requirements. It establishes mandatory test methodologies and physical layer performance parameters necessary to ensure reliable data recovery and signal integrity under stressed channel conditions.

children.title: Section 8.4.1 Receiver Stressed Eye Specification
children.description: This section specifies Transmitter (Tx) electrical performance requirements, including Electrical Idle transition timings, AC coupling capacitance, differential DC impedance, and short-circuit current limits. It answers technical questions regarding mandatory signaling stability, interoperability thresholds, and safety parameters for high-speed link initialization.

children.title: Section 8.4.1.2 Calibration Channel Insertion Loss Characteristics
children.description: This section details the calibration of Rx DUT measurement paths using replica channels to accurately represent insertion loss at the Rx pin. It defines critical impedance targets (85Ω/100Ω) and measurement reference points (TP1–TP3/TP2) necessary to characterize and de-embed signal source losses for stressed eye calibration.

children.title: Section 8.4.1.3 Post Processing Procedures
children.description: This section outlines the requirements for calibrating stressed eye characteristics at the accessible TP2, detailing the post-processing calculations necessary to model effects at the non-accessible TP2P reference point for 8.0 through 128.0 GT/s data rates. It specifies the technical necessity of accounting for behavioral Rx package and equalization effects to define valid Eye Height (EH) and Eye Width (EW) limits, supported by reference stack-up specifications for calibration channels.

children.title: Section 8.4.1.6 No Behavioral Rx Equalization for 2.5 and 5.0 GT/s
children.description: This section mandates that receivers for 2.5 GT/s and 5.0 GT/s PCIe data rates must not implement behavioral Rx equalization, detailing compliance requirements for signal integrity and signal processing. It clarifies specifications by answering whether adaptive equalization logic, such as CTLE or DFE, is permitted or required for lower-speed link training protocols.

children.title: Section 8.4.1.7 Behavioral Rx Equalization for 8.0, 16.0, 32.0, 64.0, and 128.0 GT/s
children.description: This section specifies behavioral Rx package model requirements for PCIe eye calibration at 8.0 through 128.0 GT/s, differentiating between Root Port and Non-Root device loss characteristics. It defines technical criteria for applying these models in stressed eye testing based on device topology, socketing assumptions, and signal integrity performance.

children.title: Section 8.4.1.8 Behavioral CTLE (8.0 and 16.0 GT/s)
children.description: This section defines the behavioral Continuous Time Linear Equalizer (CTLE) specifications for 8.0 and 16.0 GT/s PCIe signaling. It details the 1st order transfer function mathematical model, frequency-dependent gain parameters (ADC, $\omega_{P1}$, $\omega_{P2}$), and clarifies that these algorithmic requirements serve as channel compliance benchmarks rather than mandated architectural hardware implementations.

children.title: Section 8.4.1.9 Behavioral CTLE (32.0 and 64.0 GT/s)
children.description: This section defines the behavioral Continuous Time Linear Equalizer (CTLE) transfer functions, pole-zero locations, and adjustable DC gain (ADC) parameters for 32.0 and 64.0 GT/s PCIe receiver specifications. It provides the mathematical models, frequency constraints, and gain-curve requirements necessary for accurate time-domain post-processing and signal integrity simulation.

children.title: Section 8.4.1.10 Behavioral CTLE (128.0 GT/s)
children.description: This section defines the 128.0 GT/s behavioral Continuous Time Linear Equalizer (CTLE) for PCIe 7.0, detailing the transfer function $H(s)$, pole-zero locations, and the adjustable DC gain (ADC) range of 0 to -15 dB. It provides the mathematical framework and frequency parameters required to model receiver equalization performance at 128 GT/s data rates.

children.title: Section 8.4.1.11 Behavioral DFE (8.0, 16.0, 32.0, and 64.0 GT/s Only) and FFE/DFE (128.0 GT/s)
children.description: This section defines behavioral Decision Feedback Equalizer (DFE) constraints and tap limit specifications (d1, d2, d3, and h1/h0 ratios) for calibration at 8.0, 16.0, 32.0, and 64.0 GT/s. It addresses how to bound reference receiver performance and mitigate data error risks through optional precoding mechanisms at 32.0 GT/s.

children.title: Section 8.4.2 Stressed Eye Test
children.description: This section outlines the compliance testing procedures for stressed eye verification, covering jitter tolerance, EH/EW calibration, and clocking architectures for receiver (Rx) DUTs. It details the required calibration methodologies, test setups, and reference clock modes necessary to ensure signal integrity and receiver performance under stressed conditions.

children.title: Section 8.4.2.1 Procedure for Calibrating a Stressed EH/EW Eye
children.description: This section details the calibration procedure for a stressed EH/EW (Eye Height/Eye Width) at 128 GT/s, focusing on Rx reference equalizer constraints including 1-tap DFE magnitude limits and 29-tap FFE coefficient specifications. It defines the mathematical relationship for Rx FFE output, clarifies the use of an $L_\infty$ norm for coefficient limitation, and addresses technical requirements for mitigating non-main cursors in high-speed interconnect responses.

children.title: Section 8.4.2.1.1 Post Processing Tool Requirements
children.description: This section defines post-processing tool requirements for receiver tolerance testing, specifically establishing signal integrity parameters such as common mode noise voltage limits and SSC residual timing tolerances across data rates from 2.5 to 128.0 GT/s. It provides technical specifications for calibration, measurement reference points (TP1/TP2), and behavioral equalization necessary for validating high-speed serial link performance compliance.

children.title: Section 8.4.2.2 Procedure for Testing Rx DUT
children.description: This section defines the compliance verification methodology for Receiver (Rx) Devices Under Test (DUT), focusing on Sinusoidal Jitter (Sj) interference tolerance and stressed eye calibration. It details the procedural requirements for validating signal integrity margins against defined jitter masks and specifies the diagnostic testing parameters necessary to ensure Rx performance conformity.

children.title: Section 8.4.2.2.1 Sj Mask
children.description: This section defines the calibration methodology for the Sj (sinusoidal jitter) mask, detailing step pattern acquisition, Tx EQ preset selection, and stress-testing parameters for multiple data rates. It specifies the technical procedures for achieving target eye height (EH) and eye width (EW) at defined BER levels by balancing Rx stressed eye calibration, transmitter swing, and jitter injection.

children.title: Section 8.4.2.3 Receiver Refclk Modes
children.description: This section defines Receiver Reference Clock (Refclk) modes, specifically contrasting Common Refclk and Independent Refclk architectures. It addresses technical requirements for clock distribution, jitter tolerances, and frequency compensation mechanisms necessary to establish signal integrity between high-speed serial links.

children.title: Section 8.4.2.3.1 Common Refclk Mode
children.description: Section 8.4.2.3.1 defines the Common Clock (CC) Refclk Mode architecture, detailing technical requirements for synchronized clock distribution between the Generator and the Device Under Test (DUT). It answers how PCIe receivers manage dual clocking modes (CC vs. Independent Refclk) and identifies the applicable Sinusoidal Jitter (Sj) mask specifications required for 8.0 GT/s compliance testing.

children.title: Section 8.4.2.3.2 Independent Refclk Mode
children.description: This section details the Independent Refclk (IR) mode configuration for PCI Express Receiver jitter testing, specifically referencing Figures 8-57/8-58 and Table 8-12. It provides technical specifications for IR Refclk SSC requirements, Sj mask application, and Rx PLL bandwidth parameters (BWRX-PKG-PLL1/2) across data rates from 2.5 GT/s to 128.0 GT/s.

children.title: Section 8.4.3 Common Receiver Parameters
children.description: Section 8.4.3 defines essential high-speed serial Receiver performance specifications, detailing Unit Interval (UI) tolerances, Rx PLL bandwidths, and peaking parameters across data rates from 2.5 GT/s to 128.0 GT/s. It specifically provides standard values and technical constraints for Second-order PLL transfer bounding functions, answering how receiver clock recovery requirements scale with increasing data throughput.

children.title: Section 8.4.3.1 5.0 GT/s Exit From Idle Detect (EFI)
children.description: This section details the state machine transitions and signaling requirements for exiting the Idle Detect state at 5.0 GT/s data rates. It specifies the electrical compliance and handshake protocols necessary to transition from the Electrical Idle condition back into the Recovery or Detect states.

children.title: Section 8.4.3.2 Receiver Return Loss
children.description: Section 8.4.3.2 details PCIe Receiver Return Loss and impedance requirements, covering Rx DC single-ended impedance, high-impedance state definitions (ZRX-HIGH-IMP-DC), and common-mode settlement constraints. It specifies test patterns like the K28.7 “EIE” symbol for ensuring robust Rx detection during transitions between 2.5 GT/s and higher data rates (up to 128 GT/s).

children.title: Section 8.4.4 Lane Margining at the Receiver - Electrical Requirements
children.description: This section details electrical requirements for Lane Margining at the Receiver, encompassing Rx DC single-ended impedance, high-impedance state detection, and common-mode settling times. It addresses technical specifications for impedance calibration during Receiver Detect and performance considerations when transitioning between legacy 2.5/5.0 GT/s rates and higher data rates up to 128 GT/s using specific EIE pattern training sequences.

children.title: Section 8.4.5 Low Frequency and Miscellaneous Signaling Requirements
children.description: This section defines electrical compliance specifications for low-frequency signaling, including ESD protection, AC coupling, termination impedance, and Electrical Idle states. It addresses critical questions regarding transmitter/receiver circuit configuration, fault tolerance, and common-mode signal integrity required for robust link initialization and detection.

children.title: Section 8.4.5.3 Short Circuit Requirements
children.description: This section defines functional parameters for PCIe physical layer margining—including sample reporting methods, error counts, and bit-count calculations—while mandating compliance with ESDA/JEDEC standards for ESD protection and AC coupling requirements. It addresses how devices report signal integrity diagnostic data and specifies the electrical safety protocols necessary for PCIe lane hardware interface design.

children.title: Section 8.4.5.4 Transmitter and Receiver Termination
children.description: This section details electrical impedance matching requirements for high-speed serial links, specifically addressing on-die termination (ODT), differential signaling, and reflection mitigation. It answers how to correctly implement transmitter (TX) and receiver (RX) termination networks to maintain signal integrity and ensure proper common-mode voltage biasing.

children.title: Section 8.4.5.6 DC Common Mode Voltage
children.description: This section specifies the required DC common-mode voltage and differential impedance compliance criteria for PCIe transmitters and receivers during both active data transmission and Electrical Idle states. It defines mandated electrical performance parameters, impedance requirements during power-down or reset sequences, and the procedural transition requirements for entering Electrical Idle.

children.title: Section 8.4.5.7 Receiver Detection
children.description: This section details the PCIe Receiver Detection mechanism, focusing on the transient voltage analysis used to differentiate between a high-impedance state and a valid $Z_{RX-DC}$ load (40–60 Ω). It explains the procedural requirements for 2.5 GT/s transmitters, including common-mode voltage shifting and RC time-constant evaluation, to determine the presence of a connected receiver.

children.title: Section 8.5 Channel Tolerancing
children.description: This section defines channel tolerancing specifications and compliance requirements for high-speed serial links. It addresses key technical parameters such as channel operating margin (COM), insertion loss, return loss, and crosstalk, providing criteria to evaluate if a physical channel configuration meets performance standards.

children.title: Section 8.5.1 Channel Compliance Testing
children.description: This section specifies the protocol for PCIe Receiver Detection, detailing the common-mode voltage shift, RC time constant analysis, and impedance threshold requirements (ZRX-DC, ZRX-HIGH-IMP-DC). It defines the technical procedures for distinguishing between connected and disconnected loads at 2.5 GT/s based on differential pair charging rates.

children.title: Section 8.5.1.1 Behavioral Transmitter and Receiver Package Models
children.description: This section details behavioral transmitter and receiver package models for PCIe data rates ranging from 8.0 GT/s to 128.0 GT/s, specifying equalization architectures such as CTLE, DFE, and Rx FFE. It defines the methodology for modeling die and package loss, including CPIN and CPAD capacitive parasitics, for different device types like Root Ports versus endpoint devices.

children.title: Section 8.5.1.2 Measuring Package Performance (16.0 GT/s only)
children.description: This section defines the methodologies for verifying PCIe 16.0 GT/s package signal integrity, focusing on eye diagram analysis, jitter tolerance, and insertion loss compensation. It answers how to evaluate high-speed link performance and ensure compliance with standardized electrical test requirements for package-level connectivity.

children.title: Section 8.5.1.3 Simulation Tool Requirements
children.description: Section 8.5.1.3 defines simulation requirements for 128.0 GT/s reference package port configurations, detailing specific pin-to-pin crosstalk mappings for Victim, FEXT, and NEXT aggressors. This text explains the methodology for assessing package insertion loss and crosstalk compliance by substituting custom package designs into standardized reference channels to evaluate eye height and width performance.

children.title: Section 8.5.1.3.3 Simulation Tool Outputs
children.description: This section outlines requirements for high-speed channel link analysis, covering input specifications for S-parameter models, package behavior, termination impedance, and transmitter/receiver jitter and voltage parameters. It defines the technical standards necessary for simulating end-to-end connectivity, crosstalk, and stressed eye performance at data rates up to 128 GT/s.

children.title: Section 8.5.1.3.4 Open Source Simulation Tool
children.description: This section defines the mandatory open-source simulation tool for evaluating PCIe 64.0/128.0 GT/s channel performance through DFE coefficient optimization and statistical eye analysis. It details the methodology for adjusting sample points, incorporating behavioral Tx package models, and decomposing jitter (Rj/DjDD) and voltage parameters to determine pass/fail criteria at the Receiver die pad.

children.title: Section 8.5.1.4 Behavioral Transmitter Parameters
children.description: This section defines behavioral transmitter parameters, covering voltage and jitter derivation, equalization optimization, eye diagram compliance, common-mode noise characterization, and idle detection voltage verification. It answers technical requirements for validating PCIe physical layer signaling performance across data rates ranging from 8.0 to 128.0 GT/s.

children.title: Section 8.5.1.4.1 Deriving Voltage and Jitter Parameters
children.description: This section details the methodologies for deriving voltage and jitter parameters, specifically outlining DFE coefficient optimization, eye diagram metrics, and the use of reference simulation tools for 64.0 and 128.0 GT/s operations. It addresses how to decompose jitter into Rj and DjDD components and explains how to transform transmitter-referenced specifications into die-pad-referenced values for channel simulation accuracy.

children.title: Section 8.5.1.4.2 Optimizing Tx/Rx Equalization (8.0, 16.0, 32.0, 64.0, and 128.0 GT/s only)
children.description: This section defines critical Tx jitter and voltage parameters for 32.0, 64.0, and 128.0 GT/s PCIe data rates, including uncorrelated Rj, DjDD, and pulse-width jitter specifications. It provides technical reference values for behavioral Tx die pad signal edge rates and jitter component decomposition to ensure signal integrity during equalization optimization.

children.title: Section 8.5.1.4.3 Pass/Fail Eye Characteristics
children.description: This section establishes channel compliance criteria by defining eye mask specifications (EH/EW) for simulation-based pass/fail testing. It details application-specific package modeling, identifies alignment between channel tolerancing and Rx test limits, and provides reference values for GT/s-specific eye margins at the Receiver die pad.

children.title: Section 8.5.1.4.4 Characterizing Channel Common Mode Noise
children.description: This section outlines methodologies for characterizing common-mode (CM) noise in high-speed signaling channels using differential-to-common mode conversion ($S_{cd21}$) parameters and modal decomposition. It addresses how to quantify CM generation, identify mode conversion sources, and assess signal integrity impacts caused by asymmetry in interconnect topologies.

children.title: Section 8.5.1.4.5 Verifying VCH-IDLE-DET-DIFF-pp
children.description: This section prescribes technical specifications for 128G signaling, including Rx DFE/FFE coefficient constraints and sample granularity requirements. It addresses how to verify `VCH-IDLE-DET-DIFF-pp` and establishes the budgeting methodology for channel common-mode noise contributions to maintain signal integrity.

children.title: Section 8.6 Refclk Specifications
children.description: This section defines physical layer requirements for reference clock (Refclk) signals, including AC specifications, jitter measurement methodologies, and architectural filtering functions. It addresses technical requirements for Common Refclk (CC) architectures, defining the compliance limits and form factor constraints necessary to maintain signal integrity across various data rates.

children.title: Section 8.6.2 REFCLK AC Specifications
children.description: This section defines AC specifications and test methodologies for PCI Express Refclk across data rates ranging from 2.5 to 128.0 GT/s. It details technical requirements for signal integrity, including voltage parameters for EIEOS signaling and specific load termination configurations required for jitter measurement and system-level compliance.

children.title: Section 8.6.3 Data Rate Independent Refclk Parameters
children.description: This section defines data-rate-independent reference clock (Refclk) measurement parameters, specifically focusing on differential signal characteristics like duty cycle, period, rise/fall times, and ringback. It clarifies technical implementation constraints, such as T-TRANSPORT-DELAY for Common Refclk architectures and SSC modulation sources for SRIS mode.

children.title: Section 8.6.3.1 Low Frequency Refclk Jitter Limits
children.description: This section defines physical layer specifications for Refclk frequency, SSC modulation range, deviation limits for SRIS modes, and transport delay constraints. It establishes critical timing requirements for PCI Express architectures, specifying frequency tolerances and slew rate parameters necessary for system-level signal integrity.

children.title: Section 8.6.5 Filtering Functions Applied to Raw Data
children.description: This section details the filtering methodologies—specifically edge, PLL, and CDR-based jitter filtering—applied to raw Reference Clock (Refclk) data to determine effective jitter performance. It defines the differences between Common Refclk (CC) and Independent Refclk (IR) architectures and clarifies how implementers evaluate jitter limits to satisfy Receiver sample latch requirements.

children.title: Section 8.6.5.1 PLL Filter Transfer Function Example
children.description: This section derives the transfer function for a Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) loop filter, covering key technical concepts such as pole-zero placement, Laplace domain analysis, and Bode plot characteristics. It answers how filter component values influence loop stability, damping ratio, and the natural frequency of the PLL system.

children.title: Section 8.6.5.2 CDR Transfer Function Examples
children.description: This section details the behavioral modeling of second-order CDR and PLL transfer functions using damping factors ($\zeta$) and natural frequencies ($\omega_n$). It provides the mathematical relationship between natural frequency and 3 dB bandwidth, alongside specific examples for common reference clock architecture jitter tracking.

children.title: Section 8.6.6 Common Refclk Rx Architecture (CC)
children.description: This section details the Common Refclk (CC) architecture, utilizing shared reference clocks and PLL transfer functions to calculate differential jitter impact in PCIe data transmission. It specifically addresses how transmitter-to-receiver signal paths and delay mismatches are modeled to quantify jitter propagation and CDR performance across varying data rates.

children.title: Section 8.6.6.1 Determining the Number of PLL BW and peaking Combinations
children.description: This section specifies the systematic process for calculating valid Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) bandwidth and peaking combinations within a signal integrity or frequency synthesis design. It addresses requirements for choosing optimal loop filter constants, stability margins, and settling time parameters to ensure compliant clock synthesis performance.

children.title: Section 8.6.6.2 CDR and PLL BW and Peaking Limits for Common Refclk
children.description: This section specifies the combinatorial limits for Tx/Rx PLL bandwidth and peaking in Common Refclk (CC) architectures, detailing how these parameters integrate with CDR high-pass characteristics to determine the jitter transfer function. It quantifies the total number of corner case permutations across various PCIe data rates (2.5 GT/s to $N$ GT/s) to define the specific boundary conditions required for compliant jitter performance.

children.title: Section 8.6.7 Jitter Limits for Refclk Architectures
children.description: This section defines reference clock (Refclk) jitter specifications for Common Clock (CC) architectures across PCIe data rates from 2.5 GT/s to 128.0 GT/s. It details technical requirements for PLL/CDR bandwidth, peaking, and damping, while clarifying measurement methodologies (peak-to-peak vs. RMS) and filtering constraints for determining compliance.

children.title: Section 8.6.8 Form Factor Requirements for RefClock Architectures
children.description: This section defines interface-specific clocking architecture requirements (Common, SRNS, SRIS) and CLKREQ# signaling mandates for device-level form factor specifications. It provides technical templates for documenting jitter limits, architectural support statuses, and motherboard or add-in card constraints essential for system-level signal integrity.

children.title: Chapter 9. I/O Virtualization and Sharing
children.description: This section details the architectural frameworks, initialization sequences, and configuration procedures for PCIe SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization) and SIOV (Scalable I/O Virtualization). It addresses resource allocation, device pass-through, and hardware-assisted sharing mechanisms required to scale virtualized I/O performance in modern data center environments.

children.title: Section 9.1 IOV Architectural Overview
children.description: Section 9.1 provides a technical overview of IOV architectures, specifically contrasting the hardware-heavy Single Root I/O Virtualization (SR-IOV) with the software-defined, lower-complexity Scalable I/O Virtualization (SIOV). This section defines how these methodologies enable multiple System Images (SI) to share PCI Express hardware resources within a Root Complex (RC) environment.

children.title: Section 9.1.1 RID in Endpoints where a PF contains an SIOV Extended Capability Structure
children.description: This section details SIOV (Scalable I/O Virtualization) Endpoint addressing by comparing RID allocation methods: static SR-IOV stride-based offsets versus dynamic RID-to-SDI association. It explains how device architects can maximize SDI scaling within complex topologies like Flattening Portal Bridges or multi-Root Complex PCIe switches.

children.title: Section 9.1.2 SDI Reset
children.description: This section defines the algorithm for determining valid Requestor IDs (RIDs) for Scalable Device Interfaces (SDIs) by systematically excluding existing PFs, VFs, and Phantom/Shadow functions across the PCIe hierarchy. It answers how an SDI reset identifies available RID space and ensures non-interference with established SR-IOV or SIOV configuration structures during device-level reset procedures.

children.title: Section 9.2 SR-IOV Initialization and Resource Allocation
children.description: This section details the lifecycle management of SR-IOV resources, covering PCIe resource discovery, function-level reset mechanisms, and dynamic re-initialization procedures. It addresses critical questions regarding hardware configuration, device state recovery, and the systematic reallocation of Virtual Functions within a virtualized IO environment.

children.title: Section 9.2.1 SR-IOV Resource Discovery
children.description: Section 9.2.1 covers SR-IOV resource discovery, detailing the configuration of PCIe Virtual Functions (VF), function dependency mapping, and interrupt resource allocation. This section addresses how systems identify available I/O resources, manage hardware capabilities, and provision interrupts for virtualized network environments.

children.title: Section 9.2.1.1 Configuring SR-IOV Capabilities
children.description: This section defines security and operational requirements for resetting SR-IOV-capable SDIs, specifically mandating the scrubbing of sensitive host-accessible state and enforcing strict DMA, interrupt, and translation service (ATS/PRS) completion protocols. It governs how adapters must ensure hardware isolation and data sanitization during reset cycles to prevent information leakage and system instability between host assignments.

children.title: Section 9.2.1.1.1 Configuring the VF BAR Mechanisms
children.description: This section details SR-IOV configuration for VF BAR mechanisms, covering memory mapping, system page size alignment, and aperture sizing requirements. It explains how VF BARs function as multiples of standard PCI Memory Space BARs and establishes the process for determining VF memory aperture requirements.

children.title: Section 9.2.1.2 VF Discovery
children.description: This section outlines the mechanism for identifying Virtual Function (VF) Routing IDs through the SR-IOV extended capability, focusing on the interdependent configuration of `NumVFs`, `VF Stride`, `First VF Offset`, and `ARI Capable Hierarchy`. It defines the algorithmic process for address computation and specifies the constraints on modifying these registers to ensure proper PCI Express bus enumeration and memory space allocation.

children.title: Section 9.2.1.3 Function Dependency Lists
children.description: This section details PCI Express bus number allocation, SR-IOV configuration requirements, and the functional mapping of Virtual Functions (VFs). It establishes specific rules for handling Configuration Requests and Memory Space Enable (MSE) logic while defining how devices must communicate the availability or non-existence of specific functions within the fabric.

children.title: Section 9.2.1.4 Interrupt Resource Allocation
children.description: This section details SR-IOV interrupt resource allocation, specifying constraints for PF/VF MSI/MSI-X support, address isolation requirements, and the impact of Conventional Reset and Function Level Reset (FLR) on virtualized PCIe functions. It addresses how software manages function dependencies and the lifecycle of VFs during hardware reset events.

children.title: Section 9.2.2 SR-IOV Reset Mechanisms
children.description: Section 9.2.2 details SR-IOV reset mechanisms, covering Conventional Reset and Function Level Reset (FLR) protocols for Physical Functions (PFs) and Virtual Functions (VFs). It defines the specific hardware reset behaviors, scope of impact, and architectural requirements for restoring device state during SR-IOV lifecycle management.

children.title: Section 9.2.2.2 FLR That Targets a VF
Section 9.2.2.3 FLR That Targets a PF
children.description: This section outlines Function Level Reset (FLR) protocols, SR-IOV dependency management, and interrupt configurations (MSI/MSI-X) for PFs and VFs. It clarifies how reset mechanisms—specifically Conventional Reset and FLR—impact configuration space, BAR assignments, and the operational lifecycle of virtualized functions.

children.title: Section 9.2.3 IOV Re-initialization and Reallocation
children.description: This section defines SR-IOV reset behaviors and re-initialization protocols, covering Function Dependency Lists, interrupt configurations (MSI/MSI-X), and the impact of Conventional Resets versus Function Level Resets (FLR) on VFs. It addresses how hardware state transitions, VF persistence, and dependency requirements are managed during device resets and configuration cycles.

children.title: Section 9.3 SIOV Initialization and Resource Allocation
children.description: This section details the Scalable I/O Virtualization (SIOV) startup sequence, covering PCIe device discovery, Virtual Function (VF) provisioning, and memory-mapped I/O resource assignment. It addresses how the system enumerates SIOV-capable devices, initializes Work Queues (WQs), and manages the lifecycle of hardware-backed virtualized resources within the I/O subsystem.

children.title: Section 9.3.1 SIOV Resource Discovery
children.description: This section details the Scalable I/O Virtualization (SIOV) resource discovery process, architectural configuration of device capabilities, and MSI/MSI-X interrupt message resource allocation. It addresses how the system enumerates virtual function resources and provisions hardware-level interrupt mechanisms to facilitate efficient device virtualization.

children.title: Section 9.3.1.1 Configuring SIOV Capabilities
children.description: This section details the architectural configuration of Scalable I/O Virtualization (SIOV), focusing on the provisioning of Scalable Device Instances (SDIs), Virtual Interfaces (VIs), and Scalable Interface (SI) requirements. It addresses how to instantiate sub-functions and defines the operational dependencies and functional behaviors necessary for managing virtualized PCIe device resources.

children.title: Section 9.3.1.1.1.1 Behavior of a VI
children.description: This section details the lifecycle management of Virtual Functions (VFs) and Scalable I/O Virtualization (SIOV) Device Instances (SDIs), covering architectural state sanitization requirements, configuration persistence, and SDI enumeration. It answers how SIOV hardware handles state clearing for security, differentiates between architected and unarchitected configuration, and determines necessary Bus Numbers for Endpoint devices.

children.title: Section 9.3.1.1.1.2 Requirements of an SI
children.description: This section details the architectural requirements for presenting a Secondary Device Instance (SDI) as a Function to a System Image (SI) using SR-PCIM, focusing on Type 0 Header emulation, virtual BAR mapping, and page-based trap-and-emulate mechanisms. It defines how device-specific extensions manage memory mapping, software-assisted virtualization of configuration spaces, and MSI-X handling to allow independent SI interaction with virtualized PCI Express functions.

children.title: Section 9.3.1.2 MSI/MSI-X Interrupt Message Resource Allocation
children.description: This section details the virtualization of MSI/MSI-X interrupt resources and BAR management within an SR-PCIM framework, specifically addressing how System Interfaces (SIs) interact with virtualized SDIs through address emulation and page-table mapping. It answers how hardware resources are abstracted for Type 0 Header emulation and how software traps manage invalid memory pages to facilitate efficient device virtualization.

children.title: Section 9.4 SR-IOV Configuration
children.description: This section defines the SR-IOV configuration architecture, detailing PF/VF configuration space headers, PCIe extended capabilities, and standard PCI capability modifications for virtualized environments. It addresses how hardware-level support for single-root I/O virtualization is implemented and managed within the PCI Express configuration space hierarchy.

children.title: Section 9.4.3 SR-IOV Extended Capability
children.description: This section specifies the virtualization requirements for the SR-IOV Extended Capability, focusing on MSI/MSI-X interrupt handling, Type 0 Header presentation, and configuration space management for Virtual Instances (VIs) and Software-Defined Interfaces (SDIs). It clarifies how SR-PCIM manages interrupt message resources and identifies mechanisms for exposing isomorphic functionality when bridging physical device interfaces to software instances.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.1 SR-IOV Extended Capability Header (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section defines the PCIe SR-IOV Extended Capability Header structure, specifying the 16-bit Capability ID (0010h), versioning, and offset pointer mechanisms. It answers technical questions regarding the configuration space layout, register access permissions (RO/RW), and the bitwise mapping of SR-IOV Virtual Function (VF) control and status registers.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.2 SR-IOV Capabilities Register (04h)
children.description: This section details the SR-IOV Extended Capability Header and the bit-level layout of the SR-IOV Capabilities Register, including specifications for VF migration, ARI hierarchy, and Tag Requester support. It addresses how to identify SR-IOV extended capability structures and defines the functional attributes of the Physical Function (PF) within the PCI Express configuration space.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.2.2 ARI Capable Hierarchy Preserved
children.description: This section defines the criteria for the **ARI Capable Hierarchy Preserved** bit, detailing how **PCIe-to-PCIe bridges** and **Root Ports** handle **Alternative Routing-ID Interpretation (ARI)** across a device hierarchy. It addresses whether a hierarchical structure supports extended **Function numbers** beyond 7 and under what conditions **ARI-forwarding** must remain enabled for compliant **Transaction Layer Packet (TLP)** routing.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.2.3 VF Larger-Tag Requester Support
children.description: This section details PCI Express configuration requirements for VF Larger-Tag Requester support, including 14-Bit Tag Requester capabilities for VFs and the legacy status of MR-IOV-related fields. It clarifies the mandatory hardware dependencies between PF and VF capabilities, provides guidance for new hardware designs regarding migration interrupts, and defines reset behavior for ARI Capable Hierarchy.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.2.4 VF Migration Interrupt Message Number
children.description: This section details SR-IOV larger-Tag Requester requirements for VF Non-Posted Requests, specifying valid Tag ranges, keep-out ranges, and error handling practices for invalid Completions. It addresses technical specifications for larger-Tag configurations, constraints for Unexpected Completion error management, and the deprecation of VF Migration interrupt fields in modern designs.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.3 SR-IOV Control Register (Offset 08h)
children.description: This section details the configuration and operational constraints of the SR-IOV Control Register, specifically addressing larger-Tag Requester Enable settings for Virtual Functions (VFs). It defines permitted Tag ranges, keep-out bit criteria, error-handling recommendations for invalid Tags, and the deprecation of VF Migration.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.3.1 VF Enable
children.description: This section defines the **VF Enable** and **14-Bit Tag Requester Enable** control bits for SR-IOV, specifying operational requirements for PCI Express fabric accessibility and Larger-Tag support. It addresses when VFs become visible as Endpoints, the rules for tag generation, and the mandatory device initialization period following VF activation.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.3.4 VF MSE (Memory Space Enable)
children.description: This section details the lifecycle management of SR-IOV Virtual Functions (VFs) through the **VF Enable** and **Memory Space Enable (MSE)** bits, specifically governing VF state transitions between D0uninitialized and active. It addresses critical technical constraints regarding the **Function Readiness Status (FRS)** messaging protocol, mandatory **1.0 s latency timers** for configuration access, and the architectural implications of disabling VFs while **SIOV** is active.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.3.5 ARI Capable Hierarchy
children.description: This section defines the **ARI Capable Hierarchy** bit, detailing its role as a configuration hint for **SR-IOV** devices triggered by the **ARI Forwarding Enable** status in upstream ports. It specifies visibility constraints across Physical Functions (PFs), dependencies for **First VF Offset** and **VF Stride** calculations, and persistence rules during power state transitions and resets.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.4 SR-IOV Status Register (Offset 0Ah)
children.description: This section defines the **ARI Capable Hierarchy** bit within the **SR-IOV Status Register**, detailing its role as a configuration hint for **ARI Forwarding** in Root/Switch Ports. It clarifies how this bit governs **VF Offset** and **VF Stride** calculations, specifies reset/power state persistence behaviors, and notes its inapplicability to **RCiEPs**.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.4.1 VF Migration Status
children.description: This section details the SR-IOV Configuration register fields, covering the deprecated VF Migration Status, TotalVFs capacity, and NumVFs control logic. It specifies programming requirements for SR-PCIM fabric visibility, HwInit constraints, and the operational dependencies governed by the Function Dependency Link.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.8 Function Dependency Link (Offset 12h)
children.description: This section defines the Function Dependency Link register and SR-IOV status bits, addressing technical requirements for legacy field deprecation and the configuration of TotalVFs and NumVFs. It specifies hardware initialization constraints and functional dependencies for managing Virtual Function (VF) scalability within the PCI Express fabric.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.11 VF Device ID (Offset 1Ah)
children.description: This section details the calculation of Virtual Function (VF) routing identifiers using **First VF Offset** and **VF Stride** parameters relative to the Physical Function (PF). It answers how system software determines the PCI Routing ID and Device ID for each VF, while defining constraints for address allocation within the ARI-capable hierarchy.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.12 Supported Page Sizes (Offset 1Ch)
children.description: This section specifies the `Supported Page Sizes` register (Offset 1Ch), detailing bit-field mappings for memory page granularity within the controller interface. It answers questions regarding which page sizes a device supports and how host software must configure system memory alignment to ensure architectural compliance.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.14 VF BAR0 (Offset 24h), VF BAR1 (Offset 28h), VF BAR2 (Offset 2Ch), VF BAR3 (Offset 30h), VF BAR4 (Offset 34h), VF BAR5 (Offset 38h)
children.description: This section defines the alignment requirements and page size configuration for SR-IOV Virtual Function (VF) Base Address Registers (BARs). It specifies how PF-supported page sizes determine valid system-level mapping, detailing the configuration of "Supported Page Sizes" and "System Page Size" to ensure proper VF memory resource alignment.

children.title: Section 9.4.3.15 VF Migration State Array Offset (Deprecated) (Offset 3Ch)
children.description: This section defines the mandatory procedure for calculating address space requirements for SR-IOV Virtual Function (VF) Base Address Registers (BARs) by applying scaling factors based on the number of VFs and memory alignment constraints. It addresses technical inquiries regarding configuration space sizing, BAR masking, and the deprecated status of VF Migration state registers within the PCIe specification.

children.title: Section 9.4.6 PCI Standard Capabilities
children.description: This section defines the algorithm for calculating the address space size and alignment requirements for SR-IOV Virtual Function (VF) BARs, including handling Resizable BARs and multiplying by the number of VFs (`NumVFs`). It specifies register offset mappings for Type 0 headers and identifies the deprecated status of VF Migration.

children.title: Section 9.4.7 PCI Express Extended Capabilities Changes
children.description: This section provides the SR-IOV standard requirements for implementing PCI and PCI Express Extended Capabilities on Physical Functions (PFs) and Virtual Functions (VFs). It serves as a definitive reference for determining which configuration space capabilities are mandatory, optional, prohibited, or not applicable within an SR-IOV architecture.

children.title: Section 9.5 SIOV Configuration
children.description: This section details the SIOV architecture, covering the configuration space structure, extended capabilities, and specific register definitions for Total SDIs, SDI offsets, and stride. It provides the necessary technical specifications to configure and manage Scalable I/O Virtualization (SIOV) and SDI (Scalable Device Interface) resource addressing within an PCIe-based environment.

children.title: Section 9.5.3 SIOV Extended Capability
children.description: This section specifies mandatory PCI Express Extended Capability requirements for Scalable I/O Virtualization (SIOV), focusing on PF-specific implementation rules, configuration space header types, and the exclusion of VFs and SDIs. It addresses technical criteria for discovering and controlling SIOV capabilities within multi-function PCIe devices while defining the structural constraints of the SIOV Extended Capability (0038h).

children.title: Section 9.5.4 SIOV Extended Capability Header (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section defines the PCIe Extended Capability Header for SIOV, detailing the mandatory Capability ID, versioning, and next-capability pointer fields. It explains the structure and bit-level layout required to identify and traverse the Scalable I/O Virtualization (SIOV) configuration space.

children.title: Section 9.5.5 SIOV Capabilities Register (04h)
children.description: This section defines the architectural structure and register map for the SIOV Extended Capability, specifically detailing the Capability ID (0038h), versioning, and offset organization. It answers technical questions regarding the configuration space layout, including the implementation of the SIOV Capabilities Register and the Next Capability Offset for linked-list traversal.

children.title: Section 9.5.7 SIOV Status Register (Offset 0Bh)
children.description: This section defines the SIOV Capabilities and Status registers, detailing key technical specifications such as Strided RID Mapping, Mixed IOV support, and SDI enablement. It clarifies how system software identifies active SDIs and manages interoperability between SIOV and SR-IOV configurations for Physical Functions.

children.title: Section 9.5.9 SDI Stride (Offset 0Eh)
children.description: This section defines the **SDI Stride (Offset 0Eh)** and **First SDI Offset (Offset 0Ch)** registers used to calculate **Requester ID (RID)** mappings for **Scalable I/O Virtualization (SIOV)**. It specifies the mathematical procedure for deriving iterative RID offsets and conditions for **Read-Only (RO)** register validity based on the **Strided RID Mapping** bit in the **SIOV Capabilities Register**.

children.title: Chapter 10. Address Translation Services (ATS)
children.description: This section details Address Translation Services (ATS) mechanisms, including PCIe translation requests, invalidation protocols, and Page Request Services (PRS) for managing I/O page faults. It addresses how I/O devices perform address translation, maintain cache coherency via invalidations, and configure hardware to support DMA operations in virtualized environments.

children.title: Section 10.1 ATS Architectural Overview
children.description: This section outlines the architectural necessity of Address Translation Services (ATS) for DMA I/O functions, detailing technical rationales such as scatter/gather, memory protection, and virtualization support. It addresses how translating I/O "handles" into system physical addresses impacts DMA latency and host memory performance.

children.title: Section 10.1.1 Address Translation Services (ATS) Overview
children.description: This section defines the Address Translation Services (ATS) framework, detailing the interaction between PCIe Devices, Root Complex Integrated Endpoints (RCiEPs), Translation Agents (TA), and Address Translation and Protection Tables (ATPT). It explains how the ATS request-completion protocol and the memory request "AT" field enable hardware to perform address translations and identify the translation status of TLP traffic.

children.title: Section 10.1.2 Page Request Interface Extension
children.description: This section outlines the functional requirements for processing Address Translation Services (ATS) Invalidation Requests and the subsequent issuance of Invalidation Completions. It addresses protocol mandates for flushing pending transactions, ensuring memory consistency for stale addresses, and synchronizing cache invalidation completion signaling within the PCIe hierarchy.

children.title: Section 10.1.3 Process Address Space ID (PASID)
children.description: This section discusses **Page Request Interface (PRI)** optimization for devices using **Process Address Space IDs (PASID)** to manage unpinned virtual memory. It addresses how systems efficiently handle **page table status flags** and the performance trade-offs of **dirty-on-write-permission-grant** policies for pageable versus pinned memory.

children.title: Section 10.1.4 ATS Memory Attributes
children.description: This section defines the technical specifications for Address Translation Services (ATS) memory attributes, specifically covering PASID (Process Address Space ID) allocation, constraints, and operational rules for Translated Requests.
