It clarifies how hardware must manage PASID bit-width, enforce request-response consistency, and validate privilege and execution permissions during memory translations.

children.title: Section 10.2 ATS Translation Services
children.description: Section 10.2 defines the Address Translation Services (ATS) protocol, covering memory requests, translation request/completion cycles, and handling multiple translations. This section provides the technical specifications required to answer how PCIe devices perform address translation and manage completion sequences between endpoints and the Root Complex.

children.title: Section 10.2.1 Memory Requests with Address Type
children.description: This section details protocols for managing **PASID (Process Address Space ID)** allocation and **Translated Requests** within **ATC (Address Translation Cache)** architectures. It defines constraints for PASID width, rules for matching PASID/Privileged/Execute attributes between translation and memory requests, and introduces **ATS Memory Attributes (AMAs)** for optimized memory operations.

children.title: Section 10.2.2 Translation Requests
children.description: This section defines the Address Type (AT) field in PCIe Flit Mode memory request headers, specifying how Address Translation Cache (ATC) functions handle memory transactions. It clarifies the usage of “Untranslated,” “Translation Request,” and “Translated” encodings while detailing protocol requirements for Translation Agent (TA) compliance and Unsupported Request (UR) signaling.

children.title: Section 10.2.2.3 Tag Field
children.description: Section 10.2.2.3 defines the **Translation Request** format and constraints for **Flit Mode**, focusing on the **Tag**, **Length**, and **Attr** field specifications. It clarifies ordering requirements, **Relaxed Ordering (RO)** applicability, and the **Root Port RCB** limitations governing the **Length** field for **Translation Agent (TA)** responses.

children.title: Section 10.2.2.7 CXL Src
children.description: This section details CXL Translation Request parameters, defining the Untranslated Address field structure, implied address ranges, and Page Aligned Request constraints. It explains how the Translation Agent (TA) processes address bits, mandates byte range compliance, and specifies bit-masking rules for page-aligned versus unaligned requests.

children.title: Section 10.2.3 Translation Completion
children.description: This section defines the structural requirements and status response codes for PCIe Translation Completions (Cpl/CplD) returned by a Translation Agent (TA). It specifies protocol behaviors for Success and Unsupported Request (UR) conditions, including how the Relaxed Ordering bit and Completion Status fields function during address translation operations.

children.title: Section 10.2.3.2 Translation Range Size (S) Field
children.description: Section 10.2.3.2 defines the Translation Range Size (S) and access permission fields (R, W, Priv) for PCIe translation requests. It specifies how these indicators regulate Read/Write transaction authorization, privilege levels, and the criteria for valid or cached Translated Addresses within an Address Translation Cache (ATC).

children.title: Section 10.2.3.4 Untranslated Access Only (U) Field
children.description: This section defines the "Untranslated Access Only" (U) field and the "S" size field, detailing how the S-bit determines memory translation range sizes in 4KB multiples. It explains technical constraints for STU-compliant translations, invalidation request formats, and handling of undefined or invalid size configurations.

children.title: Section 10.2.3.5 Read (R) and Write (W) Fields
children.description: This section defines the Read (R), Write (W), and Untranslated (U) fields, detailing how the U field manages Address Translation Cache (ATC) invalidation requirements for memory regions with varying locality. It addresses how to optimize cache coherence and minimize invalidation overhead by selectively caching translation attributes for specific buffer types.

children.title: Section 10.2.3.7 Privileged Mode Access (Priv)
children.description: This section defines the **Privileged Mode (Priv)** bit functionality and **Execute (Exe)** bit constraints for memory translation permissions. It specifies how these bits qualify **Read (R)** and **Write (W)** access for **Privileged versus Non-Privileged entities** and outlines mandatory requirements for enabling **Execute permissions** within a **Translation Completion Data Entry**.

children.title: Section 10.2.3.8 Global Mapping (Global)
children.description: This section defines the architectural requirements for caching Privileged/Non-Privileged translation permissions and the usage of the Global Mapping bit in Address Translation Caches (ATC). It specifies ATC caching rules, privilege-level independence, and the functional implications of the Global bit for cross-PASID translation entries.

children.title: Section 10.2.3.9 ATS Memory Attributes
children.description: This section defines the ATS Memory Attributes (AMA) mechanism, detailing its opaque nature, implementation-specific enablement, and the handling of Translation Completion TLPs. It addresses technical requirements for caching auxiliary attributes alongside translated addresses and specifies how PCIe components manage data entry alignment relative to the Read Completion Boundary (RCB).

children.title: Section 10.2.3.10 TEE Exclusive Memory Attribute (TE)
children.description: This section defines the TEE Exclusive Memory (TE) attribute and its role in distinguishing between protected TEE memory, accessible by the Trusted Computing Base, and non-TEE memory. It specifies the functional requirements for TE bit usage in Address Translation Services (ATS), including prerequisite capabilities in the ATS Capability Register and protocols for memory classification in TEE Device Interfaces (TDIs).

children.title: Section 10.2.4 Completions with Multiple Translations
children.description: This section defines TEE-secured memory versus non-TEE memory, detailing the TEE Exclusive Memory Attribute (TE) bit for differentiating memory access within Trusted Execution Environments. It answers how TDIs verify TEE memory status and outlines the technical requirements for ATC address translations in compliant TCB architectures.

children.title: Section 10.3 ATS Invalidation
children.description: This section details the PCIe Address Translation Services (ATS) Invalidation protocol, covering request/completion mechanics, flow control, ordering semantics, and PASID-specific invalidation requirements. It addresses how devices correctly synchronize translation caches, maintain memory coherency during updates, and handle hardware-level invalidation request acceptance and completion.

children.title: Section 10.3.1 Invalidate Request
children.description: This section defines the multi-TLP packetization logic for ATS Translation Completion (CplD) messages, specifying how byte counts signaling truncation or completion are interpreted. It addresses the technical requirements for handling address boundary crossings and truncated page translations without padding invalid entries.

children.title: Section 10.3.2 Invalidate Completion
children.description: This section defines the "Invalidate Completion" message body structure for PCIe Address Translation Services (ATS), specifying the usage of the S (Size) bit and Global Invalidate bit for memory coherency. It explains how functions associate invalidation completions with original requests and outlines implementation constraints regarding Function Level Reset (FLR) and D-state power transitions.

children.title: Section 10.3.3 Invalidate Completion Semantics
children.description: This section defines serialization and consistency requirements for Invalidate Completions, including mandatory protocol handling for ATS-enabled functions and Bus Master Enable status. It specifies the error reporting mechanisms for unexpected completions and the prerequisite state transitions—governing memory requests and pending completions—necessary before an Address Translation Cache (ATC) can safely validate a translation invalidation.

children.title: Section 10.3.4 Request Acceptance Rules
children.description: This section outlines protocol requirements for PCI Express Address Translation Services (ATS) Invalidate Requests and Completions, specifically focusing on Requester ID matching, Shadow Function targeting, and the prohibition of circular dependencies between posted transactions. It clarifies implementation obligations for ATS-capable functions and mandates that Invalidate Completion responses effectively flush pending posted writes across main and shadow functions.

children.title: Section 10.3.6 Invalidate Ordering Semantics
children.description: This section details the flow control mechanisms and buffering requirements for managing PCIe Invalidate Request traffic between a Translation Agent (TA) and an Address Translation Cache (ATC). It addresses how implementations manage invalidation congestion, credit-based throttling, and the use of the Invalidate Queue Depth field to prevent performance degradation.

children.title: Section 10.3.7 Implicit Invalidation Events
children.description: This section specifies conditions for **ATC (Address Translation Cache) entry invalidation**, including **implicit invalidation events** like resets or PASID cessation and the handling of **Translation Request** range overlaps. It defines how hardware protocols manage cache consistency and addresses how to resolve address boundary crossing scenarios during invalidation operations.

children.title: Section 10.3.8 PASID and Global Invalidate
children.description: This section defines the Address Translation Cache (ATC) invalidation protocols for PCI Express Functions supporting Process Address Space IDs (PASID). It specifies the operational requirements for handling Global Invalidate bits, PASID range validation, and completion signaling when managing cached memory mapping entries.

children.title: Section 10.4 Page Request Services
children.description: This section defines the mandatory and optional hardware responses for Address Translation Cache (ATC) Invalidate Requests associated with Process Address Space IDs (PASID). It specifies error signaling for out-of-range PASIDs, conditions for returning Invalidate Completions, and the required scope of cache invalidation based on Global Invalidate bit settings.

children.title: Section 10.4.1 Page Request Message
children.description: Section 10.4.1 defines the PCIe Page Request Message (PRM) protocol, detailing message routing to the Root Complex, Traffic Class 0 constraints, and the PRG (Page Request Group) mechanism. This section mandates that devices utilize PRG indexing to process collective page resolutions and explains how endpoints reliably request resident memory mapping via ATS-integrated messaging.

children.title: Section 10.4.1.2 Managing PASID Usage on PRG Requests
children.description: This section defines the management of Process Address Space IDs (PASIDs) and Page Request Group (PRG) indices within PCIe Page Request Messages. It outlines technical specifications for handling PRG index verification, untranslated page addressing, and the use of PASID TLP prefixes or OHC-A1 structures to specify process-specific memory access requirements.

children.title: Section 10.4.1.2.1 Stop Marker Messages
children.description: This section outlines the protocol for quiescing the PCIe Page Request Interface (PRI) using two distinct methods: waiting for PRG Responses or employing Stop Marker Messages. It defines the technical procedures for halting Page Request Message (PRM) transmission, managing stale requests, and signaling PASID state changes to the host.

children.title: Section 10.4.2 Page Request Group Response Message
children.description: This section defines the Page Request Group (PRG) Response message, specifying the signaling protocols for host-side PRG completion and failure conditions. It details the all-or-nothing nature of multi-page requests and enumerates technical failure criteria, including invalid Untranslated Addresses, PASID irregularities, and insufficient access permissions.

children.title: Section 10.4.2.1 Response Code Field
children.description: This section defines the **Page Request Group (PRG) Response** message format, detailing technical specifications for **Response Code** fields, **Non-Flit and Flit Mode** packet structures, and **overflow condition** recovery. It outlines how software processes queue entries, assigns **Requestor/Destination IDs**, and clears hardware status to manage Page Request acceptance in a PCIe-based system.

children.title: Section 10.4.2.2 PASID Usage on PRG Responses
children.description: This section defines protocol requirements for Page Request Group (PRG) Response codes and PASID handling within PCI Express ATS architectures. It specifies how Functions must process "Response Failure" states, determine PASID inclusion based on the "PRG Response PASID Required" bit, and map session-specific PASID values for request-response correlation.

children.title: Section 10.5 ATS Configuration
children.description: This section details the configuration of Address Translation Services (ATS) and Input/Output Memory Management Unit (IOMMU) mechanisms, specifically covering ATS Extended Capability registers and Page Request Interface (PRI) structures. It addresses technical requirements for PCIe-based memory management, peripheral-to-host address translation, and fault-handling protocols.

children.title: Section 10.5.1 ATS Extended Capability
children.description: This section specifies the behavior of ATS Extended Capability response codes, focusing on the handling of "Response Failure" states, Page Request Interface (PRI) fault conditions, and PASID-based protocol matching. It delineates how Functions manage catastrophic errors, re-enablement requirements, and the technical implementation of PRG Response Messages according to PASID availability.

children.title: Section 10.5.1.1 ATS Extended Capability Header (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section defines the PCIe ATS Extended Capability Header, utilizing the PCI-SIG Capability ID (000Fh) and Version field to identify Address Translation Services support. It details the header structure and next-capability pointer, answering how system software discovers the presence and versioning of ATS hardware capabilities on a PCI Express device.

children.title: Section 10.5.1.2 ATS Capability Register (Offset 04h)
children.description: This section defines the ATS Capability Register (offset 04h) and the ATS Extended Capability Header (offset 00h), detailing bit-level fields such as Invalidate Queue Depth, Page Aligned Request, and Global Invalidate support. It provides technical specifications for identifying the ATS Extended Capability structure and its associated hardware implementation attributes within a PCIe system.

children.title: Section 10.5.1.3 ATS Control Register (Offset 06h)
children.description: This section defines the **ATS Capability Register (Offset 04h)**, detailing configuration bits for **Invalidate Queue Depth**, **Page Aligned Requests**, **Global Invalidation** support, and **Relaxed Ordering**. It specifies hardware requirements, read-only (RO) attributes, and architectural constraints for PFs, VFs, and PASID-enabled devices.

children.title: Section 10.5.2 Page Request Extended Capability Structure
children.description: This section specifies the **ATS Control Register**, detailing configuration parameters such as the **Smallest Translation Unit (STU)**, **ATS Memory Attributes Enable (AMAE)**, and **ATS Memory Attributes Default (AMAD)**. It defines how these fields manage memory translation granularity and provide optional **TPH TLP Prefix** support for requester performance optimization.

children.title: Section 10.5.2.1 Page Request Extended Capability Header (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section defines the structural layout and register definitions of the Page Request Extended Capability for SR-IOV and SIOV devices. It specifies how PFs, VFs, and SDIs share a common Page Request Interface while maintaining distinct identification via Requester IDs in PRG messages.

children.title: Section 10.5.2.2 Page Request Control Register (Offset 04h)
children.description: This section defines the **Page Request Control Register (PRCR)** bit-fields, including **Page Request Enable (PRE)** and **Reset (PRR)**, used to manage **PCIe I/O Virtualization (IOV)** page request services. It details hardware-level interface protocols, addressing how host software initializes, monitors, and clears pending page request queues within the device memory-mapped I/O structure.

children.title: Section 10.5.2.3 Page Request Status Register (Offset 06h)
children.description: This section defines the Page Request Control Register (offset 06h), detailing bit-level attributes for the Enable (E) and Reset (R) functions. It explains how to manage Page Request Interface activity, clear operation-specific status flags, and reset implementation-dependent request credits.

children.title: Section 10.5.2.4 Outstanding Page Request Capacity (Offset 08h)
children.description: This section defines the **Outstanding Page Request Capacity** register, specifying the maximum number of simultaneous page request messages a device can issue. It details bit-level field definitions and associated constraints to answer how the PCIe **Page Request Service (PRS)** manages buffer resources and limits pending transaction throughput.

children.title: Section 10.5.2.5 Outstanding Page Request Allocation (Offset 0Ch)
children.description: This section defines the **Outstanding Page Request Allocation** register (Offset 0Ch), detailing the architectural limits for **Page Request Interface (PRI)** message credits and **Page Request Group (PRG)** issuance. It addresses how hardware enforces resource constraints, manages **PASID** requirements for completion messages, and mandates pre-allocation of request credits before PRG execution.

children.title: Chapter 11. TEE Device Interface Security Protocol (TDISP)
children.description: This section introduces the TEE Device Interface Security Protocol (TDISP), a framework enabling trust relationships between Trusted Execution Environment VMs (TVMs) and PCIe devices. It details how TEE-I/O architectures use SR-IOV, SIOV, and ATS to maintain secure resource assignment while operating under untrusted Virtual Machine Monitors (VMMs).

children.title: Section 11.1 Overview of the TEE-I/O Security Model as it Relates to Devices
children.description: This section outlines the TEE-I/O security model defined by TDISP, focusing on the categorization of hardware resources as "TEE-assignable" or "Non-TEE-assignable." It addresses how devices, including PFs, VFs, and SDIs, are managed within a TEE environment and establishes the requirements for securing system resources directly assigned to VMs.

children.title: Section 11.2 TDISP Rules
children.description: This section outlines the identification and addressing mechanisms for TEE Device Interfaces (TDI) within the TDISP architecture, focusing on the use of `INTERFACE_ID`, `RID`, `PASID`, and `HPA` for routing MMIO and device-originated requests. It defines the structural composition of `INTERFACE_ID` and specifies the technical requirements for device enumeration, function assignment, and DSM-managed routing validation.

children.title: Section 11.2.1 TDISP TLP Rules
children.description: This section details requirements for TDISP peer-to-peer (P2P) communication, specifically covering `BIND_P2P_STREAM_REQUEST` protocol, ATS/ACS configuration mandates, and MMIO attribute updates. It defines the technical constraints under which a TDI functions within a TVM, dictating when traffic must be steered to the Root Complex versus local device-to-device paths.

children.title: Section 11.2.2 TDISP Message Transport
children.description: This section defines the TDISP message transport protocol, specifying requirements for IDE-bound Stream_IDs, T-bit alignment, and XT Mode handling for memory requests. It details the conditions under which a TDI processes requests based on `IS_NON_TEE_MEM` attributes, XT bits, and T-bit status to ensure secure TLP communication.

children.title: Section 11.2.3 Requirements for Requesters (TSM)
children.description: This section mandates the use of [SPDM] VENDOR_DEFINED message formats and [Secured-SPDM] encapsulation for TDISP transactions between a TSM Requester and DSM Responder. It defines mandatory PCI-SIG identifier requirements, Protocol ID tagging, and the structural byte-level layout for secure, authenticated TDISP request/response message exchange.

children.title: Section 11.2.4 Requirements for Responders (DSM)
children.description: This section outlines the operational criteria and qualification standards for Demand Side Management (DSM) responders, covering dispatch latency, telemetry accuracy, and communication protocols. It answers questions regarding technical baseline requirements, performance verification mandates, and response-time obligations essential for grid reliability and program compliance.

children.title: Section 11.2.6 DSM Tracking and Handling of Locked TDI Configurations (Informative)
children.description: This section outlines TDISP request handling rules, requester-responder concurrent session management, and the architectural requirements for tracking configuration modifications while a TDI is in `CONFIG_LOCKED` or `RUN` states. It addresses protocol-level constraints for `ERROR_CODE=BUSY` responses and the security implementation responsibility for protecting Physical and Virtual Function configuration controls.

children.title: Section 11.2.7 TVM Acceptance of a TDI
children.description: Section 11.2.7 details the requirements for a Trusted Virtual Machine (TVM) to authorize a Trusted Device Instance (TDI) into its Trusted Computing Base (TCB). It outlines mandatory security verification criteria involving SDPM secure sessions, IDE key management, and TSM/DSM coordination, while defining architectural handling for PCIe capabilities and TDISP request codes.

children.title: Section 11.3 TDISP Message Formats and processing
children.description: This section defines the TDISP protocol messaging framework, outlining request/response codes, versioning mechanisms, and interface lifecycle management (lock, start, stop). It specifies the technical standards for querying device capabilities and reporting interface states to facilitate standardized communication between TDISP-compliant components.

children.title: Section 11.3.1 TDISP Request Codes
children.description: This section details the TDISP request codes and the rigorous attestation protocols—including identity verification, SPDM session security, and IDE key management—required for a Trusted VM (TVM) to admit a Trusted Device Interface (TDI) into its Trusted Computing Base (TCB). It clarifies how TVMs utilize TSM and DSM interfaces to validate device configuration and security before granting communication clearance.

children.title: Section 11.3.2 TDISP Response Codes
children.description: This section specifies the TDISP response codes, message values, and state requirements for managing device interface operations such as initialization, configuration, and P2P streaming. It provides technical definitions for standard command responses and outlines the protocol requirements for handling successful operations, error reporting, and TDISP configuration updates.

children.title: Section 11.3.3 TDISP Message Format and Protocol Versioning
children.description: Section 11.3.3 defines the TDISP message format, specifying Little Endian byte ordering, field reservation rules, and bit-field mapping requirements. It addresses how to interpret protocol versioning through the TDISPVersion field, specifically detailing bit assignments for Major and Minor versions to ensure backward compatibility.

children.title: Section 11.3.5 TDISP_VERSION
children.description: This section defines the TDISP version negotiation protocol, detailing the `TDISP_GET_VERSION` request flow, `TDISP_VERSION` response requirements, and mandatory error codes. It answers how Requesters and Responders establish backward-compatible protocol versioning and specifies the initialization sequence required before issuing secondary communication requests.

children.title: Section 11.3.7 TDISP_CAPABILITIES
children.description: This section specifies the TDISP capability exchange mechanism, detailing data formats for `GET_TDISP_CAPABILITIES` and `TDISP_CAPABILITIES` messages. It defines technical parameters including `DSM_CAPS` (XT Mode support), `REQ_MSGS_SUPPORTED` bitmasks, `DEV_ADDR_WIDTH`, and lock interface flags to establish the cryptographic and addressing constraints between the Requester and Responder.

children.title: Section 11.3.8 LOCK_INTERFACE_REQUEST
children.description: This section outlines error conditions for the `LOCK_INTERFACE_REQUEST` command, which transitions a TDI to `CONFIG_LOCKED`. It details failure criteria involving IDE key validation, configuration state, and address space overlaps for PF/VF BARs and Expansion ROMs to ensure peripheral device integrity.

children.title: Section 11.3.9 LOCK_INTERFACE_RESPONSE
children.description: This section specifies the protocol requirements for the `LOCK_INTERFACE_RESPONSE` message, detailing state transitions to `CONFIG_LOCKED`, the lifecycle of the `START_INTERFACE_NONCE`, and the mandatory synchronization of DMA, ATS, and PRI operations. It answers how a device confirms a successful TDI lock, handles existing work-in-flight, and manages secure resource provisioning during configuration changes.

children.title: Section 11.3.11 DEVICE_INTERFACE_REPORT
children.description: This section details the `GET_DEVICE_INTERFACE_REPORT` command, specifying how requesters manage large data payloads using `OFFSET` and `LENGTH` parameters for incremental retrieval. It establishes mandatory error conditions, including interface ID validation and state requirements (`CONFIG_LOCKED` or `RUN`), to ensure successful data transmission.

children.title: Section 11.3.13 DEVICE_INTERFACE_STATE
children.description: This section specifies the `DEVICE_INTERFACE_STATE` data structure and the `GET_DEVICE_INTERFACE_STATE` request mechanism used to retrieve Target Device Interface (TDI) status. It defines error handling protocols for `INVALID_REQUEST` and `INVALID_INTERFACE_STATE` conditions, clarifying how to validate interface IDs and communicate the `CONFIG_LOCKED` or `CONFIG_UNLOCKED` states.

children.title: Section 11.3.16 STOP_INTERFACE_REQUEST
children.description: This section details the `STOP_INTERFACE_REQUEST` and `START_INTERFACE_REQUEST` protocols, specifying the transitions of a Trusted Device Interface (TDI) between `CONFIG_LOCKED` and `RUN` states. It defines mandated error handling, cryptographic nonce validation requirements, and the command structures necessary for the Trusted Security Module (TSM) to manage device interface operational states.

children.title: Section 11.3.18 BIND_P2P_STREAM_REQUEST
children.description: This section defines the mandatory flush, cleanup, and security sanitization procedures for a Trusted Device Interface (TDI) following a `STOP_INTERFACE_REQUEST`. It addresses operational termination requirements, including DMA abortion, ATS/PRI status management, secret scrubbing, and resource reclamation.

children.title: Section 11.3.19 BIND_P2P_STREAM_RESPONSE
children.description: This section defines the **BIND_P2P_STREAM_RESPONSE** protocol, detailing how the **Device Setup Manager (DSM)** locks **IDE configuration registers** and ensures transaction ordering between **default Stream_IDs** and **P2P-bound Stream_IDs**. It clarifies how **Target Device Interfaces (TDI)** prioritize stream routing based on address or **Requester ID** matching while mandating the use of default streams for **ATS requests** and untranslated addresses.

children.title: Section 11.3.22 SET_MMIO_ATTRIBUTE_REQUEST
children.description: This section defines the `UNBIND_P2P_STREAM_REQUEST` protocol, detailing requirements for aborting P2P DMA operations, managing IDE configuration register locks, and enforcing transaction ordering. It specifies validation criteria (Interface ID, TDI state, and Stream_ID binding) and governs the transition mechanism for removing P2P stream associations from a Target Device Interface (TDI).

children.title: Section 11.3.24 TDISP_ERROR
children.description: Section 11.3.24 defines the `TDISP_ERROR` structure, detailing the payload format for error reporting, including `ERROR_CODE`, `ERROR_DATA`, and `EXTENDED_ERROR_DATA` fields. It specifies how device-level faults are signaled in response to `SET_MMIO_ATTRIBUTE_REQUEST` operations, covering validation failures, interface state constraints, and the mapping of specific error conditions to their respective technical identifiers.

children.title: Section 11.3.27 SET_TDISP_CONFIG_REQUEST
children.description: This section defines the `SET_TDISP_CONFIG_REQUEST` VDM structure used by the TSM to manage TDISP device configurations, specifically addressing requirements for enabling XT Mode and setting the `XT_BIT_FOR_LOCKED_MSIX`. It details the `VDM_REQUEST` and `VDM_RESPONSE` formats and specifies that all TDIs must be in a `CONFIG_UNLOCKED` state to successfully process configuration changes.

children.title: Section 11.3.29 Device Security Requirements
children.description: This section establishes security requirements for hardware devices, covering identity authentication, firmware measurement, secure interconnects, protected memory, and debug mode management. It addresses how to ensure data integrity, implement access control via ATS/ACS, and maintain secure reset protocols for device-level subsystems.

children.title: Section 11.3.29.1 Device Identity and Authentication
children.description: This section defines protocols for establishing secure device identity, covering cryptographic attestation, mutual authentication, and trust anchor management. It addresses how systems verify hardware authenticity, mitigate impersonation risks, and perform secure initial enrollment during lifecycle provisioning.

children.title: Section 11.3.29.2 Firmware and Configuration Measurements
children.description: This section details the `SET_TDISP_CONFIG_REQUEST` payload structure and error conditions for TEE-I/O device configuration management. It specifies SPDM-based requirements for identity authentication, TCG DICE architecture integration, and secure reporting of firmware measurements and version numbers to the TSM.

children.title: Section 11.3.29.4 Device Attached Memory
children.description: This section defines runtime firmware update requirements for devices with attached memory, specifying protocols for SVN versioning, DICE security compliance, and SPDM measurement handling. It details mandatory error state transitions, IDE stream termination, and TEE-I/O security procedures required when performing firmware updates on devices in CONFIG_LOCKED or RUN states.

children.title: Section 11.3.29.5 TDI Security
children.description: This section details security requirements for TEE-I/O capable devices, specifically mandates for Secure SPDM, TDISP device interface management, and IDE stream key synchronization. It defines protocol constraints for transitioning TDI states (CONFIG_LOCKED, RUN, ERROR) and recovery procedures following SPDM session termination or IDE stream security failures.

children.title: Section 11.3.29.8 Conventional Reset
children.description: This section outlines security requirements for PCI Express "Conventional Reset" when handling Trusted Domain Interfaces (TDIs), specifically detailing how hostile configuration changes or non-recoverable device errors must trigger a transition to the ERROR state to prevent data exposure. It defines the technical expectations for TDI state machine transitions—from `CONFIG_LOCKED/RUN` to `ERROR`—following unauthorized BAR reprogramming, poisoned TLP receipt, or internal DSM failure.

children.title: Section 11.3.29.10 Address Translation Services (ATS) and Access Control Services (ACS)
children.description: This section outlines mandatory hardware security requirements for sanitizing sensitive data, including TVM data, IDE keys, and SPDM session keys, during device reset transitions. It addresses how TEE-I/O devices must prevent plaintext data leakage via debug interfaces and provides protocols for clearing internal structures and measurement registers after conventional or warm resets.

children.title: Section 11.3.30 Requirements Placed on Host Security due to TDI Requirements
children.description: This section defines the security engineering requirements for the host environment when implementing Trusted Device Interface (TDI) protocols, specifically addressing address translation, MMIO/DMA access control, device binding, and interconnect integrity. It answers how a system must configure its I/O architecture and Device-based Security Module (TSM) interfaces to prevent unauthorized memory access and maintain data integrity across peripheral communication channels.

children.title: Section 11.3.30.2 MMIO Access Control
children.description: This section specifies MMIO access control mechanisms, including ACS translation and redirection protocols, to ensure secure memory integrity between TVMs and TDIs. It addresses how the Translation Agent (TA) prevents VMM-based tampering, enforces TDI access permissions, and maintains consistency between the Address Translation and Protection Tables (ATPT) and hardware caches.

children.title: Section 11.3.30.4 Device Binding
children.description: This section defines the mandatory T-bit and XT-bit TLP signaling requirements for secure TEE and non-TEE memory access. It details technical constraints for TVM-assigned MMIO resources and specifies TSM access control policies governing TDI device binding, TCB isolation, and DMA requester ID integrity.

children.title: Section 11.3.30.7 TSM Tracking and Handling of Locked Root Port Configurations (Informative)
children.description: This section outlines security requirements for TSM-managed IDE streams and SPDM sessions, covering topics such as cryptographic key management, sub-stream isolation, and poisoned TLP containment. It addresses how a TSM confirms device binding and safeguards sensitive encryption parameters within the trusted computing base to prevent unauthorized access or system-level data corruption.

children.title: Section 11.3.30.8 IDE Extended Capability registers
children.description: This section defines the modification policy and security impact of IDE Extended Capability registers on PCIe port operations, specifically addressing how configurations like Dynamic Power Allocation, Multicast, and Protocol Multiplexing interface with Integrity and Data Encryption (IDE) streams. It details the conditions under which IDE streams transition to an "Insecure" state following specific register changes or link failures during TEE-I/O operations.

children.title: Section 11.3.31 Overview of Threat Model and Mitigations
children.description: This section details the security architecture for interconnect protocols, device identity, measurement reporting, and TDI lifecycle management. It addresses critical security requirements regarding trusted execution, bus-level access control, and the cryptographic verification of hardware-assigned components.

children.title: Section 11.3.31.1 Interconnect Security
children.description: This section outlines security requirements for TEE-I/O interconnects, covering threat models involving physical attacks, data manipulation (replay/reordering), and key compromise on link protection schemes. It addresses the TSM’s role in IDE (Integrity and Data Encryption) stream lifecycle management and the necessity for robust hardening against adversaries capable of modifying switch firmware, routing tables, or hardware links.

children.title: Section 11.3.31.2 Identity and Measurement Reporting
children.description: This section outlines protocols for Identity and Measurement Reporting, covering key topics such as device authentication, telemetry data structures, and cryptographic verification. It answers questions regarding how systems uniquely identify network endpoints and standardize the transmission of performance measurements and operational metrics.

children.title: Section 11.3.31.3 TDI Assignment and Detach
children.description: This section outlines adversarial threat models for TDI (TEE Device Interface) assignment and detachment, specifically addressing risks related to malicious firmware, custom hardware spoofing, and debug-interface exploitation. It answers how an adversary can compromise device-to-TVM (Trusted VM) security, integrity, and confidentiality through unauthorized firmware downgrades, physical tampering, and subversion of the root of trust during host Root Port resets.

children.title: Chapter 12. Architectural Out-of-Band Management
children.description: This chapter defines the architectural framework for out-of-band (OOB) hardware management, covering signaling protocols, sideband interface standards (USB 2.0, 2-wire), and telemetry for Field Replacement Units (FRU) and Retimers. It addresses how systems perform platform monitoring, component identification, and internal cable management when the primary data path is inaccessible.

children.title: Section 12.2 Framework for Sidebands
children.description: Section 12.2 defines the framework for sideband interfaces, covering technologies such as SMBus, I3C-Basic, and managed USB 2.0 to enable out-of-band (OOB) platform management. It establishes electrical and logical standards for non-host CPU communication, addressing how to implement peripheral subsystem management without relying on the primary high-speed data link.

children.title: Section 12.3 Sideband Signaling Mechanisms
children.description: Section 12.3 details high-speed interconnect sideband signaling, covering discrete interfaces, Flex I/O, and the Peripheral Sideband Tunnelling Interface (PESTI). This section addresses how chip-to-chip auxiliary communication, signal tunneling, and power/control management protocols are architected across standardized peripheral bus structures.

children.title: Section 12.3.1 Discrete Sidebands
children.description: Section 12.3.1 defines the architectural framework for discrete sideband signaling, covering physical topologies, discovery mechanisms, and functional roles for platform management. It addresses how form factor specifications utilize discrete wires, Flexible I/O (Flex I/O), and Peripheral Sideband Tunneling Interfaces (PESTI) to standardize power management (PERST#), link control (CLKREQ#), and non-link communications.

children.title: Section 12.3.2 Flex I/O Sidebands
children.description: This section details Flex I/O sideband protocols, covering default states, discovery phases, compatibility verification, and control negotiation. It provides technical guidance on managing interface configurations and inter-device communication requirements during initial link setup.

children.title: Section 12.3.2.1 Flex I/O Default State Guidelines
children.description: This section defines the Flex I/O architecture, covering default signal states, discovery, and control negotiation for reconfigurable interfaces. It addresses how to manage SMBus reset protocols and flexible pin repurposing to ensure backward compatibility and optimized hardware utilization.

children.title: Section 12.3.2.2 Flex I/O Discovery Phase Guidelines
children.description: This section outlines the mandatory discovery sequence for Flex I/O modules, detailing device enumeration, handshake protocols, and hardware initialization requirements. It addresses critical questions regarding network topology mapping, slave node identification, and the diagnostic parameters necessary to ensure reliable system-wide configuration during power-up.

children.title: Section 12.3.2.3 Flex I/O Compatibility Check Guidelines
children.description: This section defines guidelines for implementing Flex I/O compatibility, covering technical specifications for cross-power domain safety, switchable pin functionality, and mandatory FRU Information disclosure. It addresses how to safely manage adapter interface discovery, signal integrity, and default electrical state transitions to prevent hardware damage or undefined behavior.

children.title: Section 12.3.2.4 Flex I/O Control Negotiation Guidelines
children.description: This section outlines protocols for Flex I/O control negotiation, covering synchronization handshakes, signaling parameters, and interface readiness states. It answers critical questions regarding successful link establishment, error handling during parameter exchange, and hardware interoperability requirements for high-speed I/O configurations.

children.title: Section 12.3.2.5 General Flex I/O Control Guidelines
children.description: This section outlines safety protocols and operational requirements for configuring Flex I/O interfaces, covering pre-wired circuit activation, platform-side control, and peripheral-side command logic. It addresses how to safely transition interface modes and specifies the technical prerequisites—such as discovery, compatibility checks, and electrical safety—necessary to prevent hardware damage during dynamic reconfiguration.

children.title: Section 12.3.3 Peripheral Sideband Tunnelling Interface (PESTI) Sidebands
children.description: This section details the Peripheral Sideband Tunnelling Interface (PESTI) architecture, covering physical layer specifications, DC/AC electrical requirements, protocol command structures, and state machine phases including discovery, active, and reset handling. It provides the necessary technical reference for implementing PESTI interconnect functionality, defining sideband signal integrity, fan-out topologies, and security considerations for initiator and target communication.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.1 PESTI Introduction
children.description: This section details the PESTI (Peripheral Sideband Tunnelling Protocol) communication architecture, covering Flex I/O run-time reconfiguration constraints, technical UART-based signal tunneling, and fault-recovery defaults. It defines how PESTI enables low-logic, half-duplex, point-to-point virtual wire communication for platform-to-peripheral discovery and sideband signal consolidation.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.3 PESTI Electrical Circuit
children.description: This section defines the PESTI electrical interface, detailing UART framing parameters, 3.3V LVCMOS open-drain signaling, and half-duplex communication protocols. It specifies the hardware implementation for power-state presence detection using P-channel FET switching and provides component-side pulldown resistance requirements.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.5 PESTI Target Detection
children.description: This section defines PESTI DC electrical specifications and signal detection logic, detailing UART-based communication protocols for target presence and readiness. It addresses technical requirements for initiator-target signaling, including voltage thresholds, BREAK condition timing, and error state handling for UART frames.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.6 PESTI Protocol Commands
children.description: This section details the command-level operation of the PESTI protocol, specifically covering Discovery Payload Requests (DPR), Virtual Wire Exchange (VWE) mechanisms, and Fanout MUX Control logic. It provides technical specifications answering how nodes perform device discovery, establish virtual signaling paths, and manage signal routing across fanout multiplexers.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.6.1 Discovery Payload Request (DPR)
children.description: This section details the Discovery Payload Request (DPR) protocol, covering frame structure, payload encapsulation, and field-specific validation requirements. It addresses how devices initiate discovery sequences, define payload headers, and ensure interoperability during initial network handshake exchanges.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.6.2 PESTI Virtual Wire Exchange (VWE)
children.description: This section details the architectural implementation of the PESTI Virtual Wire Exchange (VWE), focusing on interface messaging protocols, signal mapping, and synchronization mechanisms. It defines how VWE ensures low-latency data transmission and addresses critical questions regarding hardware-software abstraction, wire emulation, and fault-tolerance procedures within the PESTI framework.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.6.3 PESTI Fanout MUX Control
children.description: This section details PESTI Fanout MUX control, covering target detection rules, BREAK assertion requirements, and the Discovery Payload Request process. It defines communication protocols, timing constraints (tDBREAK), and the sequence for initiator-target discovery and payload retrieval.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.8 PESTI Broadcast
children.description: This section outlines the PESTI Broadcast transaction protocol, specifically detailing MUX/switch control (02h) and initiator-driven sequence termination procedures. It addresses how to handle transaction cancellations during initiator or target transmission phases using BREAK conditions and specifies timing requirements (tTIDLE) for aborts.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.9 PESTI Initiator Control and Status Registers
children.description: This section specifies the PESTI Initiator Control and Status Registers, detailing discovery states via the DSTAT field and broadcast command timing for managing multi-target power transitions. It addresses implementation requirements for preventing inrush current through staggered PWR_BRK de-assertion and defines compliant behavioral constraints for PESTI wire state transitions.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.10 PESTI AC Specifications
children.description: This section details the PESTI interface AC specifications, including baud rate, signal rise/fall times, and timing constraints for physical layer protocol phases like Discovery and Abort. It defines the electrical and temporal characteristics—specifically parameters such as `tBAUD`, `tABREAK`, and `tDBREAK`—required to govern reliable communication between Initiator and Target devices during G3 to S5 transitions.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.11 PESTI Discovery Phase
children.description: This section defines timing parameters for the PESTI Discovery Phase, detailing specifications for turnaround times, bit-sampling intervals, and communication timeouts including $t_{TIDLE}$, $t_{MARK}$, and $t_{DPRTO}$. It addresses technical requirements for bus arbitration, initiator-target synchronization, and watchdog functionality to ensure reliable state transitions and signal integrity during Discovery and Active phases.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.12 PESTI Active Phase
children.description: This section defines the PESTI Active Phase, detailing hardware-controlled virtual wire exchange protocols, message framing (VWOUT/VWIN), and state-transition rules for device-class-specific communication. It addresses how initiators and targets manage multi-byte data exchanges, checksum requirements, and bus idle timing following the transition from discovery to an active state.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.13 PESTI Target Reset and Fault Handling
children.description: This section details PESTI Target Reset sequences and fault management protocols, covering error recovery states, hardware reset signals, and diagnostic signaling. It defines expected system behavior during fault conditions and specifies the required procedural steps for target-side error mitigation and reset synchronization.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.14 PESTI Fan-Out
children.description: This section defines Packet Error Code (PEC) implementation requirements, including initiator/target responsibilities for command validation, response appending, and error handling. It clarifies protocol behavior during target resets, specifically addressing the interaction between discovery state (DSTAT), payload requests (DPEN), and active phase transitions.

children.title: Section 12.3.3.15 PESTI Security Considerations
children.description: This section outlines PESTI (Platform Environmental and System Telemetry Interface) broadcast protocols, fanout switch requirements, and security frameworks for USB 2.0-based out-of-band management. It addresses hardware-level security threats, including physical tampering and man-in-the-middle attacks, by specifying SPDM (Security Protocol and Data Model) and payload encryption mitigations for firmware updates, telemetry, and attestation.

children.title: Section 12.4 Managed USB 2.0
children.description: This section details the PESTI protocol’s managed USB 2.0 broadcast sequence, fanout switch operation, and the subsequent selection of downstream channels via MSC commands. It addresses security threats like physical signal redirection and man-in-the-middle attacks while outlining management use cases including firmware updates, telemetry, and SPDM-based attestation.

children.title: Section 12.5 2-Wire Interface
children.description: This section outlines technical requirements and compatibility standards for 2-Wire bus interfaces, including [SMBus-3.2], [I2C], and [I3C-Basic] protocols. It defines hardware deployment guidelines for managing legacy JTAG repurposing, component compliance, and electrical specifications for managed USB host and FRU information devices.

children.title: Section 12.5.2 2-Wire Addressing
children.description: This section explains 2-Wire interface (SMBus, I2C, I3C) implementation for PCIe-based form factors, focusing on hardware discovery, security attestation, and component management. It addresses requirements for default target address allocation, peripheral firmware updates, and the transition protocols necessary when switching from SMBus to I3C modes.

children.title: Section 12.5.3 2-Wire Bus Sharing
children.description: This section addresses 2-wire bus expansion strategies, covering multi-drop topologies, SMBus multiplexers, and hub-based architectures. It defines technical implementation requirements for managing signal integrity and address conflicts when connecting multiple I2C/SMBus slave devices to a shared controller interface.

children.title: Section 12.5.3.2 SMBus MUX Use
children.description: This section defines 2-Wire interface multi-drop topologies and SMBus MUX configurations for managing fanout between carrier form factors and adapter interfaces. It addresses how to resolve address conflicts via static allocation, SMBus ARP, or I3C Dynamic Addressing, while detailing the security implications of shared-segment communication.

children.title: Section 12.5.3.3 2-Wire Hub Use
children.description: This section outlines the benefits of 2-Wire Hub components, including bus capacitance isolation, voltage translation, and MCTP-compatible asynchronous messaging. It further addresses interoperability challenges with standard MUX components and provides implementation guidance regarding security risks and arbitration limitations in multi-initiator architectures.

children.title: Section 12.5.4 [I3C-Basic] Support on Existing SMBus Signals
children.description: This section defines the interoperability requirements for implementing I3C Basic on existing SMBus physical interfaces, detailing DC and AC electrical specifications, bus discovery, and mode-switching protocols. It addresses how legacy SMBus hardware can transition to I3C Basic signaling while maintaining protocol compatibility and standardized signal integrity.

children.title: Section 12.5.4.1 I3C Basic Overview
children.description: This section details the implementation of [I3C-Basic] as an interoperable, high-speed 2-wire management interface for PCI Express platforms, leveraging existing [SMBus] physical signaling. It addresses technical requirements for interface discovery (DCR 0xC2), bus capacitance constraints, and the operational transition between [SMBus] and [I3C-Basic] modes.

children.title: Section 12.5.4.2 I3C Basic Discovery and Mode Changing
children.description: This section defines the I3C Basic discovery and mode-switching protocol, detailing the use of reserved address 0x7E to distinguish between I3C Basic and legacy SMBus components. It addresses the technical requirements for maintaining bus compatibility, prohibiting mixed-mode operation, and managing voltage/protocol transitions during initialization.

children.title: Section 12.5.4.3 I3C Basic DC and AC Signal Requirements
children.description: This section defines the electrical characteristics and timing requirements for I3C Basic interfaces, specifically detailing operating voltage (Vddi3c), total bus capacitance (Ci3c), and transition timings between SMBus and I3C protocols. It establishes technical specifications for platform signal integrity and host-to-component switching intervals to ensure reliable bus operation.

children.title: Section 12.6 Field Replacement Unit (FRU) Information
children.description: This section details the Field Replacement Unit (FRU) information architecture, covering device requirements, data format specifications, and PCI-SIG MultiRecord descriptors. It provides essential technical guidance for engineers defining field-serviceable hardware identification, serialization protocols, and modular component records within system management firmware.

children.title: Section 12.6.1 FRU Information Device Requirements
children.description: This section defines requirements for FRU Information Devices, covering inventory and configuration data delivered via 2-Wire (SMBus/I2C/I3C) or MCTP protocols. It clarifies implementation mandates for form-factor-specific components and excludes in-band management methods like MMPT or MMBI from its scope.

children.title: Section 12.6.1.1 FRU Information Device Requirements Specific to SMBus/I2C Mode
children.description: This section details requirements for FRU Information Device SMBus/I2C implementations, covering write endurance, power state accessibility, and timing constraints for bus availability. It answers questions regarding hardware compatibility, data protection protocols, and recovery mechanisms for 2-Wire interface communication.

children.title: Section 12.6.1.2 [SMBus]/[I2C] Access Protocol
children.description: This section specifies requirements for handling FRU Information Devices during the transition from SMBus/I2C to I3C mode, prohibiting the use of 2-Wire MUXes or hubs to ensure consistent discovery. It provides the technical signaling protocols for Byte Write and Page Write operations, clarifying physical hardware management and bus frequency limitations for mixed-mode environments.

children.title: Section 12.6.2 FRU Information Format
children.description: This section specifies the technical implementation of the PCI-SIG MultiRecord format within IPMI-compliant FRU Information Storage, detailing required header fields, record type IDs, and format bits. It defines the structural requirements for storing authenticated component metadata and addresses how firmware should validate data integrity and cryptographic signatures during initialization.

children.title: Section 12.6.3 Common PCI-SIG MultiRecord Descriptors
children.description: This section details the physical and logical configuration of PCI-SIG MultiRecord descriptors, specifically focusing on the Connector Subdivision format and its associated Group and Sub-Type identifiers. It answers how system architectures define sub-component topology and interface constraints within the MultiRecord data structure.

children.title: Section 12.6.3.1 Connector Subdivision (Group ID 0h, Sub-Type 0h)
children.description: This section defines the PCI-SIG MultiRecord Connector Subdivision (Group ID 0h, Sub-Type 0h) descriptors used to communicate supported PCIe upstream-facing port configurations to the host. It specifies when these FRU descriptors are required, detailing how to document supported subdivision combinations for multi-port adapters like x16-to-dual-x8 breakouts.

children.title: Section 12.7 Out-of-Band Management Control Mechanism
children.description: This section details the Out-of-Band Management Control Mechanism, specifically the usage of Connector Subdivision Descriptors to define lane mapping for PCIe-based adapter port configurations. It addresses how to programmatically assign lane ranges—from x16 to tiered x4 subdivisions—to facilitate flexible physical port topology management.

children.title: Section 12.7.1 PCIe-MI Architectural Model
children.description: This section outlines the PCIe-MI architectural model, defining protocol layer structures, timing constraints for Command-to-Completion latency, and requirements for PCIe-MI Components and Completers. It addresses how PCIe-MI integrates with MCTP transports, identifies architectural characteristics of Functions and adapters, and establishes implementation standards for compliant hardware interfaces.

children.title: Section 12.7.1.1 Operational Times
children.description: This section defines the mandatory operational constraints and state-based visibility for PCIe-MI Ports, including requirements for unique Port Identifiers, port exposure logic, and the structural association of MMPT PCIe-MI Completers with upstream and downstream functions. It answers questions regarding how hardware identifies PCIe-MI ports, the conditions under which ports are exposed to host systems, and how internal functions are organized to support PCIe-MI over MMPT.

children.title: Section 12.7.1.2 PCIe-MI Completer Reset
children.description: This section details mandatory operational readiness timing requirements for PCIe-MI Completers following power application or Target Reset Pattern assertion. It addresses performance specifications for power-on initialization, auxiliary vs. main power dependencies, and the impact of power state transitions on Completer availability.

children.title: Section 12.7.1.3 Example PCIe-MI Component
children.description: This section details the architectural layout of a PCIe-MI component by defining Port Identifiers, PCIe Functions, and supported management interface protocols including MMPT, MCTP over VDM, and SMBus. It answers how multi-port PCIe devices and 2-Wire interface FRU devices integrate specific management transport mechanisms and addressing schemes.

children.title: Section 12.7.1.4 Example PCIe-MI Management Traffic Flows
children.description: This section details PCIe-MI management traffic architectures, specifically utilizing **MMPT**, **MCTP over MMBI**, and **PCIe VDM** transport protocols. It defines routing mechanisms for **in-band OS/UEFI communication** and **out-of-band BMC-to-component messaging**, clarifying how **Route by ID** and **Root Complex broadcast** methods enable device management.

children.title: Section 12.7.2 PCIe-MI Message Format
children.description: This section defines the structural format and message headers for PCIe-MI (Management Interface) communications, detailing the specific layouts for Commands and Completions. It addresses how to interpret the PCIe-MI Message Type (PMT), Instance ID, and Opcode fields to facilitate proper message decoding and error handling in MMPT and MCTP environments.

children.title: Section 12.7.2.1 PCIe-MI Opcodes
children.description: This section defines the PCIe-MI (Management Interface) message structure, header parameters, and command opcode table for MCTP and MMPT transport bindings. It specifies the configuration for message headers, completion content fields, and mandatory/optional command support requirements for device management operations.

children.title: Section 12.7.2.2 PCIe-MI Status
children.description: Section 12.7.2.2 defines the status reporting mechanism for PCIe-MI (PCIe Management Interface) communications, detailing error handling protocols and the classification of completion status codes. It provides technical specifications for identifying aborted command conditions, distinguishing between internal and external component errors, and managing multi-fault scenarios.

children.title: Section 12.7.2.2.1 Invalid Input Error Completion
children.description: This section defines the technical requirements for handling **Invalid Input Error Completions** (status code 12h) in **PCIe-MI** communication, including field-level error reporting and selection logic for multiple concurrent violations. It answers how a **PCIe-MI Completer** must format status responses and identify non-compliant reserved, unimplemented, or conflicting bit field values within a command message.

children.title: Section 12.7.3 PCIe-MI Commands
children.description: This section defines the structural and processing requirements for PCIe-MI Commands and Completions, detailing protocols for concurrency management, reserved bit handling, and Instance Identifier collision resolution. It addresses how PCIe-MI Completers process simultaneous operations and manage command lifecycle states to ensure interoperable communication across PCIe-MI components.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.1 Get Information (Opcode 00h)
children.description: This section specifies the **Get Information (Opcode 00h)** command protocol, detailing how **PCIe-MI Completers** must handle version-specific **Message Content** lengths and **Information Identifiers**. It defines the architectural requirements for querying component capabilities and provides a reference table (**Table 12-31**) for supported identifiers, including **Supported Information Identifiers** and **Supported Opcodes**.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.1.1 Supported Information Identifiers (Information Identifier 00h)
children.description: This section specifies the protocol for querying the "Supported Information Identifiers" (00h) diagnostic service, detailing the required Request and Response parameters for identifying available data identifiers. It addresses how to retrieve a list of supported Information Identifiers (IIDs) and defines the technical structure for device-level information reporting.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.1.2 Supported Opcodes (Information Identifier 01h)
children.description: This section details the "Supported Opcodes" (Information Identifier 01h) mechanism within the PCIe-MI specification, specifically defining how to retrieve a list of supported Information Identifiers via the Get Information command. It addresses requirements for command field formatting, error handling for invalid identifiers, and the structure of the resulting PCIe-MI Completion packet.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.1.3 Supported Parameters (Information Identifier 02h)
children.description: This section defines the PCIe-MI "Get Information" command structure for the "Supported Parameters" Information Identifier (02h), detailing the byte-level completion format, opcode ordering requirements, and associated parameter availability. It specifies how an MCTP endpoint communicates its supported parameter list, clarifying the mandatory support relationship between Get/Set commands and the required ascending order for returned Parameter Identifiers.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.1.4 Component Information (Information Identifier 03h)
children.description: This section outlines the structure of the Component Information (03h) response, defining its use of Parameter Identifiers to map supported features and Set Parameters command capabilities within the PCIe-MI protocol. It specifies byte-level field mapping for parameters ranging from the second-lowest to the highest value, facilitating device-level hardware configuration discovery.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.1.5 Port Information (Information Identifier 04h)
children.description: This section defines the Get Information – Port Information command (Identifier 04h) for PCIe-MI, detailing the message header, port identifier specifications, and completion data structures. It addresses requirements for querying specific PCIe-MI ports, handling invalid port identifiers, and interpreting the Port Description field, including host exposure status.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.1.6 Function List (Information Identifier 05h)
children.description: This section defines the Get Information – Function List (Information Identifier 05h) command used by PCIe-MI components to report implemented non-IOV and Physical Functions. It details the retrieval of unique Function Identifier (FID) data structures, specifies sorting and filtering requirements based on function type, and outlines the required command message format.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.1.7 Function Information (Information Identifier 06h)
children.description: This section specifies the protocol for retrieving Function Information (Identifier 06h) via the PCIe-MI "Get Information" command structure. It details the request format—encompassing function identifiers and command headers—and the completion response, providing the technical requirements for mapping Vendor IDs to specific PCIe functions.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.2 Get Parameters (Opcode 01h) and Set Parameters (Opcode 02h)
children.description: This section details the **Path** field mechanism for **Get Parameters (01h)** and **Set Parameters (02h)** opcodes within **PCIe-MI components**. It explains how to construct a hierarchical **Routing ID** sequence for both non-ARI and ARI functions, effectively answering how a requester directs commands through a multi-function topology to a specific target function.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.2.1 Configuration Space Writability (Parameter Identifier 00h)
children.description: This section defines the configuration space writability mechanism via Parameter Identifier 00h, detailing how to retrieve a list of writeable PCIe Function bits and specify which fields become lockable to read-only status. It addresses how out-of-band management systems identify mutable configuration registers and how to restrict subsequent in-band software write access to those parameters.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.2.1.1 Get Parameters – Configuration Space Writability
children.description: This section details the "Get Parameters – Configuration Space Writability" command, specifying how to retrieve a list of writeable and lockable PCIe Configuration Space bits accessible via the Set Parameters command. It provides technical requirements for identifying management-interface-accessible fields, utilizing _OSC control bits, and defining Capability structure writability for out-of-band management systems.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.2.1.2 Set Parameters – Configuration Space Writability
children.description: This section defines the parameter structure for managing PCIe Configuration Space writability, specifically detailing how to target Capability structures via byte offsets or _OSC Control fields via bit offsets. It provides the technical specifications required to execute commands that lock specific Function configuration bits to prevent unauthorized modification.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.2.2 Routing Information (Parameter Identifier 01h)
children.description: This section defines the structure and encoding of Routing Information parameters (ID 01h) within network frames, detailing address field formats, path metrics, and hop-count limitations. It addresses how data link layers resolve next-hop addressing, validate path integrity, and manage segmented routing protocols to ensure reliable end-to-end packet delivery.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.2.2.1 Get Parameters – Routing Information
children.description: This section defines the "Get Parameters – Routing Information" command (Identifier 01h), detailing its use of the `_OSC Control Field Bit Offset` to query or configure Segment Numbers and downstream bus ranges. It addresses how to retrieve PCIe-MI routing data and manages error handling for non-lockable Configuration Space bits during downstream Configuration Write Requests.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.2.2.2 Set Parameters – Routing Information
children.description: Section 12.7.3.2.2.2 defines the PCIe-MI "Get Parameters – Routing Information" command, detailing the required message format, Function Identifier inputs, and the resulting completion data. It addresses how to retrieve specific bus topology details, including Secondary Bus Number, Subordinate Bus Number, and PCI Segment Number validity.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.2.3 Configuration Space (Parameter Identifier 02h)
children.description: This section details the **Configuration Space (Parameter Identifier 02h)**, defining the data structures, register offsets, and bit-field assignments for peripheral device initialization. It addresses how to map memory-mapped I/O, configure device-specific capabilities, and manage hardware state transitions during the system boot sequence.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.2.3.1 Get Parameters – Configuration Space
children.description: This section defines the "Set Parameters – Routing Information" command for PCIe-MI, specifying requirements for routing IDs, function identifiers, and error handling for configuration space write requests. It details the command format, input validation rules for downstream ports, and the specific completion statuses returned when parameters violate architectural constraints.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.2.3.2 Set Parameters – Configuration Space
children.description: This section details the **PCIe-MI "Get Parameters – Configuration Space" command**, defining the required **message header**, **Function Identifier**, **Length**, and **Offset** parameters. It specifies the technical constraints for accessing **PCIe Configuration Space** and dictates the **Invalid Input Error Completion** protocols when addressing invalid offsets, lengths, or non-existent functions.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.2.4 Connector Subdivision (Parameter Identifier 03h)
children.description: This section details the **Connector Subdivision** parameter (03h) within the interface communication protocol, defining specifications for secondary sub-connector configurations and signal routing. It addresses implementation parameters for hardware port mapping, hierarchical physical architecture, and parameter-specific data field definitions.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.2.4.1 Get Parameters – Connector Subdivision
children.description: This section defines the **Set Parameters – Configuration Space Command** within the **PCIe-MI** framework, detailing the technical requirements for specifying **Function Identifiers**, **DWORD-aligned offsets**, and **AND/OR bitmasks** for register modification. The documentation outlines the command structure for accessing **Upstream Port connector subdivisions** and specifies the required **Invalid Input Error Completion** conditions for malformed configuration requests.

children.title: Section 12.7.3.2.4.2 Set Parameters – Connector Subdivision
children.description: This section defines the PCIe-MI "Set Parameters – Connector Subdivision" command, detailing the message header, parameters, and index-based configuration process for subdivision combinations. It specifies how to identify supported subdivisions and navigate the command structure to modify component settings that persist until a cold reset.

children.title: Section 12.9 Internal Cable Management
children.description: Section 12.9 addresses Internal Cable Management and PCIe connector subdivision, specifying message header formats, link status requirements, and the role of interposers within CopperLink topologies. It guides implementers on handling cable tiers, sideband signaling, and device-to-carrier connectivity within complex PCIe environments.

children.title: Appendix
children.description: This section provides supplemental technical specifications covering physical layer operations, protocol multiplexing, isochronous data handling, and error-control mechanisms. It answers queries regarding CRC generator matrices, flow control latency calculations, ID-based ordering, and cryptographic test key implementation.

children.title: Appendix A. Isochronous Applications
children.description: Appendix A details the architectural requirements for isochronous traffic in PCI Express systems, covering transaction ordering, data coherency, flow control mechanisms, and bandwidth allocation strategies. It addresses technical queries regarding quality-of-service (QoS) guarantees, isochronous contract validation, and component-level implementation for latency-sensitive data streams.

children.title: Section A.1 Introduction
children.description: This section defines the design goals and architectural models for PCI Express isochronous traffic, specifically addressing bandwidth allocation, starvation prevention, and mechanisms for Endpoint-to-Root-Complex and peer-to-peer communication. It establishes the foundational roles of Port arbitration, Traffic Class (TC) labels, and Virtual Channel (VC) resources in managing differentiated service levels within the PCIe topology.

children.title: Section A.2 Isochronous Contract and Contract Parameters
children.description: Section A.2 defines the requirements for establishing an isochronous contract to ensure deterministic latency and guaranteed bandwidth within a PCI Express fabric. It specifically addresses how resource reservation and traffic regulation mitigate the technical risks of over-subscription and congestion for isochronous traffic.

children.title: Section A.2.1 Isochronous Time Period and Isochronous Virtual Timeslot
children.description: This section defines the temporal parameters for isochronous data transmission, specifically detailing the **Isochronous Time Period (ITP)** and **Isochronous Virtual Timeslots (IVT)** within a synchronized network architecture. It addresses how periodic transmission windows are calculated and how virtual resource allocation ensures deterministic latency for real-time traffic streams.

children.title: Section A.2.3 Isochronous Bandwidth Allocation
children.description: This section details the isochronous bandwidth allocation framework for PCI Express, focusing on virtual timeslots ($t$), isochronous time periods ($T$), and unified request budgeting. It answers how components manage traffic constraints and regulate bandwidth distribution via WRR Port arbitration and the Reference Clock mechanism.

children.title: Section A.2.4 Isochronous Transaction Latency
children.description: This section defines isochronous transaction latency for PCI Express, specifically detailing measurement criteria for read round-trip delays and write global visibility. It clarifies how to calculate the maximum isochronous transaction latency ($L$) to determine requester buffer requirements, establishing a conservative framework that assumes a zero-latency lower bound.

children.title: Section A.2.5 An Example Illustrating Isochronous Parameters
children.description: This section defines isochronous transaction parameters within PCI Express, specifically detailing bandwidth reservation, transaction latency ($L$), and period ($T$) requirements for mixed read/write traffic. It addresses how Requesters must manage non-uniform injection patterns and comply with $T$x\_MPS\_Limit constraints to guarantee latency-sensitive performance.

children.title: Section A.5 Isochronous Data Coherency
children.description: This section defines isochronous data coherency requirements for PCI Express, focusing on transaction latency ($L$), bandwidth reservation ($T$), and non-uniform injection patterns. It establishes constraints on read request lengths ($Tx\_MPS\_Limit$) and service-time independence between read completions and write requests to ensure deterministic performance.

children.title: Section A.7 Considerations for Bandwidth Allocation
children.description: This section details the architectural requirements for managing isochronous bandwidth across PCI Express links, endpoints, switches, and the Root Complex. It addresses technical specifications for deterministic traffic guarantees, resource reservation protocols, and link-layer overhead calculations necessary to ensure quality-of-service compliance in system-level bandwidth allocation.

children.title: Section A.7.1 Isochronous Bandwidth of PCI Express Links
children.description: This section defines protocols for managing isochronous traffic streams within PCI Express fabrics, focusing on technical terms such as TLP packet prioritization, virtual channels, and bandwidth allocation constants. It addresses how PCIe architectures ensure deterministic latency and guaranteed quality-of-service (QoS) for time-sensitive data transmissions.

children.title: Section A.7.2 Isochronous Bandwidth of Endpoints
children.description: Section A.7.2 details the calculation and allocation of **isochronous bandwidth** for USB **endpoints**, defining constraints for **bus periodic scheduling**, **maximum packet size**, and **service interval** requirements. This section answers how host controllers determine bandwidth capacity limits and adhere to frame-time budgets to ensure guaranteed data delivery for time-sensitive transmissions.

children.title: Section A.7.3 Isochronous Bandwidth of Switches
children.description: This section covers the management of Root Complex isochronous bandwidth, focusing on cache coherency requirements, No Snoop Attribute configuration, and flow control mechanisms for isochronous traffic. It addresses how system software determines coherency capabilities and the extent to which switches and completers must accommodate uniform or bursty isochronous transaction patterns.

children.title: Section A.7.4 Isochronous Bandwidth of Root Complex
children.description: This section outlines the configuration and management of isochronous bandwidth within a PCI Express Root Complex, detailing the use of RCRB structures, VC Resource Capability registers, and Traffic Class (TC) assignments. It addresses how software must initialize isochronous VC resources and how requesters must size their buffers based on PCI Express fabric latency ($L_{Fabric}$) and completion latency ($L_{Completer}$) to account for uniform versus non-uniform transaction injection patterns.

children.title: Section A.8 Considerations for PCI Express Components
children.description: This section details PCI Express architecture roles, focusing on the functional requirements and transaction responsibilities of Endpoints, Switches, and Root Complexes. It addresses how these components handle packet routing, requester/completer mechanics, and system-level topology constraints.

children.title: Section A.8.1 An Endpoint as a Requester
Section A.8.2 An Endpoint as a Completer
Section A.8.3 Switches
children.description: This section outlines the configuration and performance requirements for isochronous traffic in PCIe Switches, specifically addressing Root Complex bandwidth reporting via RCRB structures, Virtual Channel (VC) initialization, and the impact of traffic uniformity on flow control and buffer sizing. It defines the technical prerequisites for deploying isochronous endpoints and clarifies how fabric latency affects requester buffer management.

children.title: Section A.8.4 Root Complex
