children.description: This section details the Root Complex and Switch configuration for isochronous traffic management, specifically addressing Port Arbitration Table programming, VC arbitration schemes, and "just-in-time" link scheduling. It explains how system architects allocate bandwidth and minimize latency for time-sensitive PCI Express traffic.

children.title: Appendix B. Symbol Encoding
children.description: This section details the 8b/10b Symbol Encoding standard for data transmission, specifically defining byte-to-symbol mappings and Running Disparity (RD) requirements for data characters. It provides the technical lookup data necessary to determine the transmitted 10-bit symbol for a given hex byte value based on current lane disparity states.

children.title: Appendix C. Physical Layer Appendix
children.description: This appendix provides architectural examples and operational workflows for 8b/10b and 128b/130b data scrambling protocols within the physical layer. It addresses technical requirements for DC balancing, clock recovery, and electromagnetic interference reduction by detailing specific encoding transformation processes.

children.title: Section C.1 8b/10b Data Scrambling Example
children.description: This section provides a parallelized implementation of the $x^{16}+x^{5}+x^{4}+x^{3}+1$ LFSR polynomial for 8b/10b data scrambling/descrambling, utilizing C-language bitwise logic for efficient eight-bit clock cycles. It defines the specific XOR matrix and bit-shifting requirements necessary to ensure standard-compliant PCI Express physical layer data transformation.

children.title: Section C.2 128b/130b Data Scrambling Example
children.description: This section details the 128b/130b data scrambling mechanism, providing reference LFSR state values and a power spectrum analysis for 2.5 GT/s operation. It addresses implementation requirements by providing C-language subroutines to compute LFSR progression and bitwise XOR data scrambling.

children.title: Appendix D. Request Dependencies
children.description: This section outlines the framework for managing resource dependencies—defined by Traffic Class (TC) and TLP type (Posted, Non-Posted, Completion)—when forwarding or translating packets between ports. It establishes a formal notation for dependency mapping and mandates the classification of these operations as legal or illegal to prevent system-level deadlocks in buffered interconnect architectures.

children.title: Appendix E. ID-Based Ordering Usage
children.description: This section covers ID-Based Ordering (IDO) implementation, detailing the architectural advantages, specific operational use cases, and software-level configuration controls. It answers questions regarding when to deploy IDO for hardware optimization and when to avoid it to ensure system compatibility and performance.

children.title: Section E.1 Introduction
children.description: This section introduces ID-Based Ordering (IDO) as a performance-optimization mechanism that permits TLP passing between different originator streams, overriding standard PCIe ordering constraints. It defines "TLP streams" and references defined topologies to explain how the IDO bit enables flexible request and completion routing within complex fabric environments.

children.title: Section E.2 Potential Benefits with IDO Use
children.description: This section details the operational advantages of Initial Device Onboarding (IDO) across MFD/RP direct connects, switched environments, and integrated endpoints. It addresses technical deployment queries regarding security, network configuration, and interoperability when utilizing IDO in conjunction with Registry Objects (RO).

children.title: Section E.2.1 Benefits for MFD/RP Direct Connect
children.description: This section outlines the operational and architectural advantages of MFD/RP Direct Connect, covering integration protocols, latency reduction, and network optimization. It addresses how these connections streamline data throughput and resolve infrastructure bottlenecks for enterprise-level device connectivity.

children.title: Section E.2.2 Benefits for Switched Environments
children.description: This section examines performance enhancements in switched environments, focusing on microsegmentation, collision domain reduction, and full-duplex communication. It addresses how switching architecture optimizes network throughput, minimizes latency, and resolves congestion issues inherent in shared-medium topologies.

children.title: Section E.2.3 Benefits for Integrated Endpoints
children.description: This section details the performance advantages of using IDO (ID-based Ordering) to mitigate traffic congestion in PCIe systems by allowing high-priority transactions to bypass stalled streams. It addresses how IDO facilitates request and completion reordering within Root Complexes and Switches when encountering Flow Control credit limitations or Transaction Layer Packet (TLP) bottlenecks.

children.title: Section E.2.4 IDO Use in Conjunction with RO
children.description: This section defines the interoperability of IDO (ID-Based Ordering) and RO (Relaxed Ordering) attributes within PCIe transactions, detailing performance-optimization trade-offs for Endpoints and Root Ports. It addresses when control traffic may bypass stalled requests and clarifies safety guidelines for setting IDO/RO bits in single-entity versus multi-entity communication environments.

children.title: Section E.3 When to Use IDO
Section E.4 When Not to Use IDO
children.description: This section details technical constraints regarding IDO (ID-based Ordering) implementation, specifically addressing scenarios involving Endpoints and Root Ports. It clarifies when to disable IDO, focusing on PCIe ordering rules, performance impacts, and hardware compatibility requirements.

children.title: Section E.4.1 When Not to Use IDO with Endpoints
children.description: This section defines the constraints and safety protocols for using IDO (ID-Based Ordering) in PCIe transactions by distinguishing between Endpoint and Root Port traffic scopes. It addresses technical queries regarding the interaction between IDO and RO (Relaxed Ordering), multi-entity communication, and appropriate TLP (Transaction Layer Packet) configuration for DMA and PIO traffic.

children.title: Section E.4.2 When Not to Use IDO with Root Ports
children.description: This section addresses the risks of using TLP IDO (ID-Based Ordering) with Root Ports in PCIe hierarchies featuring Peer-to-Peer (P2P) traffic. It defines why enabling IDO can violate architectural ordering guarantees between P2P writes and host PIOWs, detailing the necessary role of software drivers in enforcing safe TLP ordering configurations.

children.title: Section E.5 Software Control of IDO Use
children.description: This section details the software configuration mechanisms for managing ID-Based Ordering (IDO) in PCIe systems, specifically covering Endpoint and Root Port traffic control. It addresses how system software enables, restricts, and optimizes transaction ordering attributes to improve performance and maintain data coherency.

children.title: Section E.5.1 Software Control of Endpoint IDO Use
children.description: This section details the risks of enabling IDO (ID-based Ordering) on Root Ports and Endpoints within PCIe hierarchies involving Peer-to-Peer (P2P) traffic. It defines how host software must manage IDO enablement to prevent TLP reordering violations that would otherwise break standard PCIe ordering rules during concurrent P2P and host-memory communications.

children.title: Section E.5.2 Software Control of Root Port IDO Use
children.description: This section details the software control policies for IDO (ID-based Ordering) Bit usage within Root Ports and Endpoints. It addresses technical requirements for device-specific ordering control mechanisms and explains why standard Root Port IDO implementation is discouraged due to potential communication failures and lack of identified high-benefit use cases.

children.title: Appendix F. Message Code Usage
children.description: Appendix F provides a definitive reference table for PCI Express Message Codes, mapping specific binary codes to their corresponding routing types and technical definitions. This section answers questions regarding the identification, packet routing, and standardized functional purpose of PCIe protocol messages such as LTR, PME, and interrupt signaling.

children.title: Appendix G. Protocol Multiplexing
children.description: This section defines the optional Protocol Multiplexing mechanism, detailing how PMUX Packets and PCI Express TLPs coexist on a synchronized PMUX Link. It addresses how arbiter and demultiplexer architectures manage data encapsulation, queue traffic, and transmission modes between PMUX-capable devices and processors.

children.title: Section G.1 Protocol Multiplexing Interactions with PCI Express
children.description: This section outlines the operational framework for Protocol Multiplexing (PMUX) within PCI Express, detailing technical requirements for link-level coexistence, software-defined configuration, and packet-specific metadata handling. It addresses how systems manage bandwidth sharing, internal resource isolation, and error resiliency protocols for concurrent multiprotocol traffic.

children.title: Section G.2 PMUX Packets
children.description: Section G.2 defines error-handling protocols for incoming PMUX (Protocol Multiplexing) packets, addressing invalid packet lengths, disabled channel reception, and compatibility mismatches across 8b/10b and 128b/130b encoding schemes. It clarifies system behavior regarding discarded data, Framing Errors, and LCRC validation for components lacking PMUX support.

children.title: Section G.3 PMUX Packet Layout
children.description: This section details PMUX packet encapsulation and structural formatting, specifically addressing 8b/10b and 128b/130b line coding requirements. It defines technical specifications for data framing, synchronization, and bit-level mapping to resolve questions regarding physical layer protocol compatibility.

children.title: Section G.3.1 PMUX Packet Layout for 8b/10b Encoding
children.description: This section details the PMUX packet architecture for 8b/10b encoded data, specifically defining the frame layouts for 2.5 and 5.0 GT/s operations. It specifies the configuration of the PMUX Channel ID, 12-bit Packet Metadata, and variable-length Packet Data, while contrasting this structure with standard PCI Express TLP formatting.

children.title: Section G.3.2 PMUX Packet Layout at 128b/130b Encoding
children.description: This section defines the PMUX packet structure and LCRC calculation logic under 128b/130b encoding, specifically detailing metadata positioning, modified seed values, and Channel ID inclusion. It explains how unique LCRC computation prevents legacy PCIe receivers from misinterpreting PMUX packets as standard TLPs.

children.title: Section G.5 PMUX Extended Capability
children.description: This section details the PCIe Protocol Multiplexing (PMUX) Extended Capability, covering mandatory enablement requirements, packet handling rules, and the structure of the PMUX Protocol Array. It defines where this optional capability must reside within function hierarchies and addresses how software initializes and manages PMUX channels across a communication link.

children.title: Section G.5.1 PMUX Extended Capability Header (Offset 00h)
children.description: This section details the PMUX Extended Capability Header structure, covering DWORD 0 register offsets, Capability IDs, Version fields, and Next Capability pointers. It defines the architectural requirements for identifying and traversing the PMUX extended capability block within the PCIe configuration space.

children.title: Section G.5.2 PMUX Capability Register (Offset 04h)
children.description: This section details the PMUX Extended Capability register structure, including the capability header, versioning, and linked-list offset mapping. It defines the technical specifications of the 001Ah PCI Express Extended Capability ID and explains how the system navigates the capability configuration space.

children.title: Section G.5.3 PMUX Control Register (Offset 08h)
children.description: This section defines the **PMUX Capability Register**, detailing functionality for **Protocol Multiplexing** through the **PMUX Protocol Array Size** and **PMUX Supported Link Speeds** fields. It provides technical specifications for identifying supported **PCIe Link speeds** (2.5 GT/s to 32.0 GT/s) and mandatory compliance for hardware-level protocol packet handling.

children.title: Section G.5.4 PMUX Status Register (Offset 0Ch)
children.description: This section defines the **PMUX Status Register** layout and bit-field allocations for monitoring **channel disablement** states, including **Link Speed**, **Link Width**, and **Protocol Specific** faults. It specifies how these registers report error conditions and channel availability across four independent PMUX channels when link requirements are not met.

children.title: Section G.5.5 PMUX Protocol Array (Offsets 10h through 48h)
children.description: This section defines the **PMUX Protocol Array** status register bits (offsets 10h–48h), which specify reasons for **Channel 1, 2, and 3** disablement. It addresses how hardware reports **Protocol Multiplexing** failures due to incompatible **Link Speed**, **Link Width**, or **protocol-specific** constraints.

children.title: Appendix H. Flow Control Update Latency and ACK Update Latency Calculations
children.description: This section details the mathematical modeling and timing analysis for Flow Control Update Latency and ACK Update Latency within network transmission protocols. It addresses how link-layer congestion management and acknowledgment feedback loops impact overall throughput, latency, and system response times.

children.title: Section H.1 Flow Control Update Latency
children.description: This section details the historical derivation of Flow Control Update Latency and Ack Update Latency, providing cross-references for simplified tables across PCIe specification revisions. It defines the latency measurement window—from buffer space availability to UpdateFC DLLP transmission—and specifies the mathematical formula utilizing `Rx_MPS_Limit`, `TLPOverhead`, and `UpdateFactor`.

children.title: Section H.2 Ack Latency
children.description: This section details PCIe Ack Latency and UpdateFC transmission latency guidelines for 8.0 GT/s operations across varying link widths (x1–x32) and Max Payload Sizes (MPS). It provides reference tables for system architects to determine maximum symbol time latencies and documents the organizational history of these specification tables from Base 3.1 to 5.0.

children.title: Appendix I. Async Hot-Plug Reference Model
children.description: This section outlines a non-normative reference model for asynchronous PCIe hot-plug, focusing on configuration and interrupt handling for Hot-Plug Surprise (HPS) and Downstream Port Containment (DPC). It defines implementation strategies for managing device removal, hot-add events, and error recovery, providing technical guidance for system firmware and OS-level hardware management.

children.title: Section I.1 Async Hot-Plug Initial Configuration
children.description: This section outlines procedures for initializing PCIe asynchronous hot-plug operations by detailing capability control handoffs between the OS and SFW, selection logic between HPS and DPC mechanisms, and adapter detection via OOB PD or link training. It defines specific timing requirements and readiness protocols—such as DRS or mandatory 1000ms delay—necessary to safely initiate configuration of hot-plugged PCIe components.

children.title: Section I.2 Async Removal Configuration and Interrupt Handling
children.description: This section details configuration and interrupt handling procedures for PCIe Hot Plug System (HPS) and Downstream Port Containment (DPC) during asynchronous removal. It specifies requirements for configuring error handling, Advanced Error Reporting (AER), and interrupt service routines to manage link activity, firmware-first (FWF) interactions, and Out-of-Band (OOB) Presence Detect (PD) signaling.

children.title: Section I.3 Async Hot-Add Configuration and Interrupt Handling
children.description: This section outlines protocols for PCI Express asynchronous hot-add and removal, detailing hardware-specific configurations for HPS and DPC, interrupt handling, and link-training synchronization. It provides implementation guidance on managing adapter insertion, Power Controller activation, and Data Link Layer (DLL) state transitions to ensure seamless OS device integration.

children.title: Appendix J. Alpha Power and Reverse lookup assignment
children.description: This section provides the reference implementation files and cryptographic checksums for PCIe 7.0 Forward Error Correction (FEC) logic, including alpha power mappings and encoder/decoder modules for 84/86 and 250/256-bit ECC schemes. It answers technical requirements for verifying data integrity, mapping lookup tables, and implementing hardware-level ECC transformations compliant with the Base Specification.

children.title: Section J.1 Alpha Powers
children.description: Section J.1 of the PCI Express Base Specification details the lookup table for alpha powers used in error correction logic, specifically providing the `i_to_alpha_power_i` system verilog representation of the Galois Field $GF(2^8)$ exponents. It defines the mapping required for finite field arithmetic operations, answering how to map index values to their corresponding alpha power equivalents for polynomial calculations in PCIe protocol.

children.title: Section J.2 84 Byte to 86 Byte Encoder
children.description: This section defines the SystemVerilog implementation of an 84B to 86B ECC encoder compliant with PCIe 6.0/7.0 specifications. It explains how to calculate parity and check bytes using alpha power mapping to provide systematic error correction for 84-byte data blocks.

children.title: Section J.3 250 Byte to 256 Byte Encoder example
children.description: This section details the SystemVerilog implementation of the 250-byte to 256-byte Flit-level encoder defined in the PCIe 7.0 specification. It describes the parallel processing architecture, including input data partitioning into three 84-byte groups and the integration of `ecc_84to86_encoder` sub-modules to generate parity bytes.

children.title: Section J.4 86 Byte to 84 Byte Decoder
children.description: This section defines the SystemVerilog implementation for the PCIe 6.0/7.0 86-to-84 byte ECC decoder, specifying the interface for error detection, syndrome calculation, and single-symbol error correction. It answers how to programmatically decode Reed-Solomon-based encoded data and map error locations and magnitudes for link-layer reliability verification.

children.title: Section J.5 256 Byte to 250 Byte decoder
children.description: This section defines the SystemVerilog implementation for the PCIe 6.0/7.0 ECC 256-to-250 byte decoder designed to provide forward error correction (FEC) and error metric signaling. It addresses how to reconstruct corrected data flits and categorize syndrome checks and parity errors for diagnostic logging.

children.title: Appendix K. MATLAB created generator matrix for CRC code
children.description: Appendix K provides reference implementation files including `gen_matrix.txt`, `gen_matrix.v`, and `pci_sig_8B_crc.sv` to support CRC generation for PCI Express 7.0 flits. This section documents the checksum validation logic and provides the MD5 hash-verified source code required for verifying 8-byte LCRC calculations.

children.title: Section K.1 Generator Matrix output
children.description: This section defines the 1936x64 Generator Matrix used for LCRC (Link Cyclic Redundancy Check) and framing error detection in the PCI Express 7.0 specification. It provides the hexadecimal input values required to implement the parity-check encoding and error-correcting code (ECC) logic essential for reliable high-speed data integrity.

children.title: Section K.2 Flit 8 byte LCRC
children.description: This section provides the official SystemVerilog implementation for the 8-byte Link CRC (LCRC) calculation used in PCIe 7.0 Flit Mode. It defines the algorithmic logic for appending an 8-byte integrity check to 242-byte flit payloads, serving as the standard reference for hardware validation and error detection implementation.

children.title: Appendix L. Example IDE TLPs and Test Keys
children.description: This appendix provides technical reference examples for Integrity and Data Encryption (IDE) Transaction Layer Packets (TLPs), detailing both Non-Flit Mode (NFM) and Flit Mode (FM) configurations with optional partial header encryption. It serves as a guide for validating PCIe IDE implementations by providing concrete packet structures and standardized test keys.

children.title: Section L.1 Example NFM IDE TLP Without Partial Header Encryption
children.description: This section provides a technical specification for verifying Integrity and Data Encryption (IDE) implementations by detailing an NFM Memory Write TLP example without partial header encryption. It lists requisite AES-GCM parameters, input/output values, and payload structures to resolve questions regarding correct IDE packet formatting and cryptographic validation.

children.title: Section L.2 Example FM IDE TLP Without Partial Header Encryption
children.description: This section provides a technical specification and binary validation example for AES-GCM encryption within the IDE (Integrity and Data Encryption) TLP (Transaction Layer Packet) framework. It details the mapping of TLP fields, security tags, and cryptographic outputs, answering how specific header fields and packet data are structured and protected during non-partial header encryption.

children.title: Section L.3 Example NFM IDE TLP With Partial Header Encryption
children.description: This section details the structure of a Memory Write IDE TLP with Partial Header Encryption, specifically covering MAC, Data Dual Words (DW), and encrypted header fields like Stream_ID and PASID. It defines the layout of cryptographic elements and packet headers to answer how to format protected PCIe traffic using the IDE (Integrity and Data Encryption) protocol.

children.title: Section L.4 Example FM IDE TLP With Partial Header Encryption
children.description: This section details the structure of an NFM Memory Write IDE TLP utilizing partial header encryption, specifically defining the bit-level arrangement of MAC, Data, and encrypted header fields. It serves as a technical reference to answer how IDE-compliant PCIe packets are formatted and mapped across double-words (DW) when partial encryption is applied.

children.title: Section L.5 IDE Test Keys
children.description: Section L.5 provides bit-level IDE (Integrity and Data Encryption) test vectors, detailing the structure of Memory Write TLPs with partial header encryption. It answers questions regarding the precise alignment of MAC, PCRC, and Data double-words, while specifying required values for Stream_ID, PASID, and Requester ID fields in cryptographic validation testing.

children.title: Acknowledgements
children.description: This section provides a formal list of industry contributors and their respective corporate affiliations involved in the development of the PCI Express® Base Specification Revision 7.0. It documents the collaborative expertise behind this hardware standard and identifies the specific technology companies represented during the technical drafting process.
